chunks.text:
Concept INFLU
Family: C-ETAS
Title: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
Context: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
Aliases: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science | Engineers improve existing technologies or develop new ones to increase their benefits, decrease known risks, and meet societal demands. | Every human-made product is designed by applying some knowledge of the natural world and is built using materials derived from the natural world. | INFLU: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World | Influence of Engineering, Technology, and Science, on Society and the Natural World | Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World | People's needs and wants change over time, as do their demands for new and improved technologies.
Progressions: elementary: People's needs and wants change over time, as do their demands for new and improved technologies. | elementary: Engineers improve existing technologies or develop new ones to increase their benefits, decrease known risks, and meet societal demands. | elementary: When new technologies become available, they can bring about changes in the way people live and interact with one another. | high: Modern civilization depends on major technological systems, such as agriculture, health, water, energy, transportation, manufacturing, construction, and communications. | high: Engineers continuously modify these systems to increase benefits while decreasing costs and risks. | high: New technologies can have deep impacts on society and the environment, including some that were not anticipated. | high: Analysis of costs and benefits is a critical aspect of decisions about technology. | middle: All human activity draws on natural resources and has both short and long-term consequences, positive as well as negative, for the health of people and the natural environment. | middle: The uses of technologies and any limitations on their use are driven by individual or societal needs, desires, and values; by the findings of scientific research; and by differences in such factors as climate, natural resources, and economic conditions. | middle: Technology use varies over time and from region to region. | primary: Every human-made product is designed by applying some knowledge of the natural world and is built by using natural materials. | primary: Taking natural materials to make things impacts the environment.
chunks.title: INFLU Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World

records.description: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow, and meet their needs.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
Every human-made product is designed by applying some knowledge of the natural world and is built using materials derived from the natural world.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human problems that can be solved by mimicking plant or animal solutions could include designing clothing or equipment to protect bicyclists by mimicking turtle shells, acorn shells, and animal scales; stabilizing structures by mimicking animal tails and roots on plants; keeping out intruders by mimicking thorns on branches and animal quills; and, detecting intruders by mimicking eyes and ears.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use tools and materials to design and build a device that uses light or sound to solve the problem of communicating over a distance.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
People depend on various technologies in their lives; human life would be very different without technology.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include technological details for how communication devices work.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include a light source to send signals, paper cup and string "telephones," and a pattern of drum beats.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
Developing and using technology has impacts on the natural world.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include different designs of dikes and windbreaks to hold back wind and water, and different designs for using shrubs, grass, and trees to hold back the land.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data obtained from testing different materials to determine which materials have the properties that are best suited for an intended purpose.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Influence of Engineering, Technology, and Science, on Society and the Natural World
texts: Every human-made product is designed by applying some knowledge of the natural world and is built using materials derived from the natural world.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of quantitative measurements is limited to length.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of properties could include, strength, flexibility, hardness, texture, and absorbency.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.

edge_payload.raw_id: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
texts: People's needs and wants change over time, as do their demands for new and improved technologies.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-5-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.

edge_payload.raw_id: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
texts: Engineers improve existing technologies or develop new ones to increase their benefits, decrease known risks, and meet societal demands.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-5-ETS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make a claim about the merit of a design solution that reduces the impacts of a weather-related hazard.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World

Engineers improve existing technologies or develop new ones to increase their benefits (e.g., better artificial limbs), decrease known risks (e.g., seatbelts in cars), and meet societal demands (e.g., cell phones).

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of design solutions to weather-related hazards could include barriers to prevent flooding, wind resistant roofs, and lighting rods.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple solutions to reduce the impacts of natural Earth processes on humans.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
Engineers improve existing technologies or develop new ones to increase their benefits, to decrease known risks, and to meet societal demands.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to earthquakes, floods, tsunamis, and volcanic eruptions.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include designing an earthquake resistant building and improving monitoring of volcanic activity.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
Engineers improve existing technologies or develop new ones.

payload.assessment_boundary: Devices should be limited to those that convert motion energy to electric energy or use stored energy to cause motion or produce light or sound.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include electric circuits that convert electrical energy into motion energy of a vehicle, light, or sound; and, a passive solar heater that converts light into heat. Examples of constraints could include the materials, cost, or time to design the device.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for, and respond to, severe weather.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
People depend on various technologies in their lives; human life would be very different without technology.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on local forms of severe weather.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-2

chunks.text:
Concept INTER
Family: C-ETAS
Title: Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology
Context: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
Aliases: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science | INTER: Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology | Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology
Progressions: elementary: Science and technology support each other. | elementary: Tools and instruments are used to answer scientific questions, while scientific discoveries lead to the development of new technologies. | elementary: Scientific discoveries about the natural world can often lead to new and improved technologies, which are develpped through the engineering design process. | elementary: Knowledge of relevant scientific concepts and research findings is important in engineering. | high: Science and engineering complement each other in the cycle known as research and development (R&D). | high: Many R&D projects may involve scientists, engineers, and others with wide ranges of expertise. | middle: Engineering advances have led to important discoveries in virtually every field of science and scientific discoveries have led to the development of entire industries and engineered systems. | middle: Science and technology drive each other forward. | middle: Technologies extend the measurement, exploration, modeling, and computational capacity of scientific investigations. | primary: People encounter questions about the natural world every day. | primary: Science and engineering involve the use of tools to observe and measure things.

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem caused when the environment changes and the types of plants and animals that live there may change.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology
Knowledge of relevant scientific concepts and research findings is important in engineering.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to a single environmental change. Assessment does not include the greenhouse effect or climate change.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of environmental changes could include changes in land characteristics, water distribution, temperature, food, and other organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Define a simple design problem that can be solved by applying scientific ideas about magnets.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology
Scientific discoveries about the natural world can often lead to new and improved technologies, which are developed through the engineering design process.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of problems could include constructing a latch to keep a door shut and creating a device to keep two moving objects from touching each other.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-PS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information to describe that energy and fuels are derived from natural resources and that their uses affect the environment.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology
Knowledge of relevant scientific concepts and research findings is important in engineering.
Influence of Science, Engineering, and Technology on Society and the Natural World
Over time, people's needs and wants change, as do their demands for new and improved technologies.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of renewable energy resources could include wind energy, water behind dams, and sunlight; non-renewable energy resources are fossil fuels and fissile materials. Examples of environmental effects could include loss of habitat due to dams, loss of habitat due to surface mining, and air pollution from burning of fossil fuels.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transfer information.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology
Knowledge of relevant scientific concepts and research findings is important in engineering.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include drums sending coded information through sound waves, using a grid of 1's and 0's representing black and white to send information about a picture, and using Morse code to send text.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to Engineering, Technology, and Applications of Science
texts: Interdependence of Science, Engineering, and Technology
Engineering advances have led to important discoveries in virtually every field of science, and scientific discoveries have led to the development of entire industries and engineered systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative information.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on natural resources that undergo a chemical process to form the synthetic material. Examples of new materials could include new medicine, new foods, and alternative fuels.

chunks.text:
Concept CE
Family: CCC
Title: Cause and Effect: Mechanism and Explanation
Context: Crosscutting Concepts
Aliases: CE: Cause and Effect: Mechanism and Explanation | Cause and Effect | Cause and Effect: Mechanism and Explanation | Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change. | Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change. | Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified. | Cause and effect relationships can be suggested and predicted for complex natural and human-designed systems by examining what is known about smaller scale mechanisms within the system. | Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems. | Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems. (MS-PS1-4) | Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems. (MS-PS2-3), (MS-PS2-5) | Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural systems. | Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.
Progressions: elementary: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change. | elementary: Events that occur together with regularity might or might not be a cause and effect relationship. | high: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects. | high: Cause and effect relationships can be suggested and predicted for complex natural and human designed systems by examining what is known about smaller scale mechanisms within the system. | high: Systems can be designed to cause a desired effect. | high: Changes in systems may have various causes that may not have equal effects. | middle: Relationships can be classified as causal or correlational, and correlation does not necessarily imply causation. | middle: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems. | middle: Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause and effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability. | primary: Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes. | primary: Events have causes that generate observable patterns.
chunks.title: CE Cause and Effect: Mechanism and Explanation

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct investigations to provide evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.

texts: Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of vibrating materials that make sound could include tuning forks and plucking a stretched string. Examples of how sound can make matter vibrate could include holding a piece of paper near a speaker making sound and holding an object near a vibrating tuning fork.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that objects can be seen only when illuminated.

texts: Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of observations could include those made in a completely dark room, a pinhole box, and a video of a cave explorer with a flashlight. Illumination could be from an external light source or by an object giving off its own light.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine the effect of placing objects made with different materials in the path of a beam of light.

texts: Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the speed of light.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of materials could include those that are transparent (such as clear plastic), translucent (such as wax paper), opaque (such as cardboard), and reflective (such as a mirror).
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine if plants need sunlight and water to grow.

texts: Events have causes that generate observable patterns.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to testing one variable at a time.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-LS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data obtained from testing different materials to determine which materials have the properties that are best suited for an intended purpose.*

texts: Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of quantitative measurements is limited to length.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of properties could include, strength, flexibility, hardness, texture, and absorbency.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument with evidence that some changes caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some cannot.

texts: Events have causes that generate observable patterns.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of reversible changes could include materials such as water and butter at different temperatures. Examples of irreversible changes could include cooking an egg, freezing a plant leaf, and heating paper.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-PS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make a claim about the merit of a design solution that reduces the impacts of a weather-related hazard.*

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of design solutions to weather-related hazards could include barriers to prevent flooding, wind resistant roofs, and lighting rods.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument that some animals form groups that help members survive.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of the environment affecting a trait could include normally tall plants grown with insufficient water are stunted; and, a pet dog that is given too much food and little exercise may become overweight.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use evidence to construct an explanation for how the variations in characteristics among individuals of the same species may provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of cause and effect relationships could be plants that have larger thorns than other plants may be less likely to be eaten by predators; and, animals that have better camouflage coloration than other animals may be more likely to survive and therefore more likely to leave offspring.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument with evidence that in a particular habitat some organisms can survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence could include needs and characteristics of the organisms and habitats involved. The organisms and their habitat make up a system in which the parts depend on each other.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to one variable at a time: number, size, or direction of forces. Assessment does not include quantitative force size, only qualitative and relative. Assessment is limited to gravity being addressed as a force that pulls objects down.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include an unbalanced force on one side of a ball can make it start moving; and, balanced forces pushing on a box from both sides will not produce any motion at all.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to determine cause and effect relationships of electric or magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to forces produced by objects that can be manipulated by students, and electrical interactions are limited to static electricity.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of an electric force could include the force on hair from an electrically charged balloon and the electrical forces between a charged rod and pieces of paper; examples of a magnetic force could include the force between two permanent magnets, the force between an electromagnet and steel paperclips, and the force exerted by one magnet versus the force exerted by two magnets. Examples of cause and effect relationships could include how the distance between objects affects strength of the force and how the orientation of magnets affects the direction of the magnetic force.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-PS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations and/or measurements to provide evidence of the effects of weathering or the rate of erosion by water, ice, wind, or vegetation.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to a single form of weathering or erosion.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of variables to test could include angle of slope in the downhill movement of water, amount of vegetation, speed of wind, relative rate of deposition, cycles of freezing and thawing of water, cycles of heating and cooling, and volume of water flow.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information to describe that energy and fuels are derived from natural resources and that their uses affect the environment.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of renewable energy resources could include wind energy, water behind dams, and sunlight; non-renewable energy resources are fossil fuels and fissile materials. Examples of environmental effects could include loss of habitat due to dams, loss of habitat due to surface mining, and air pollution from burning of fossil fuels.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple solutions to reduce the impacts of natural Earth processes on humans.*

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to earthquakes, floods, tsunamis, and volcanic eruptions.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include designing an earthquake resistant building and improving monitoring of volcanic activity.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe that light reflecting from objects and entering the eye allows objects to be seen.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include knowledge of specific colors reflected and seen, the cellular mechanisms of vision, or how the retina works.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Conduct an investigation to determine whether the mixing of two or more substances results in new substances.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down.

texts: Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include mathematical representation of gravitational force.
payload.clarification_statement: "Down" is a local description of the direction that points toward the center of the spherical Earth.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to describe how variations in the flow of energy into and out of Earth’s systems result in changes in climate.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of the results of changes in climate is limited to changes in surface temperatures, precipitation patterns, glacial ice volumes, sea levels, and biosphere distribution.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of the causes of climate change differ by timescale, over 1-10 years: large volcanic eruption, ocean circulation; 10-100s of years: changes in human activity, ocean circulation, solar output; 10-100s of thousands of years: changes to Earth's orbit and the orientation of its axis; and 10-100s of millions of years: long-term changes in atmospheric composition.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how the availability of natural resources, occurrence of natural hazards, and changes in climate have influenced human activity.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of key natural resources include access to fresh water (such as rivers, lakes, and groundwater), regions of fertile soils such as river deltas, and high concentrations of minerals and fossil fuels. Examples of natural hazards can be from interior processes (such as volcanic eruptions and earthquakes), surface processes (such as tsunamis, mass wasting and soil erosion), and severe weather (such as hurricanes, floods, and droughts). Examples of the results of changes in climate that can affect populations or drive mass migrations include changes to sea level, regional patterns of temperature and precipitation, and the types of crops and livestock that can be raised.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the evidence for the role of group behavior on individual and species’ chances to survive and reproduce.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on: (1) distinguishing between group and individual behavior, (2) identifying evidence supporting the outcomes of group behavior, and (3) developing logical and reasonable arguments based on evidence. Examples of group behaviors could include flocking, schooling, herding, and cooperative behaviors such as hunting, migrating, and swarming.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-8

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to clarify relationships about the role of DNA and chromosomes in coding the instructions for characteristic traits passed from parents to offspring.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.clarification_statement: Assessment Boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make and defend a claim based on evidence that inheritable genetic variations may result from: (1) new genetic combinations through meiosis, (2) viable errors occurring during replication, and/or (3) mutations caused by environmental factors.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using data to support arguments for the way variation occurs.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that the process of evolution primarily results from four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the heritable genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for limited resources, and (4) the proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in the environment.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include other mechanisms of evolution, such as genetic drift, gene flow through migration, and co-evolution.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using evidence to explain the influence each of the four factors has on the number of organisms, behaviors, morphology, or physiology in terms of ability to compete for limited resources and subsequent survival of individuals and adaptation of species. Examples of evidence could include mathematical models such as simple distribution graphs and proportional reasoning.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how natural selection leads to adaptation of populations.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using data to provide evidence for how specific biotic and abiotic differences in ecosystems (such as ranges of seasonal temperature, long-term climate change, acidity, light, geographic barriers, or evolution of other organisms) contribute to a change in gene frequency over time, leading to adaptation of populations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the evidence supporting claims that changes in environmental conditions may result in: (1) increases in the number of individuals of some species, (2) the emergence of new species over time, and (3) the extinction of other species.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on determining cause and effect relationships for how changes to the environment such as deforestation, fishing, application of fertilizers, drought, flood, and the rate of change of the environment affect distribution or disappearance of traits in species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create or revise a simulation to test a solution to mitigate adverse impacts of human activity on biodiversity.*

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on designing solutions for a proposed problem related to threatened or endangered species, or to genetic variation of organisms for multiple species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data to support the claim that Newton’s second law of motion describes the mathematical relationship among the net force on a macroscopic object, its mass, and its acceleration.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to one-dimensional motion and to macroscopic objects moving at non-relativistic speeds.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include tables or graphs of position or velocity as a function of time for objects subject to a net unbalanced force, such as a falling object, an object rolling down a ramp, or a moving object being pulled by a constant force.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific and engineering ideas to design, evaluate, and refine a device that minimizes the force on a macroscopic object during a collision.*

texts: Systems can be designed to cause a desired effect.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative evaluations and/or algebraic manipulations.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evaluation and refinement could include determining the success of the device at protecting an object from damage and modifying the design to improve it. Examples of a device could include a football helmet or a parachute.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that an electric current can produce a magnetic field and that a changing magnetic field can produce an electric current.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to designing and conducting investigations with provided materials and tools.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model of two objects interacting through electric or magnetic fields to illustrate the forces between objects and the changes in energy of the objects due to the interaction.

texts: Cause and effect relationships can be suggested and predicted for complex natural and human-designed systems by examining what is known about smaller scale mechanisms within the system.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to systems containing two objects.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include drawings, diagrams, and texts, such as drawings of what happens when two charges of opposite polarity are near each other.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support a claim regarding relationships among the frequency, wavelength, and speed of waves traveling in various media.

texts: Empirical evidence is required to differentiate between cause and correlation and make claims about specific causes and effects.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to algebraic relationships and describing those relationships qualitatively.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include electromagnetic radiation traveling in a vacuum and glass, sound waves traveling through air and water, and seismic waves traveling through the Earth.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the validity and reliability of claims in published materials of the effects that different frequencies of electromagnetic radiation have when absorbed by matter.

texts: Cause and effect relationships can be suggested and predicted for complex natural and human-designed systems by examining what is known about smaller scale mechanisms within the system.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative descriptions.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the idea that photons associated with different frequencies of light have different energies, and the damage to living tissue from electromagnetic radiation depends on the energy of the radiation. Examples of published materials could include trade books, magazines, web resources, videos, and other passages that may reflect bias.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate technical information about how some technological devices use the principles of wave behavior and wave interactions with matter to transmit and capture information and energy.*

texts: Systems can be designed to cause a desired effect.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessments are limited to qualitative information. Assessments do not include band theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include solar cells capturing light and converting it to electricity; medical imaging; and communications technology.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for, and respond to, severe weather.*

texts: Events have causes that generate observable patterns.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on local forms of severe weather.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the land, water, air, and/or other living things in the local environment.*

texts: Events have causes that generate observable patterns.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human impact on the land could include cutting trees to produce paper and using resources to produce bottles. Examples of solutions could include reusing paper and recycling cans and bottles.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to compare the effects of different strengths or different directions of pushes and pulls on the motion of an object.

texts: Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to different relative strengths or different directions, but not both at the same time. Assessment does not include non-contact pushes or pulls such as those produced by magnets.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of pushes or pulls could include a string attached to an object being pulled, a person pushing an object, a person stopping a rolling ball, and two objects colliding and pushing on each other.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data to determine if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a push or a pull.*

texts: Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include friction as a mechanism for change in speed.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of problems requiring a solution could include having a marble or other object move a certain distance, follow a particular path, and knock down other objects. Examples of solutions could include tools such as a ramp to increase the speed of the object and a structure that would cause an object such as a marble or ball to turn.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations to determine the effect of sunlight on Earth’s surface.

texts: Events have causes that generate observable patterns.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of temperature is limited to relative measures such as warmer/cooler.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of Earth’s surface could include sand, soil, rocks, and water.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use tools and materials to design and build a structure that will reduce the warming effect of sunlight on an area.*

texts: Events have causes that generate observable patterns.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of structures could include umbrellas, canopies, and tents that minimize the warming effect of the sun.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Collect data to provide evidence for how the motions and complex interactions of air masses result in changes in weather conditions.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include recalling the names of cloud types or weather symbols used on weather maps or the reported diagrams from weather stations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how air masses flow from regions of high pressure to low pressure, causing weather (defined by temperature, pressure, humidity, precipitation, and wind) at a fixed location to change over time, and how sudden changes in weather can result when different air masses collide. Emphasis is on how weather can be predicted within probabilistic ranges. Examples of data can be provided to students (such as weather maps, diagrams, and visualizations) or obtained through laboratory experiments (such as with condensation).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth's mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how these resources are limited and typically non-renewable, and how their distributions are significantly changing as a result of removal by humans. Examples of uneven distributions of resources as a result of past processes include but are not limited to petroleum (locations of the burial of organic marine sediments and subsequent geologic traps), metal ores (locations of past volcanic and hydrothermal activity associated with subduction zones), and soil (locations of active weathering and/or deposition of rock).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing a human impact on the environment.

texts: Relationships can be classified as causal or correlational, and correlation does not necessarily imply causation.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of the design process include examining human environmental impacts, assessing the kinds of solutions that are feasible, and designing and evaluating solutions that could reduce that impact. Examples of human impacts can include water usage (such as the withdrawal of water from streams and aquifers or the construction of dams and levees), land usage (such as urban development, agriculture, or the removal of wetlands), and pollution (such as of the air, water, or land).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence include grade-appropriate databases on human populations and the rates of consumption of food and natural resources (such as freshwater, mineral, and energy). Examples of impacts can include changes to the appearance, composition, and structure of Earth's systems as well as the rates at which they change. The consequences of increases in human populations and consumption of natural resources are described by science, but science does not make the decisions for the actions society takes.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use argument based on empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support an explanation for how characteristic animal behaviors and specialized plant structures affect the probability of successful reproduction of animals and plants respectively.

texts: Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause and effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of behaviors that affect the probability of animal reproduction could include nest building to protect young from cold, herding of animals to protect young from predators, and vocalization of animals and colorful plumage to attract mates for breeding. Examples of animal behaviors that affect the probability of plant reproduction could include transferring pollen or seeds, and creating conditions for seed germination and growth. Examples of plant structures could include bright flowers attracting butterflies that transfer pollen, flower nectar and odors that attract insects that transfer pollen, and hard shells on nuts that squirrels bury.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.

texts: Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause and effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include genetic mechanisms, gene regulation, or biochemical processes.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of local environmental conditions could include availability of food, light, space, and water. Examples of genetic factors could include large breed cattle and species of grass affecting growth of organisms. Examples of evidence could include drought decreasing plant growth, fertilizer increasing plant growth, different varieties of plant seeds growing at different rates in different conditions, and fish growing larger in large ponds than they do in small ponds.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and synthesize information that sensory receptors respond to stimuli by sending messages to the brain for immediate behavior or storage as memories.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include mechanisms for the transmission of this information.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-8

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on cause and effect relationships between resources and growth of individual organisms and the numbers of organisms in ecosystems during periods of abundant and scarce resources.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural systems.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using models such as Punnett squares, diagrams, and simulations to describe the cause and effect relationship of gene transmission from parent(s) to offspring and resulting genetic variation.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that describes how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some individuals’ probability of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.

texts: Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause and effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using simple probability statements and proportional reasoning to construct explanations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and synthesize information about the technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.

texts: Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause and effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on synthesizing information from reliable sources about the influence of humans on genetic outcomes in artificial selection (such as genetic modification, animal husbandry, gene therapy); and, on the impacts these technologies have on society as well as the technologies leading to these scientific discoveries.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support explanations of how natural selection may lead to increases and decreases of specific traits in populations over time.

texts: Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause and effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using mathematical models, probability statements, and proportional reasoning to support explanations of trends in changes to populations over time.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model that predicts and describes changes in particle motion, temperature, and state of a pure substance when thermal energy is added or removed.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems. (MS-PS1-4)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include recalling the names of specific periods and epochs and events within them.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on qualitative molecular-level models of solids, liquids, and gases to show that adding or removing thermal energy increases or decreases kinetic energy of the particles until a change of state occurs. Examples of models could include drawings and diagrams. Examples of particles could include molecules or inert atoms. Examples of pure substances could include water, carbon dioxide, and helium.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems. (MS-PS2-3), (MS-PS2-5)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment about questions that require quantitative answers is limited to proportional reasoning and algebraic thinking.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices that use electric and magnetic forces could include electromagnets, electric motors, or generators. Examples of data could include the effect of the number of turns of wire on the strength of an electromagnet, or the effect of increasing the number or strength of magnets on the speed of an electric motor.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Conduct an investigation and evaluate the experimental design to provide evidence that fields exist between objects exerting forces on each other even though the objects are not in contact.

texts: Cause and effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems. (MS-PS2-3), (MS-PS2-5)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to electric and magnetic fields, and limited to qualitative evidence for the existence of fields.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of this phenomenon could include the interactions of magnets, electrically-charged strips of tape, and electrically-charged pith balls. Examples of investigations could include first-hand experiences or simulations.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-5
