chunks.text:
Concept PAT
Family: CCC
Title: Patterns
Context: Crosscutting Concepts
Aliases: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena. | Empirical evidence is needed to identify patterns. | Graphs and charts can be used to identify patterns in data. (MS-PS4-1) | Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data. | Macroscopic patterns are related to the nature of microscopic and atomic-level structure. (MS-PS1-2) | PAT: Patterns | Patterns | Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation. | Patterns can be used to identify cause and effect relationships. | Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships. | Patterns in rates of change and other numerical relationships can provide information about natural systems. | Patterns in the natural and human designed world can be observed and used as evidence.
Progressions: elementary: Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort, classify, communicate and analyze simple rates of change for natural phenomena and designed products. | elementary: Patterns of change can be used to make predictions. | elementary: Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation. | high: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena. | high: Classifications or explanations used at one scale may fail or need revision when information from smaller or larger scales is introduced, thus requiring improved investigations and experiments. | high: Patterns of performance of designed systems can be analyzed and interpreted to reengineer and improve the system. | high: Mathematical representations are needed to identify some patterns. | high: Empirical evidence is needed to identify patterns. | middle: Macroscopic patterns are related to the nature of microscopic and atomic-level structure. | middle: Patterns in rates of change and other numerical relationships can provide information about natural and human designed systems. | middle: Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships. | middle: Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.
chunks.title: PAT Patterns

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use observations of the sun, moon, and stars to describe patterns that can be predicted.

texts: Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of star patterns is limited to stars being seen at night and not during the day.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include that the sun and moon appear to rise in one part of the sky, move across the sky, and set; and stars other than our sun are visible at night but not during the day.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations at different times of year to relate the amount of daylight to the time of year.

texts: Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to relative amounts of daylight, not quantifying the hours or time of daylight.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on relative comparisons of the amount of daylight in the winter to the amount in the spring or fall.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Read texts and use media to determine patterns in behavior of parents and offspring that help offspring survive.

texts: Patterns in the natural and human designed world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns of behaviors could include the signals that offspring make (such as crying, cheeping, and other vocalizations) and the responses of the parents (such as feeding, comforting, and protecting the offspring).
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that young plants and animals are like, but not exactly like, their parents.

texts: Patterns in the natural and human designed world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include inheritance or animals that undergo metamorphosis or hybrids.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include features plants or animals share. Examples of observations could include leaves from the same kind of plant are the same shape but can differ in size; and, a particular breed of dog looks like its parents but is not exactly the same.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to represent the shapes and kinds of land and bodies of water in an area.

texts: Patterns in the natural world can be observed.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include quantitative scaling in models.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain information to identify where water is found on Earth and that it can be solid or liquid.

texts: Patterns in the natural world can be observed.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to describe and classify different kinds of materials by their observable properties.

texts: Patterns in the natural and human designed world can be observed.

payload.clarification_statement: Observations could include color, texture, hardness, and flexibility. Patterns could include the similar properties that different materials share.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-PS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Represent data in tables and graphical displays to describe typical weather conditions expected during a particular season.

texts: Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of graphical displays is limited to pictographs and bar graphs. Assessment does not include climate change.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include average temperature, precipitation, and wind direction.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information to describe climates in different regions of the world.

texts: Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death.

texts: Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of plant life cycles is limited to those of flowering plants. Assessment does not include details of human reproduction.
payload.clarification_statement: Changes organisms go through during their life form a pattern.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from parents and that variation of these traits exists in a group of similar organisms.

texts: Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort and classify natural phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include genetic mechanisms of inheritance and prediction of traits. Assessment is limited to non-human examples.
payload.clarification_statement: Patterns are the similarities and differences in traits shared between offspring and their parents, or among siblings. Emphasis is on organisms other than humans.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations and/or measurements of an object’s motion to provide evidence that a pattern can be used to predict future motion.

texts: Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include technical terms such as period and frequency.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of motion with a predictable pattern could include a child swinging in a swing, a ball rolling back and forth in a bowl, and two children on a see-saw.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-PS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Identify evidence from patterns in rock formations and fossils in rock layers to support an explanation for changes in a landscape over time.

texts: Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific knowledge of the mechanism of rock formation or memorization of specific rock formations and layers. Assessment is limited to relative time.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence from patterns could include rock layers with marine shell fossils above rock layers with plant fossils and no shells, indicating a change from land to water over time; and, a canyon with different rock layers in the walls and a river in the bottom, indicating that over time a river cut through the rock.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data from maps to describe patterns of Earth’s features.

texts: Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation.

payload.clarification_statement: Maps can include topographic maps of Earth’s land and ocean floor, as well as maps of the locations of mountains, continental boundaries, volcanoes, and earthquakes.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength and that waves can cause objects to move.

texts: Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort, classify, and analyze simple rates of change for natural phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include interference effects, electromagnetic waves, non-periodic waves, or quantitative models of amplitude and wavelength.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include diagrams, analogies, and physical models using wire to illustrate wavelength and amplitude of waves.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transfer information.*

texts: Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort and classify designed products.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include drums sending coded information through sound waves, using a grid of 1's and 0's representing black and white to send information about a picture, and using Morse code to send text.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.

texts: Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort, classify, communicate and analyze simple rates of change for natural phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include causes of seasons.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include the position and motion of Earth with respect to the sun and selected stars that are visible only in particular months.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate evidence of the past and current movements of continental and oceanic crust and the theory of plate tectonics to explain the ages of crustal rocks.

texts: Empirical evidence is needed to identify patterns.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the ability of plate tectonics to explain the ages of crustal rocks. Examples include evidence of the ages of oceanic crust increasing with distance from mid-ocean ridges (a result of plate spreading) and the ages of North American continental crust decreasing with distance away from a central ancient core of the continental plate (a result of past plate interactions).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific information that common ancestry and biological evolution are supported by multiple lines of empirical evidence.

texts: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a conceptual understanding of the role each line of evidence has relating to common ancestry and biological evolution. Examples of evidence could include similarities in DNA sequences, anatomical structures, and order of appearance of structures in embryological development.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to support explanations that organisms with an advantageous heritable trait tend to increase in proportion to organisms lacking this trait.

texts: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to basic statistical and graphical analysis. Assessment does not include allele frequency calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on analyzing shifts in numerical distribution of traits and using these shifts as evidence to support explanations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use the periodic table as a model to predict the relative properties of elements based on the patterns of electrons in the outermost energy level of atoms.

texts: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to main group elements. Assessment does not include quantitative understanding of ionization energy beyond relative trends.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of properties that could be predicted from patterns could include reactivity of metals, types of bonds formed, numbers of bonds formed, and reactions with oxygen.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct and revise an explanation for the outcome of a simple chemical reaction based on the outermost electron states of atoms, trends in the periodic table, and knowledge of the patterns of chemical properties.

texts: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to chemical reactions involving main group elements and combustion reactions.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of chemical reactions could include the reaction of sodium and chlorine, of carbon and oxygen, or of carbon and hydrogen.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to gather evidence to compare the structure of substances at the bulk scale to infer the strength of electrical forces between particles.

texts: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Raoult’s law calculations of vapor pressure.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on understanding the strengths of forces between particles, not on naming specific intermolecular forces (such as dipole-dipole). Examples of particles could include ions, atoms, molecules, and networked materials (such as graphite). Examples of bulk properties of substances could include the melting point and boiling point, vapor pressure, and surface tension.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific principles and evidence to provide an explanation about the effects of changing the temperature or concentration of the reacting particles on the rate at which a reaction occurs.

texts: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to simple reactions in which there are only two reactants; evidence from temperature, concentration, and rate data; and qualitative relationships between rate and temperature.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on student reasoning that focuses on the number and energy of collisions between molecules.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS1-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations of Newton’s Law of Gravitation and Coulomb’s Law to describe and predict the gravitational and electrostatic forces between objects.

texts: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to systems with two objects.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both quantitative and conceptual descriptions of gravitational and electric fields.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use and share observations of local weather conditions to describe patterns over time.

texts: Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of quantitative observations limited to whole numbers and relative measures such as warmer/cooler.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of qualitative observations could include descriptions of the weather (such as sunny, cloudy, rainy, and warm); examples of quantitative observations could include numbers of sunny, windy, and rainy days in a month. Examples of patterns could include that it is usually cooler in the morning than in the afternoon and the number of sunny days versus cloudy days in different months.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.

texts: Patterns in the natural and human designed world can be observed and used as evidence.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include that animals need to take in food but plants do not; the different kinds of food needed by different types of animals; the requirement of plants to have light; and, that all living things need water.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model of the Earth-sun-moon system to describe the cyclic patterns of lunar phases, eclipses of the sun and moon, and seasons.

texts: Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models can be physical, graphical, or conceptual.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions.

texts: Patterns in rates of change and other numerical relationships can provide information about natural systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Paleomagnetic anomalies in oceanic and continental crust are not assessed.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data include similarities of rock and fossil types on different continents, the shapes of the continents (including continental shelves), and the locations of ocean structures (such as ridges, fracture zones, and trenches).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on natural hazards to forecast future catastrophic events and inform the development of technologies to mitigate their effects.

texts: Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how some natural hazards, such as volcanic eruptions and severe weather, are preceded by phenomena that allow for reliable predictions, but others, such as earthquakes, occur suddenly and with no notice, and thus are not yet predictable. Examples of natural hazards can be taken from interior processes (such as earthquakes and volcanic eruptions), surface processes (such as mass wasting and tsunamis), or severe weather events (such as hurricanes, tornadoes, and floods). Examples of data can include the locations, magnitudes, and frequencies of the natural hazards. Examples of technologies can be global (such as satellite systems to monitor hurricanes or forest fires) or local (such as building basements in tornado-prone regions or reservoirs to mitigate droughts).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation that predicts patterns of interactions among organisms across multiple ecosystems.

texts: Patterns can be used to identify cause and effect relationships.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on predicting consistent patterns of interactions in different ecosystems in terms of the relationships among and between organisms and abiotic components of ecosystems. Examples of types of interactions could include competitive, predatory, and mutually beneficial.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.

texts: Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the names of individual species or geological eras in the fossil record.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on finding patterns of changes in the level of complexity of anatomical structures in organisms and the chronological order of fossil appearance in the rock layers.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for the anatomical similarities and differences among modern organisms and between modern and fossil organisms to infer evolutionary relationships.

texts: Patterns can be used to identify cause and effect relationships.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on explanations of the evolutionary relationships among organisms in terms of similarity or differences of the gross appearance of anatomical structures.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze displays of pictorial data to compare patterns of similarities in the embryological development across multiple species to identify relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.

texts: Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of comparisons is limited to gross appearance of anatomical structures in embryological development.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on inferring general patterns of relatedness among embryos of different organisms by comparing the macroscopic appearance of diagrams or pictures.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.

texts: Macroscopic patterns are related to the nature of microscopic and atomic-level structure. (MS-PS1-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to analysis of the following properties: density, melting point, boiling point, solubility, flammability, and odor.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of reactions could include burning sugar or steel wool, fat reacting with sodium hydroxide, and mixing zinc with hydrogen chloride.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave.

texts: Graphs and charts can be used to identify patterns in data. (MS-PS4-1)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include electromagnetic waves and is limited to standard repeating waves.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on describing waves with both qualitative and quantitative thinking.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-1

payload.full_name: PAT: Patterns

progression_statements.description: Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort, classify, communicate and analyze simple rates of change for natural phenomena and designed products.

payload.text: Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort, classify, communicate and analyze simple rates of change for natural phenomena and designed products.

progression_statements.title: PAT-E1

progression_statements.description: Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.

payload.text: Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.

progression_statements.title: PAT-E2

progression_statements.description: Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation.

payload.text: Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation.

progression_statements.title: PAT-E3

progression_statements.description: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

payload.text: Different patterns may be observed at each of the scales at which a system is studied and can provide evidence for causality in explanations of phenomena.

progression_statements.title: PAT-H1

progression_statements.description: Classifications or explanations used at one scale may fail or need revision when information from smaller or larger scales is introduced, thus requiring improved investigations and experiments.

payload.text: Classifications or explanations used at one scale may fail or need revision when information from smaller or larger scales is introduced, thus requiring improved investigations and experiments.

progression_statements.title: PAT-H2

progression_statements.description: Patterns of performance of designed systems can be analyzed and interpreted to reengineer and improve the system.

payload.text: Patterns of performance of designed systems can be analyzed and interpreted to reengineer and improve the system.

progression_statements.title: PAT-H3

progression_statements.description: Mathematical representations are needed to identify some patterns.

payload.text: Mathematical representations are needed to identify some patterns.

progression_statements.title: PAT-H4

progression_statements.description: Empirical evidence is needed to identify patterns.

payload.text: Empirical evidence is needed to identify patterns.

progression_statements.title: PAT-H5

progression_statements.description: Macroscopic patterns are related to the nature of microscopic and atomic-level structure.

payload.text: Macroscopic patterns are related to the nature of microscopic and atomic-level structure.

progression_statements.title: PAT-M1

progression_statements.description: Patterns in rates of change and other numerical relationships can provide information about natural and human designed systems.

payload.text: Patterns in rates of change and other numerical relationships can provide information about natural and human designed systems.

progression_statements.title: PAT-M2

progression_statements.description: Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships.

payload.text: Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships.

progression_statements.title: PAT-M3

progression_statements.description: Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.

payload.text: Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.

progression_statements.title: PAT-M4

progression_statements.description: Patterns in the natural and human designed world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.

payload.text: Patterns in the natural and human designed world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.

progression_statements.title: PAT-P1

aliases.alias_text: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible.
aliases.normalized_alias: change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. some system changes are irreversible.

aliases.alias_text: Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales. (MS-PS2-2)
aliases.normalized_alias: explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales. (ms-ps2-2)

aliases.alias_text: Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and processes at different scales, including the atomic scale.
aliases.normalized_alias: explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and processes at different scales, including the atomic scale.

aliases.alias_text: Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system.

aliases.alias_text: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.
aliases.normalized_alias: much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

aliases.alias_text: SC: Stability and Change
aliases.normalized_alias: sc: stability and change

aliases.alias_text: Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.
aliases.normalized_alias: small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.

aliases.alias_text: Stability and Change
aliases.normalized_alias: stability and change

aliases.alias_text: Stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time.
aliases.normalized_alias: stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time.

chunks.text:
Concept SC
Family: CCC
Title: Stability and Change
Context: Crosscutting Concepts
Aliases: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible. | Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales. (MS-PS2-2) | Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and processes at different scales, including the atomic scale. | Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system. | Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable. | SC: Stability and Change | Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part. | Stability and Change | Stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time. | Systems can be designed for greater or lesser stability. | Things may change slowly or rapidly.
Progressions: elementary: Change is measured in terms of differences over time and may occur at different rates. | elementary: Some systems appear stable, but over long periods of time will eventually change. | high: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable. | high: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible. | high: Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system. | high: Systems can be designed for greater or lesser stability. | middle: Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales, including the atomic scale. | middle: Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part. | middle: Stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time. | middle: Systems in dynamic equilibrium are stable due to a balance of feedback mechanisms. | primary: Some things stay the same while other things change. | primary: Things may change slowly or rapidly.
chunks.title: SC Stability and Change

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use information from several sources to provide evidence that Earth events can occur quickly or slowly.

texts: Things may change slowly or rapidly.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include quantitative measurements of timescales.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of events and timescales could include volcanic explosions and earthquakes, which happen quickly and erosion of rocks, which occurs slowly.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.*

texts: Things may change slowly or rapidly.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include different designs of dikes and windbreaks to hold back wind and water, and different designs for using shrubs, grass, and trees to hold back the land.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific reasoning and evidence from ancient Earth materials, meteorites, and other planetary surfaces to construct an account of Earth’s formation and early history.

texts: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using available evidence within the solar system to reconstruct the early history of Earth, which formed along with the rest of the solar system 4.6 billion years ago. Examples of evidence include the absolute ages of ancient materials (obtained by radiometric dating of meteorites, moon rocks, and Earth’s oldest minerals), the sizes and compositions of solar system objects, and the impact cratering record of planetary surfaces.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to illustrate how Earth’s internal and surface processes operate at different spatial and temporal scales to form continental and ocean-floor features.

texts: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include memorization of the details of the formation of specific geographic features of Earth’s surface.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how the appearance of land features (such as mountains, valleys, and plateaus) and sea-floor features (such as trenches, ridges, and seamounts) are a result of both constructive forces (such as volcanism, tectonic uplift, and orogeny) and destructive mechanisms (such as weathering, mass wasting, and coastal erosion).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze geoscience data to make the claim that one change to Earth's surface can create feedbacks that cause changes to other Earth systems.

texts: Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples should include climate feedbacks, such as how an increase in greenhouse gases causes a rise in global temperatures that melts glacial ice, which reduces the amount of sunlight reflected from Earth's surface, increasing surface temperatures and further reducing the amount of ice. Examples could also be taken from other system interactions, such as how the loss of ground vegetation causes an increase in water runoff and soil erosion; how dammed rivers increase groundwater recharge, decrease sediment transport, and increase coastal erosion; or how the loss of wetlands causes a decrease in local humidity that further reduces the wetland extent.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument based on evidence about the simultaneous coevolution of Earth’s systems and life on Earth.

texts: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include a comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms of how the biosphere interacts with all of Earth’s other systems.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the dynamic causes, effects, and feedbacks between the biosphere and Earth’s other systems, whereby geoscience factors control the evolution of life, which in turn continuously alters Earth’s surface. Examples include how photosynthetic life altered the atmosphere through the production of oxygen, which in turn increased weathering rates and allowed for the evolution of animal life; how microbial life on land increased the formation of soil, which in turn allowed for the evolution of land plants; or how the evolution of corals created reefs that altered patterns of erosion and deposition along coastlines and provided habitats for the evolution of new life forms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-7

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create a computational simulation to illustrate the relationships among management of natural resources, the sustainability of human populations, and biodiversity.

texts: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment for computational simulations is limited to using provided multi-parameter programs or constructing simplified spreadsheet calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of factors that affect the management of natural resources include costs of resource extraction and waste management, per-capita consumption, and the development of new technologies. Examples of factors that affect human sustainability include agricultural efficiency, levels of conservation, and urban planning.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate or refine a technological solution that reduces impacts of human activities on natural systems.*

texts: Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data on the impacts of human activities could include the quantities and types of pollutants released, changes to biomass and species diversity, or areal changes in land surface use (such as for urban development, agriculture and livestock, or surface mining). Examples for limiting future impacts could range from local efforts (such as reducing, reusing, and recycling resources) to large-scale geoengineering design solutions (such as altering global temperatures by making large changes to the atmosphere or ocean).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze geoscience data and the results from global climate models to make an evidence-based forecast of the current rate of global or regional climate change and associated future impacts to Earth systems.

texts: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to one example of a climate change and its associated impacts.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence, for both data and climate model outputs, are for climate changes (such as precipitation and temperature) and their associated impacts (such as on sea level, glacial ice volumes, or atmosphere and ocean composition).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that feedback mechanisms maintain homeostasis.

texts: Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the cellular processes involved in the feedback mechanism.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of investigations could include heart rate response to exercise, stomate response to moisture and temperature, and root development in response to water levels.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the claims, evidence, and reasoning that the complex interactions in ecosystems maintain relatively consistent numbers and types of organisms in stable conditions, but changing conditions may result in a new ecosystem.

texts: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of changes in ecosystem conditions could include modest biological or physical changes, such as moderate hunting or a seasonal flood; and extreme changes, such as volcanic eruption or sea level rise.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Design, evaluate, and refine a solution for reducing the impacts of human activities on the environment and biodiversity.*

texts: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human activities can include urbanization, building dams, and dissemination of invasive species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-7

linked_performance_expectations.description: Refine the design of a chemical system by specifying a change in conditions that would produce increased amounts of products at equilibrium.*

texts: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to specifying the change in only one variable at a time. Assessment does not include calculating equilibrium constants and concentrations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the application of Le Chatelier’s Principle and on refining designs of chemical reaction systems, including descriptions of the connection between changes made at the macroscopic level and what happens at the molecular level. Examples of designs could include different ways to increase product formation including adding reactants or removing products.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate questions about the advantages of using a digital transmission and storage of information.

texts: Systems can be designed for greater or lesser stability.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of advantages could include that digital information is stable because it can be stored reliably in computer memory, transferred easily, and copied and shared rapidly. Disadvantages could include issues of easy deletion, security, and theft.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of Earth's materials and the flow of energy that drives this process.

texts: Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and processes at different scales, including the atomic scale.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the identification and naming of minerals.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the processes of melting, crystallization, weathering, deformation, and sedimentation, which act together to form minerals and rocks through the cycling of Earth's materials.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century.

texts: Stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of factors include human activities (such as fossil fuel combustion, cement production, and agricultural activity) and natural processes (such as changes in incoming solar radiation or volcanic activity). Examples of evidence can include tables, graphs, and maps of global and regional temperatures, atmospheric levels of gases such as carbon dioxide and methane, and the rates of human activities. Emphasis is on the major role that human activities play in causing the rise in global temperatures.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by empirical evidence that changes to physical or biological components of an ecosystem affect populations.

texts: Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on recognizing patterns in data and making warranted inferences about changes in populations, and on evaluating empirical evidence supporting arguments about changes to ecosystems.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.*

texts: Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of ecosystem services could include water purification, nutrient recycling, and prevention of soil erosion. Examples of design solution constraints could include scientific, economic, and social considerations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object's motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object.

texts: Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales. (MS-PS2-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to forces and changes in motion in one-dimension in an inertial reference frame and to change in one variable at a time. Assessment does not include the use of trigonometry.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on balanced (Newton's First Law) and unbalanced forces in a system, qualitative comparisons of forces, mass and changes in motion (Newton's Second Law), frame of reference, and specification of units.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-2

payload.full_name: SC: Stability and Change

progression_statements.description: Change is measured in terms of differences over time and may occur at different rates.

payload.text: Change is measured in terms of differences over time and may occur at different rates.

progression_statements.title: SC-E1

progression_statements.description: Some systems appear stable, but over long periods of time will eventually change.

payload.text: Some systems appear stable, but over long periods of time will eventually change.

progression_statements.title: SC-E2

progression_statements.description: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

payload.text: Much of science deals with constructing explanations of how things change and how they remain stable.

progression_statements.title: SC-H1

progression_statements.description: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible.

payload.text: Change and rates of change can be quantified and modeled over very short or very long periods of time. Some system changes are irreversible.

progression_statements.title: SC-H2

progression_statements.description: Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system.

payload.text: Feedback (negative or positive) can stabilize or destabilize a system.

progression_statements.title: SC-H3

progression_statements.description: Systems can be designed for greater or lesser stability.

payload.text: Systems can be designed for greater or lesser stability.

progression_statements.title: SC-H4

progression_statements.description: Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales, including the atomic scale.

payload.text: Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales, including the atomic scale.

progression_statements.title: SC-M1

progression_statements.description: Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.

payload.text: Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.

progression_statements.title: SC-M2

progression_statements.description: Stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time.

payload.text: Stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time.

progression_statements.title: SC-M3

progression_statements.description: Systems in dynamic equilibrium are stable due to a balance of feedback mechanisms.

payload.text: Systems in dynamic equilibrium are stable due to a balance of feedback mechanisms.

progression_statements.title: SC-M4

progression_statements.description: Some things stay the same while other things change.

payload.text: Some things stay the same while other things change.

progression_statements.title: SC-P1

progression_statements.description: Things may change slowly or rapidly.

payload.text: Things may change slowly or rapidly.

progression_statements.title: SC-P2

chunks.text:
Concept SF
Family: CCC
Title: Structure and Function
Context: Crosscutting Concepts
Aliases: SF: Structure and Function | Structure and Function
Progressions: elementary: Different materials have different substructures, which can sometimes be observed. | elementary: Substructures have shapes and parts that serve functions. | high: Investigating or designing new systems or structures requires a detailed examination of the properties of different materials, the structures of different components, and connections of components to reveal its function and/or solve a problem. | high: The functions and properties of natural and designed objects and systems can be inferred from their overall structure, the way their components are shaped and used, and the molecular substructures of their various materials. | middle: Complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the shapes, composition, and relationships among its parts; therefore, complex natural and designed structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function. | middle: Structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used. | primary: The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).
chunks.title: SF Structure and Function

chunks.title: SPQ Scale, Proportion, and Quantity

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of the organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.

texts: Observable phenomena exist from very short to very long time periods.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include identification of specific fossils or present plants and animals. Assessment is limited to major fossil types and relative ages.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include type, size, and distributions of fossil organisms. Examples of fossils and environments could include marine fossils found on dry land, tropical plant fossils found in Arctic areas, and fossils of extinct organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Support an argument that differences in the apparent brightness of the sun compared to other stars is due to their relative distances from Earth.

texts: Natural objects exist from the very small to the immensely large.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to relative distances, not sizes, of stars. Assessment does not include other factors that affect apparent brightness (such as stellar masses, age, stage).

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Describe and graph the amounts of salt water and fresh water in various reservoirs to provide evidence about the distribution of water on Earth.

texts: Standard units are used to measure and describe physical quantities such as weight, and volume.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to oceans, lakes, rivers, glaciers, ground water, and polar ice caps, and does not include the atmosphere.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen.

texts: Natural objects exist from the very small to the immensely large.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the atomic-scale mechanism of evaporation and condensation or defining the unseen particles.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence supporting a model could include adding air to expand a basketball, compressing air in a syringe, dissolving sugar in water, and evaporating salt water.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change that occurs when heating, cooling, or mixing substances, the total weight of matter is conserved.

texts: Standard units are used to measure and describe physical quantities such as weight, time, temperature, and volume.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include distinguishing mass and weight.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of reactions or changes could include phase changes, dissolving, and mixing that form new substances.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations and measurements to identify materials based on their properties.

texts: Standard units are used to measure and describe physical quantities such as weight, time, temperature, and volume.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include density or distinguishing mass and weight.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of materials to be identified could include baking soda and other powders, metals, minerals, and liquids. Examples of properties could include color, hardness, reflectivity, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, response to magnetic forces, and solubility; density is not intended as an identifiable property.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model based on evidence to illustrate the life span of the sun and the role of nuclear fusion in the sun’s core to release energy in the form of radiation.

texts: The significance of a phenomenon is dependent on the scale, proportion, and quantity at which it occurs.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include details of the atomic and sub-atomic processes involved with the sun’s nuclear fusion.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the energy transfer mechanisms that allow energy from nuclear fusion in the sun’s core to reach Earth. Examples of evidence for the model include observations of the masses and lifetimes of other stars, as well as the ways that the sun’s radiation varies due to sudden solar flares (“space weather”), the 11-year sunspot cycle, and non-cyclic variations over centuries.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical or computational representations to predict the motion of orbiting objects in the solar system.

texts: Algebraic thinking is used to examine scientific data and predict the effect of a change in one variable on another (e.g., linear growth vs. exponential growth).

payload.assessment_boundary: Mathematical representations for the gravitational attraction of bodies and Kepler’s laws of orbital motions should not deal with more than two bodies, nor involve calculus.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on Newtonian gravitational laws governing orbital motions, which apply to human-made satellites as well as planets and moons.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical and/or computational representations to support explanations of factors that affect carrying capacity of ecosystems at different scales.

texts: The significance of a phenomenon is dependent on the scale, proportion, and quantity at which it occurs.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include deriving mathematical equations to make comparisons.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on quantitative analysis and comparison of the relationships among interdependent factors including boundaries, resources, climate, and competition. Examples of mathematical comparisons could include graphs, charts, histograms, and population changes gathered from simulations or historical data sets.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support and revise explanations based on evidence about factors affecting biodiversity and populations in ecosystems of different scales.

texts: Using the concept of orders of magnitude allows one to understand how a model at one scale relates to a model at another scale.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to provided data.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of mathematical representations include finding the average, determining trends, and using graphical comparisons of multiple sets of data.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to explain the variation and distribution of expressed traits in a population.

texts: Algebraic thinking is used to examine scientific data and predict the effect of a change in one variable on another (e.g., linear growth vs. exponential growth).

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy-Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the use of mathematics to describe the probability of traits as it relates to genetic and environmental factors in the expression of traits.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system.

texts: Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include recalling facts about properties of the planets and other solar system bodies.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the analysis of data from Earth-based instruments, space-based telescopes, and spacecraft to determine similarities and differences among solar system objects. Examples of scale properties include the sizes of an object's layers (such as crust and atmosphere), surface features (such as volcanoes), and orbital radius. Examples of data include statistical information, drawings and photographs, and models.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence from rock strata for how the geologic time scale is used to organize Earth's 4.6-billion-year-old history.

texts: Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include recalling the names of specific periods or epochs and events within them.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how analyses of rock formations and the fossils they contain are used to establish relative ages of major events in Earth's history. Examples of Earth's major events could range from being very recent (such as the last Ice Age or the earliest fossils of homo sapiens) to very old (such as the formation of Earth or the earliest evidence of life). Examples can include the formation of mountain chains and ocean basins, the evolution or extinction of particular living organisms, or significant volcanic eruptions.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales.

texts: Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how processes change Earth's surface at time and spatial scales that can be large (such as slow plate motions or the uplift of large mountain ranges) or small (such as rapid landslides or microscopic geochemical reactions), and how many geoscience processes (such as earthquakes, volcanoes, and meteor impacts) usually behave gradually but are punctuated by catastrophic events. Examples of geoscience processes include surface weathering and deposition by the movements of water, ice, and wind. Emphasis is on geoscience processes that shape local geographic features, where appropriate.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that living things are made of cells; either one cell or many different numbers and types of cells.

texts: Phenomena that can be observed at one scale may not be observable at another scale.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on developing evidence that living things are made of cells, distinguishing between living and non-living things, and understanding that living things may be made of one cell or many and varied cells.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop models to describe the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures.

texts: Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small. (MS-PS1-1)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include valence electrons and bonding energy, discussing the ionic nature of subunits of complex structures, or a complete depiction of all individual atoms in a complex molecule or extended structure.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on developing models of molecules that vary in complexity. Examples of simple molecules could include ammonia and methanol. Examples of extended structures could include sodium chloride or diamonds. Examples of molecular-level models could include drawings, 3D ball and stick structures, or computer representations showing different molecules with different types of atoms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass of an object and to the speed of an object.

texts: Proportional relationships (e.g. speed as the ratio of distance traveled to time taken) among different types of quantities provide information about the magnitude of properties and processes. (MS-PS3-1), (MS-PS3-4)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on descriptive relationships between kinetic energy and mass separately from kinetic energy and speed. Examples could include riding a bicycle at different speeds, rolling different sizes of rocks downhill, and getting hit by a wiffle ball versus a tennis ball.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.

texts: Proportional relationships (e.g. speed as the ratio of distance traveled to time taken) among different types of quantities provide information about the magnitude of properties and processes. (MS-PS3-1), (MS-PS3-4)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculating the total amount of thermal energy transferred.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of experiments could include comparing final water temperatures after different masses of ice melted in the same volume of water with the same initial temperature, the temperature change of samples of different materials with the same mass as they cool or heat in the environment, or the same material with different masses when a specific amount of energy is added.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-4
