chunks.text:
Concept SYS
Family: CCC
Title: Systems and System Models
Context: Crosscutting Concepts
Aliases: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions. | Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, and computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions — including energy, matter and information flows — within and between systems at different scales. | Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions — including energy, matter and information flows — within and between systems at different scales. | Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions — including energy, matter, and information flows — within and between systems at different scales. | Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions—including energy, matter, and information flows—within and between systems at different scales. | Models can be used to predict the behavior of a system, but these predictions have limited precision and reliability due to the assumptions and approximations inherent in models. | Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions – such as inputs, processes, and outputs – and energy and matter flows within systems. (MS-PS3-2) | Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions. | Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes and outputs—and energy and matter flows within systems. (MS-PS2-1), (MS-PS2-4) | Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes and outputs—and energy, matter, and information flows within systems. | SYS: Systems and System Models | Systems and System Models
Progressions: elementary: A system is a group of related parts that make up a whole and can carry out functions its individual parts cannot. | elementary: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions. | high: Systems can be designed to do specific tasks. | high: When investigating or describing a system, the boundaries and initial conditions of the system need to be defined and their inputs and outputs analyzed and described using models. | high: Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions—including energy, matter, and information flows—within and between systems at different scales. | high: Models can be used to predict the behavior of a system, but these predictions have limited precision and reliability due to the assumptions and approximations inherent in models. | middle: Systems may interact with other systems; they may have sub-systems and be a part of larger complex systems. | middle: Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes and outputs—and energy, matter, and information flows within systems. | middle: Models are limited in that they only represent certain aspects of the system under study. | primary: Objects and organisms can be described in terms of their parts. | primary: Systems in the natural and designed world have parts that work together.
chunks.title: SYS Systems and System Models

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem caused when the environment changes and the types of plants and animals that live there may change.*

texts: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to a single environmental change. Assessment does not include the greenhouse effect or climate change.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of environmental changes could include changes in land characteristics, water distribution, temperature, food, and other organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.

texts: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to macroscopic structures within plant and animal systems.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of structures could include thorns, stems, roots, colored petals, heart, stomach, lung, brain, and skin.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to describe that animals receive different types of information through their senses, process the information in their brain, and respond to the information in different ways.

texts: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the mechanisms by which the brain stores and recalls information or the mechanisms of how sensory receptors function.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on systems of information transfer.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model using an example to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and/or atmosphere interact.

texts: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to the interactions of two systems at a time.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include the influence of the ocean on ecosystems, landform shape, and climate; the influence of the atmosphere on landforms and ecosystems through weather and climate; and the influence of mountain ranges on winds and clouds in the atmosphere. The geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere are each a system.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science ideas to protect the Earth’s resources and environment.

texts: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.

texts: A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include molecular explanations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the idea that matter that is not food (air, water, decomposed materials in soil) is changed by plants into matter that is food. Examples of systems could include organisms, ecosystems, and the Earth.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-LS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a computational representation to illustrate the relationships among Earth systems and how those relationships are being modified due to human activity.

texts: When investigating or describing a system, the boundaries and initial conditions of the system need to be defined and their inputs and outputs analyzed and described using models.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include running computational representations but is limited to using the published results of scientific computational models.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of Earth systems to be considered are the hydrosphere, atmosphere, cryosphere, geosphere, and/or biosphere. An example of the far-reaching impacts from a human activity is how an increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide results in an increase in photosynthetic biomass on land and an increase in ocean acidification, with resulting impacts on sea organism health and marine populations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a computer simulation to model the impact of proposed solutions to a complex real-world problem with numerous criteria and constraints on interactions within and between systems relevant to the problem.

texts: Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions — including energy, matter, and information flows — within and between systems at different scales.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ETS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to illustrate the hierarchical organization of interacting systems that provide specific functions within multicellular organisms.

texts: Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions—including energy, matter, and information flows—within and between systems at different scales.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include interactions and functions at the molecular or chemical reaction level.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on functions at the organism system level such as nutrient uptake, water delivery, and organism movement in response to neural stimuli. An example of an interacting system could be an artery depending on the proper function of elastic tissue and smooth muscle to regulate and deliver the proper amount of blood within the circulatory system.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to illustrate the role of cellular division (mitosis) and differentiation in producing and maintaining complex organisms.

texts: Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions — including energy, matter, and information flows — within and between systems at different scales.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific gene control mechanisms or rote memorization of the steps of mitosis.
payload.clarification_statement: Assessment Boundary: Assessment does not include specific gene control mechanisms or rote memorization of the steps of mitosis.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to illustrate the role of photosynthesis and cellular respiration in the cycling of carbon among the biosphere, atmosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere.

texts: Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions — including energy, matter and information flows — within and between systems at different scales.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the specific chemical steps of photosynthesis and respiration.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include simulations and mathematical models.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support the claim that the total momentum of a system of objects is conserved when there is no net force on the system.

texts: When investigating or describing a system, the boundaries and initial conditions of the system need to be defined.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to systems of two macroscopic bodies moving in one dimension.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the quantitative conservation of momentum in interactions and the qualitative meaning of this principle.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create a computational model to calculate the change in the energy of one component in a system when the change in energy of the other component(s) and energy flows in and out of the system are known.

texts: Models can be used to predict the behavior of a system, but these predictions have limited precision and reliability due to the assumptions and approximations inherent in models.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to basic algebraic expressions or computations; to systems of two or three components; and to thermal energy, kinetic energy, and/or the energies in gravitational, magnetic, or electric fields.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on explaining the meaning of mathematical expressions used in the model.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that the transfer of thermal energy when two components of different temperature are combined within a closed system results in a more uniform energy distribution among the components in the system (second law of thermodynamics).

texts: When investigating or describing a system, the boundaries and initial conditions of the system need to be defined and their inputs and outputs analyzed and described using models.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to investigations based on materials and tools provided to students.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on analyzing data from student investigations and using mathematical thinking to describe the energy changes both quantitatively and conceptually. Examples of investigations could include mixing liquids at different initial temperatures or adding objects at different temperatures to water.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the claims, evidence, and reasoning behind the idea that electromagnetic radiation can be described either by a wave model or a particle model, and that for some situations one model is more useful than the other.

texts: Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, and computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions — including energy, matter and information flows — within and between systems at different scales.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include using quantum theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how the experimental evidence supports the claim and how a theory is generally modified in light of new evidence. Examples of a phenomenon could include resonance, interference, diffraction, and photoelectric effect.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how plants and animals (including humans) can change the environment to meet their needs.

texts: Systems in the natural and designed world have parts that work together.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of plants and animals changing their environment could include a squirrel digs in the ground to hide its food and tree roots can break concrete.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to represent the relationship between the needs of different plants or animals (including humans) and the places they live.

texts: Systems in the natural and designed world have parts that work together.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of relationships could include that deer eat buds and leaves, therefore, they usually live in forested areas; and, grasses need sunlight so they often grow in meadows. Plants, animals, and their surroundings make up a system.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe the role of gravity in the motions within galaxies and the solar system.

texts: Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Kepler's Laws of orbital motion or the apparent retrograde motion of the planets as viewed from Earth.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis for the model is on gravity as the force that holds together the solar system and Milky Way galaxy and controls orbital motions within them. Examples of models can be physical (such as the analogy of distance along a football field or computer visualizations of elliptical orbits) or conceptual (such as mathematical proportions relative to the size of familiar objects such as students' school or state).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates.

texts: Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes and outputs—and energy, matter, and information flows within systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the dynamics of the Coriolis effect.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how patterns vary by latitude, altitude, and geographic land distribution. Emphasis of atmospheric circulation is on the sunlight-driven latitudinal banding, the Coriolis effect, and resulting prevailing winds; emphasis of ocean circulation is on the transfer of heat by the global ocean convection cycle, which is constrained by the Coriolis effect and the outlines of continents. Examples of models can be diagrams, maps and globes, or digital representations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS2-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.

texts: Systems may interact with other systems; they may have sub-systems and be a part of larger complex systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the mechanism of one body system independent of others. Assessment is limited to the circulatory, excretory, digestive, respiratory, muscular, and nervous systems.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the conceptual understanding that cells form tissues and tissues form organs specialized for particular body functions. Examples could include the interaction of subsystems within a system and the normal functioning of those systems.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply Newton's Third Law to design a solution to a problem involving the motion of two colliding objects.*

texts: Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes and outputs—and energy and matter flows within systems. (MS-PS2-1), (MS-PS2-4)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to vertical or horizontal interactions in one dimension.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of practical problems could include the impact of collisions between two cars, between a car and stationary objects, and between a meteor and a space vehicle.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct and present arguments using evidence to support the claim that gravitational interactions are attractive and depend on the masses of interacting objects.

texts: Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes and outputs—and energy and matter flows within systems. (MS-PS2-1), (MS-PS2-4)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Newton's Law of Gravitation or Kepler's Laws.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence for arguments could include data generated from simulations or digital tools; and charts displaying mass, strength of interaction, distance from the Sun, and orbital periods of objects within the solar system.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe that when the arrangement of objects interacting at a distance changes, different amounts of potential energy are stored in the system.

texts: Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions – such as inputs, processes, and outputs – and energy and matter flows within systems. (MS-PS3-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to two objects and electric, magnetic, and gravitational interactions.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on relative amounts of potential energy, not on calculations of potential energy. Examples of objects within systems interacting at varying distances could include: the Earth and either a roller coaster cart at varying positions on a hill or objects at varying heights on shelves, changing the direction/orientation of a magnet, and a balloon with static electrical charge being brought closer to a classmate’s hair. Examples of models could include representations, diagrams, pictures, and written descriptions of systems.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-2

chunks.text:
Concept AOC
Family: CNS
Title: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Context: Connections to the Nature of Science
Aliases: AOC: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems | Connections to the Nature of Science | Science assumes consistent patterns in natural systems. | Science assumes natural events happen today as they happened in the past. | Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Progressions: elementary: Science assumes consistent patterns in natural systems. | elementary: Basic laws of nature are the same everywhere in the universe. | high: Scientific knowledge is based on the assumption that natural laws operate today as they did in the past and they will continue to do so in the future. | high: Science assumes the universe is a vast single system in which basic laws are consistent. | middle: Science assumes that objects and events in natural systems occur in consistent patterns that are understandable through measurement and observation. | middle: Science carefully considers and evaluates anomalies in data and evidence. | primary: Science assumes natural events happen today as they happened in the past. | primary: Many events are repeated.
chunks.title: AOC Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems

records.description: Connections to the Nature of Science

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use observations of the sun, moon, and stars to describe patterns that can be predicted.

edge_payload.raw_id: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
texts: Science assumes natural events happen today as they happened in the past.
Many events are repeated.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of star patterns is limited to stars being seen at night and not during the day.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include that the sun and moon appear to rise in one part of the sky, move across the sky, and set; and stars other than our sun are visible at night but not during the day.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of the organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.

edge_payload.raw_id: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
texts: Science assumes consistent patterns in natural systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include identification of specific fossils or present plants and animals. Assessment is limited to major fossil types and relative ages.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include type, size, and distributions of fossil organisms. Examples of fossils and environments could include marine fossils found on dry land, tropical plant fossils found in Arctic areas, and fossils of extinct organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Identify evidence from patterns in rock formations and fossils in rock layers to support an explanation for changes in a landscape over time.

edge_payload.raw_id: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
texts: Science assumes consistent patterns in natural systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific knowledge of the mechanism of rock formation or memorization of specific rock formations and layers. Assessment is limited to relative time.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence from patterns could include rock layers with marine shell fossils above rock layers with plant fossils and no shells, indicating a change from land to water over time; and, a canyon with different rock layers in the walls and a river in the bottom, indicating that over time a river cut through the rock.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change that occurs when heating, cooling, or mixing substances, the total weight of matter is conserved.

edge_payload.raw_id: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
texts: Science assumes consistent patterns in natural systems.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include distinguishing mass and weight.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of reactions or changes could include phase changes, dissolving, and mixing that form new substances.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation of the Big Bang theory based on astronomical evidence of light spectra, motion of distant galaxies, and composition of matter in the universe.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Scientific knowledge is based on the assumption that natural laws operate today as they did in the past and they will continue to do so in the future.
Science assumes the universe is a vast single system in which basic laws are consistent.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the astronomical evidence of the red shift of light from galaxies as an indication that the universe is currently expanding, the cosmic microwave background as the remnant radiation from the Big Bang, and the observed composition of ordinary matter of the universe, primarily found in stars and interstellar gases (from the spectra of electromagnetic radiation from stars), which matches that predicted by the Big Bang theory (3/4 hydrogen and 1/4 helium).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific information that common ancestry and biological evolution are supported by multiple lines of empirical evidence.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Scientific knowledge is based on the assumption that natural laws operate today as they did in the past and they will continue to do so in the future.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a conceptual understanding of the role each line of evidence has relating to common ancestry and biological evolution. Examples of evidence could include similarities in DNA sequences, anatomical structures, and order of appearance of structures in embryological development.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how natural selection leads to adaptation of populations.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Scientific knowledge is based on the assumption that natural laws operate today as they did in the past and they will continue to do so in the future.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using data to provide evidence for how specific biotic and abiotic differences in ecosystems (such as ranges of seasonal temperature, long-term climate change, acidity, light, geographic barriers, or evolution of other organisms) contribute to a change in gene frequency over time, leading to adaptation of populations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support the claim that atoms, and therefore mass, are conserved during a chemical reaction.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Science assumes the universe is a vast single system in which basic laws are consistent.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include complex chemical reactions.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using mathematical ideas to communicate the proportional relationships between masses of atoms in the reactants and the products, and the translation of these relationships to the macroscopic scale using the mole as the conversion from the atomic to the macroscopic scale. Emphasis is on assessing students’ use of mathematical thinking and not on memorization and rote application of problem-solving techniques.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS1-7

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create a computational model to calculate the change in the energy of one component in a system when the change in energy of the other component(s) and energy flows in and out of the system are known.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Science assumes the universe is a vast single system in which basic laws are consistent.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to basic algebraic expressions or computations; to systems of two or three components; and to thermal energy, kinetic energy, and/or the energies in gravitational, magnetic, or electric fields.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on explaining the meaning of mathematical expressions used in the model.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model of the Earth-sun-moon system to describe the cyclic patterns of lunar phases, eclipses of the sun and moon, and seasons.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Science assumes that objects and events in natural systems occur in consistent patterns that are understandable through measurement and observation.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models can be physical, graphical, or conceptual.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe the role of gravity in the motions within galaxies and the solar system.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Science assumes that objects and events in natural systems occur in consistent patterns that are understandable through measurement and observation.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Kepler's Laws of orbital motion or the apparent retrograde motion of the planets as viewed from Earth.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis for the model is on gravity as the force that holds together the solar system and Milky Way galaxy and controls orbital motions within them. Examples of models can be physical (such as the analogy of distance along a football field or computer visualizations of elliptical orbits) or conceptual (such as mathematical proportions relative to the size of familiar objects such as students' school or state).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of matter and flow of energy among living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Assumes an Order and Consistency in Natural Systems
Science assumes that objects and events in natural systems occur in consistent patterns that are understandable through measurement and observation.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the use of chemical reactions to describe the processes.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on describing the conservation of matter and flow of energy into and out of various ecosystems, and on defining the boundaries of the system.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on finding patterns of changes in the level of complexity of anatomical structures in organisms and the chronological order of fossil appearance in the rock layers.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for the anatomical similarities and differences among modern organisms and between modern and fossil organisms to infer evolutionary relationships.

linked_performance_expectations.description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Addresses Questions About the Natural and Material World
Scientists study the natural and material world.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include different designs of dikes and windbreaks to hold back wind and water, and different designs for using shrubs, grass, and trees to hold back the land.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science ideas to protect the Earth’s resources and environment.

edge_payload.raw_id: Science Addresses Questions About the Natural and Material World
texts: Science findings are limited to questions that can be answered with empirical evidence.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions for developing, managing, and utilizing energy and mineral resources based on cost-benefit ratios.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Addresses Questions About the Natural and Material World
Science and technology may raise ethical issues for which science, by itself, does not provide answers and solutions.
Science knowledge indicates what can happen in natural systems — not what should happen. The latter involves ethics, values, and human decisions about the use of knowledge.
Many decisions are not made using science alone, but rely on social and cultural contexts to resolve issues.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the conservation, recycling, and reuse of resources (such as minerals and metals) where possible, and on minimizing impacts where it is not. Examples include developing best practices for agricultural soil use, mining (for coal, tar sands, and oil shales), and pumping (for petroleum and natural gas). Science knowledge indicates what can happen in natural systems—not what should happen.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Addresses Questions About the Natural and Material World
Scientific knowledge can describe the consequences of actions but does not necessarily prescribe the decisions that society takes.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence include grade-appropriate databases on human populations and the rates of consumption of food and natural resources (such as freshwater, mineral, and energy). Examples of impacts can include changes to the appearance, composition, and structure of Earth's systems as well as the rates at which they change. The consequences of increases in human populations and consumption of natural resources are described by science, but science does not make the decisions for the actions society takes.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Addresses Questions About the Natural and Material World
Scientific knowledge can describe the consequences of actions but does not necessarily prescribe the decisions that society takes.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of ecosystem services could include water purification, nutrient recycling, and prevention of soil erosion. Examples of design solution constraints could include scientific, economic, and social considerations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and synthesize information about the technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Addresses Questions About the Natural and Material World
Scientific knowledge can describe the consequences of actions but does not necessarily prescribe the decisions that society takes.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on synthesizing information from reliable sources about the influence of humans on genetic outcomes in artificial selection (such as genetic modification, animal husbandry, gene therapy); and, on the impacts these technologies have on society as well as the technologies leading to these scientific discoveries.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-5

records.description: Connections to the Nature of Science

linked_performance_expectations.description: Read texts and use media to determine patterns in behavior of parents and offspring that help offspring survive.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Scientists look for patterns and order when making observations about the world.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns of behaviors could include the signals that offspring make (such as crying, cheeping, and other vocalizations) and the responses of the parents (such as feeding, comforting, and protecting the offspring).
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations of plants and animals to compare the diversity of life in different habitats.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Scientists look for patterns and order when making observations about the world.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific animal and plant names in specific habitats.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the diversity of living things in each of a variety of different habitats.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science findings are based on recognizing patterns.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of plant life cycles is limited to those of flowering plants. Assessment does not include details of human reproduction.
payload.clarification_statement: Changes organisms go through during their life form a pattern.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations and/or measurements of an object’s motion to provide evidence that a pattern can be used to predict future motion.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science findings are based on recognizing patterns.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include technical terms such as period and frequency.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of motion with a predictable pattern could include a child swinging in a swing, a ball rolling back and forth in a bowl, and two children on a see-saw.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-PS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength and that waves can cause objects to move.

edge_payload.raw_id: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
texts: Science findings are based on recognizing patterns.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include interference effects, electromagnetic waves, non-periodic waves, or quantitative models of amplitude and wavelength.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include diagrams, analogies, and physical models using wire to illustrate wavelength and amplitude of waves.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model based on evidence of Earth’s interior to describe the cycling of matter by thermal convection.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based on empirical evidence.
Science disciplines share common rules of evidence used to evaluate explanations about natural systems.
Science includes the process of coordinating patterns of evidence with current theory.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both a one-dimensional model of Earth, with radial layers determined by density, and a three-dimensional model, which is controlled by mantle convection and the resulting plate tectonics. Examples of evidence include maps of Earth’s three-dimensional structure obtained from seismic waves, records of the rate of change of Earth’s magnetic field (as constraints on convection in the outer core), and identification of the composition of Earth’s layers from high-pressure laboratory experiments.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to describe how variations in the flow of energy into and out of Earth’s systems result in changes in climate.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science arguments are strengthened by multiple lines of evidence supporting a single explanation.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of the results of changes in climate is limited to changes in surface temperatures, precipitation patterns, glacial ice volumes, sea levels, and biosphere distribution.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of the causes of climate change differ by timescale, over 1-10 years: large volcanic eruption, ocean circulation; 10-100s of years: changes in human activity, ocean circulation, solar output; 10-100s of thousands of years: changes to Earth's orbit and the orientation of its axis; and 10-100s of millions of years: long-term changes in atmospheric composition.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use and share observations of local weather conditions to describe patterns over time.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge Is Based on Empirical Evidence
Scientists look for patterns and order when making observations about the world.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of quantitative observations limited to whole numbers and relative measures such as warmer/cooler.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of qualitative observations could include descriptions of the weather (such as sunny, cloudy, rainy, and warm); examples of quantitative observations could include numbers of sunny, windy, and rainy days in a month. Examples of patterns could include that it is usually cooler in the morning than in the afternoon and the number of sunny days versus cloudy days in different months.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Scientists look for patterns and order when making observations about the world.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include that animals need to take in food but plants do not; the different kinds of food needed by different types of animals; the requirement of plants to have light; and, that all living things need water.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for the role of photosynthesis in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical connections between evidence and explanations.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the biochemical mechanisms of photosynthesis.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on tracing movement of matter and flow of energy.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by empirical evidence that changes to physical or biological components of an ecosystem affect populations.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science disciplines share common rules of obtaining and evaluating empirical evidence.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on recognizing patterns in data and making warranted inferences about changes in populations, and on evaluating empirical evidence supporting arguments about changes to ecosystems.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical and conceptual connections between evidence and explanations.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the names of individual species or geological eras in the fossil record.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on finding patterns of changes in the level of complexity of anatomical structures in organisms and the chronological order of fossil appearance in the rock layers.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical and conceptual connections between evidence and explanations.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to analysis of the following properties: density, melting point, boiling point, solubility, flammability, and odor.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of reactions could include burning sugar or steel wool, fat reacting with sodium hydroxide, and mixing zinc with hydrogen chloride.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object's motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical and conceptual connections between evidence and explanations.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to forces and changes in motion in one-dimension in an inertial reference frame and to change in one variable at a time. Assessment does not include the use of trigonometry.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on balanced (Newton's First Law) and unbalanced forces in a system, qualitative comparisons of forces, mass and changes in motion (Newton's Second Law), frame of reference, and specification of units.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct and present arguments using evidence to support the claim that gravitational interactions are attractive and depend on the masses of interacting objects.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical and conceptual connections between evidence and explanations.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Newton's Law of Gravitation or Kepler's Laws.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence for arguments could include data generated from simulations or digital tools; and charts displaying mass, strength of interaction, distance from the Sun, and orbital periods of objects within the solar system.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical and conceptual connections between evidence and explanations

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculating the total amount of thermal energy transferred.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of experiments could include comparing final water temperatures after different masses of ice melted in the same volume of water with the same initial temperature, the temperature change of samples of different materials with the same mass as they cool or heat in the environment, or the same material with different masses when a specific amount of energy is added.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct, use, and present arguments to support the claim that when the kinetic energy of an object changes, energy is transferred to or from the object.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical and conceptual connections between evidence and explanations

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculations of energy.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of empirical evidence used in arguments could include an inventory or other representation of the energy before and after the transfer in the form of temperature changes or motion of object.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Based on Empirical Evidence
Science knowledge is based upon logical and conceptual connections between evidence and explanations.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include electromagnetic waves and is limited to standard repeating waves.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on describing waves with both qualitative and quantitative thinking.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-1

chunks.text:
Concept ENP
Family: CNS
Title: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena
Context: Connections to the Nature of Science
Aliases: Connections to the Nature of Science | ENP: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena | Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena
Progressions: elementary: Science theories are based on a body of evidence and many tests. | elementary: Science explanations describe the mechanisms for natural events. | high: Theories and laws provide explanations in science, but theories do not with time become laws or facts. | high: A scientific theory is a substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world, based on a body of facts that has been repeatedly confirmed through observation and experiment, and the science community validates each theory before it is accepted. If new evidence is discovered that the theory does not accommodate, the theory is generally modified in light of this new evidence. | high: Models, mechanisms, and explanations collectively serve as tools in the development of a scientific theory. | high: Laws are statements or descriptions of the relationships among observable phenomena. | high: Scientists often use hypotheses to develop and test theories and explanations. | middle: Theories are explanations for observable phenomena. | middle: Science theories are based on a body of evidence developed over time. | middle: Laws are regularities or mathematical descriptions of natural phenomena. | middle: A hypothesis is used by scientists as an idea that may contribute important new knowledge for the evaluation of a scientific theory. | middle: The term 'theory' as used in science is very different from the common use outside of science.
chunks.title: ENP Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument with evidence that some changes caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some cannot.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena
Science searches for cause and effect relationships to explain natural events.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of reversible changes could include materials such as water and butter at different temperatures. Examples of irreversible changes could include cooking an egg, freezing a plant leaf, and heating paper.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-PS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena
Science explanations describe the mechanisms for natural events.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include molecular explanations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the idea that matter that is not food (air, water, decomposed materials in soil) is changed by plants into matter that is food. Examples of systems could include organisms, ecosystems, and the Earth.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-LS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation of the Big Bang theory based on astronomical evidence of light spectra, motion of distant galaxies, and composition of matter in the universe.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena

A scientific theory is a substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world, based on a body of facts that have been repeatedly confirmed through observation and experiment and the science community validates each theory before it is accepted. If new evidence is discovered that the theory does not accommodate, the theory is generally modified in light of this new evidence.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the astronomical evidence of the red shift of light from galaxies as an indication that the universe is currently expanding, the cosmic microwave background as the remnant radiation from the Big Bang, and the observed composition of ordinary matter of the universe, primarily found in stars and interstellar gases (from the spectra of electromagnetic radiation from stars), which matches that predicted by the Big Bang theory (3/4 hydrogen and 1/4 helium).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific reasoning and evidence from ancient Earth materials, meteorites, and other planetary surfaces to construct an account of Earth’s formation and early history.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena

A scientific theory is a substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world, based on a body of facts that have been repeatedly confirmed through observation and experiment, and the science community validates each theory before it is accepted. If new evidence is discovered that the theory does not accommodate, the theory is generally modified in light of this new evidence.
Models, mechanisms, and explanations collectively serve as tools in the development of a scientific theory.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using available evidence within the solar system to reconstruct the early history of Earth, which formed along with the rest of the solar system 4.6 billion years ago. Examples of evidence include the absolute ages of ancient materials (obtained by radiometric dating of meteorites, moon rocks, and Earth’s oldest minerals), the sizes and compositions of solar system objects, and the impact cratering record of planetary surfaces.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific information that common ancestry and biological evolution are supported by multiple lines of empirical evidence.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena

A scientific theory is a substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world, based on a body of facts that have been repeatedly confirmed through observation and experiment and the science community validates each theory before it is accepted. If new evidence is discovered that the theory does not accommodate, the theory is generally modified in light of this new evidence.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a conceptual understanding of the role each line of evidence has relating to common ancestry and biological evolution. Examples of evidence could include similarities in DNA sequences, anatomical structures, and order of appearance of structures in embryological development.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data to support the claim that Newton’s second law of motion describes the mathematical relationship among the net force on a macroscopic object, its mass, and its acceleration.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena
Theories and laws provide explanations in science.
Laws are statements or descriptions of the relationships among observable phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to one-dimensional motion and to macroscopic objects moving at non-relativistic speeds.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include tables or graphs of position or velocity as a function of time for objects subject to a net unbalanced force, such as a falling object, an object rolling down a ramp, or a moving object being pulled by a constant force.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations of Newton’s Law of Gravitation and Coulomb’s Law to describe and predict the gravitational and electrostatic forces between objects.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena
Theories and laws provide explanations in science.
Laws are statements or descriptions of the relationships among observable phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to systems with two objects.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both quantitative and conceptual descriptions of gravitational and electric fields.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the claims, evidence, and reasoning behind the idea that electromagnetic radiation can be described either by a wave model or a particle model, and that for some situations one model is more useful than the other.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena

A scientific theory is a substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world, based on a body of facts that have been repeatedly confirmed through observation and experiment, and the science community validates each theory before it is accepted. If new evidence is discovered that the theory does not accommodate, the theory is generally modified in light of this new evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include using quantum theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how the experimental evidence supports the claim and how a theory is generally modified in light of new evidence. Examples of a phenomenon could include resonance, interference, diffraction, and photoelectric effect.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe how the total number of atoms does not change in a chemical reaction and thus mass is conserved.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science Models, Laws, Mechanisms, and Theories Explain Natural Phenomena
Laws are regularities or mathematical descriptions of natural phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the use of atomic masses, balancing symbolic equations, or intermolecular forces.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on law of conservation of matter and on physical models or drawings, including digital forms, that represent atoms.

records.description: Connections to the Nature of Science

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make a claim about the merit of a design solution that reduces the impacts of a weather-related hazard.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science is a Human Endeavor
Science affects everyday life.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of design solutions to weather-related hazards could include barriers to prevent flooding, wind resistant roofs, and lighting rods.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.*

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science is a Human Endeavor
Most scientists and engineers work in teams.
Science affects everyday life.

payload.assessment_boundary: Devices should be limited to those that convert motion energy to electric energy or use stored energy to cause motion or produce light or sound.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include electric circuits that convert electrical energy into motion energy of a vehicle, light, or sound; and, a passive solar heater that converts light into heat. Examples of constraints could include the materials, cost, or time to design the device.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create a computational simulation to illustrate the relationships among management of natural resources, the sustainability of human populations, and biodiversity.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science is a Human Endeavor
Science is a result of human endeavors, imagination, and creativity.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment for computational simulations is limited to using provided multi-parameter programs or constructing simplified spreadsheet calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of factors that affect the management of natural resources include costs of resource extraction and waste management, per-capita consumption, and the development of new technologies. Examples of factors that affect human sustainability include agricultural efficiency, levels of conservation, and urban planning.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to explain the variation and distribution of expressed traits in a population.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science is a Human Endeavor
Technological advances have influenced the progress of science and science has influenced advances in technology.
Science and engineering are influenced by society and society is influenced by science and engineering.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy-Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the use of mathematics to describe the probability of traits as it relates to genetic and environmental factors in the expression of traits.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science is a Human Endeavor
Scientists and engineers are guided by habits of mind such as intellectual honesty, tolerance of ambiguity, skepticism, and openness to new ideas.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the mechanism of one body system independent of others. Assessment is limited to the circulatory, excretory, digestive, respiratory, muscular, and nervous systems.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the conceptual understanding that cells form tissues and tissues form organs specialized for particular body functions. Examples could include the interaction of subsystems within a system and the normal functioning of those systems.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that digitized signals are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information than analog signals.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Science is a Human Endeavor
Advances in technology influence the progress of science and science has influenced advances in technology.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include binary counting. Assessment does not include the specific mechanism of any given device.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a basic understanding that waves can be used for communication purposes. Examples could include using fiber optic cable to transmit light pulses, radio wave pulses in wifi devices, and conversion of stored binary patterns to make sound or text on a computer screen.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support and revise explanations based on evidence about factors affecting biodiversity and populations in ecosystems of different scales.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Open to Revision in Light of New Evidence
Most scientific knowledge is quite durable, but is, in principle, subject to change based on new evidence and/or reinterpretation of existing evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to provided data.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of mathematical representations include finding the average, determining trends, and using graphical comparisons of multiple sets of data.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct and revise an explanation based on evidence for the cycling of matter and flow of energy in aerobic and anaerobic conditions.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Open to Revision in Light of New Evidence
Most scientific knowledge is quite durable, but is, in principle, subject to change based on new evidence and/or reinterpretation of existing evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the specific chemical processes of either aerobic or anaerobic respiration.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on conceptual understanding of the role of aerobic and anaerobic respiration in different environments.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the claims, evidence, and reasoning that the complex interactions in ecosystems maintain relatively consistent numbers and types of organisms in stable conditions, but changing conditions may result in a new ecosystem.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Open to Revision in Light of New Evidence
Scientific argumentation is a mode of logical discourse used to clarify the strength of relationships between ideas and evidence that may result in revision of an explanation.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of changes in ecosystem conditions could include modest biological or physical changes, such as moderate hunting or a seasonal flood; and extreme changes, such as volcanic eruption or sea level rise.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the evidence for the role of group behavior on individual and species’ chances to survive and reproduce.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Open to Revision in Light of New Evidence
Scientific argumentation is a mode of logical discourse used to clarify the strength of relationships between ideas and evidence that may result in revision of an explanation.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on: (1) distinguishing between group and individual behavior, (2) identifying evidence supporting the outcomes of group behavior, and (3) developing logical and reasonable arguments based on evidence. Examples of group behaviors could include flocking, schooling, herding, and cooperative behaviors such as hunting, migrating, and swarming.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-8

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions.

edge_payload.raw_id: Connections to the Nature of Science
texts: Scientific Knowledge is Open to Revision in Light of New Evidence
Science findings are frequently revised and/or reinterpreted based on new evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Paleomagnetic anomalies in oceanic and continental crust are not assessed.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data include similarities of rock and fossil types on different continents, the shapes of the continents (including continental shelves), and the locations of ocean structures (such as ridges, fracture zones, and trenches).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS2-3
