chunks.title: ESS3.C Human Impacts on Earth Systems

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science ideas to protect the Earth’s resources and environment.

edge_payload.raw_id: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems
texts: Human activities in agriculture, industry, and everyday life have had major effects on the land, vegetation, streams, ocean, air, and even outer space. But individuals and communities are doing things to help protect Earth's resources and environments.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create a computational simulation to illustrate the relationships among management of natural resources, the sustainability of human populations, and biodiversity.

edge_payload.raw_id: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems
texts: The sustainability of human societies and the biodiversity that supports them requires responsible management of natural resources.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment for computational simulations is limited to using provided multi-parameter programs or constructing simplified spreadsheet calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of factors that affect the management of natural resources include costs of resource extraction and waste management, per-capita consumption, and the development of new technologies. Examples of factors that affect human sustainability include agricultural efficiency, levels of conservation, and urban planning.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate or refine a technological solution that reduces impacts of human activities on natural systems.*

edge_payload.raw_id: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems
texts: Scientists and engineers can make major contributions by developing technologies that produce less pollution and waste and that preclude ecosystem degradation.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data on the impacts of human activities could include the quantities and types of pollutants released, changes to biomass and species diversity, or areal changes in land surface use (such as for urban development, agriculture and livestock, or surface mining). Examples for limiting future impacts could range from local efforts (such as reducing, reusing, and recycling resources) to large-scale geoengineering design solutions (such as altering global temperatures by making large changes to the atmosphere or ocean).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how plants and animals (including humans) can change the environment to meet their needs.

edge_payload.raw_id: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems (secondary)
texts: Things that people do to live comfortably can affect the world around them. But they can make choices that reduce their impacts on the land, water, air, and other living things. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of plants and animals changing their environment could include a squirrel digs in the ground to hide its food and tree roots can break concrete.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the land, water, air, and/or other living things in the local environment.*

edge_payload.raw_id: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems
texts: Things that people do to live comfortably can affect the world around them. But they can make choices that reduce their impacts on the land, water, air, and other living things.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human impact on the land could include cutting trees to produce paper and using resources to produce bottles. Examples of solutions could include reusing paper and recycling cans and bottles.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing a human impact on the environment.

edge_payload.raw_id: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems
texts: Human activities have significantly altered the biosphere, sometimes damaging or destroying natural habitats and causing the extinction of other species. But changes to Earth's environments can have different impacts (negative and positive) for different living things.
Typically as human populations and per-capita consumption of natural resources increase, so do the negative impacts on Earth unless the activities and technologies involved are engineered otherwise.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of the design process include examining human environmental impacts, assessing the kinds of solutions that are feasible, and designing and evaluating solutions that could reduce that impact. Examples of human impacts can include water usage (such as the withdrawal of water from streams and aquifers or the construction of dams and levees), land usage (such as urban development, agriculture, or the removal of wetlands), and pollution (such as of the air, water, or land).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.

edge_payload.raw_id: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems
texts: Typically as human populations and per-capita consumption of natural resources increase, so do the negative impacts on Earth unless the activities and technologies involved are engineered otherwise.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence include grade-appropriate databases on human populations and the rates of consumption of food and natural resources (such as freshwater, mineral, and energy). Examples of impacts can include changes to the appearance, composition, and structure of Earth's systems as well as the rates at which they change. The consequences of increases in human populations and consumption of natural resources are described by science, but science does not make the decisions for the actions society takes.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-4

payload.full_name: ESS3.C: Human Impacts on Earth Systems

progression_statements.description: Human activities in agriculture, industry, and everyday life have had major effects on the land, vegetation, streams, ocean, air, and even outer space. But individuals and communities are doing things to help protect Earth's resources and environments.

payload.text: Human activities in agriculture, industry, and everyday life have had major effects on the land, vegetation, streams, ocean, air, and even outer space. But individuals and communities are doing things to help protect Earth's resources and environments.

progression_statements.title: ESS3.C-E1

progression_statements.description: The sustainability of human societies and the biodiversity that supports them requires responsible management of natural resources.

payload.text: The sustainability of human societies and the biodiversity that supports them requires responsible management of natural resources.

progression_statements.title: ESS3.C-H1

progression_statements.description: Scientists and engineers can make major contributions by developing technologies that produce less pollution and waste and that preclude ecosystem degradation.

payload.text: Scientists and engineers can make major contributions by developing technologies that produce less pollution and waste and that preclude ecosystem degradation.

progression_statements.title: ESS3.C-H2

progression_statements.description: Human activities have significantly altered the biosphere, sometimes damaging or destroying natural habitats and causing the extinction of other species. But changes to Earth's environments can have different impacts (negative and positive) for different living things.

payload.text: Human activities have significantly altered the biosphere, sometimes damaging or destroying natural habitats and causing the extinction of other species. But changes to Earth's environments can have different impacts (negative and positive) for different living things.

progression_statements.title: ESS3.C-M1

progression_statements.description: Typically as human populations and per capita consumption of natural resources increase, so do the negative impacts on Earth unless the activities and technologies involved are engineered otherwise.

payload.text: Typically as human populations and per capita consumption of natural resources increase, so do the negative impacts on Earth unless the activities and technologies involved are engineered otherwise.

progression_statements.title: ESS3.C-M2

progression_statements.description: Things that people do to live comfortably can affect the world around them. But they can make choices that reduce their impacts on the land, water, air, and other living things.

payload.text: Things that people do to live comfortably can affect the world around them. But they can make choices that reduce their impacts on the land, water, air, and other living things.

progression_statements.title: ESS3.C-P1

aliases.alias_text: HS.ESS3.D
aliases.normalized_alias: hs.ess3.d

aliases.alias_text: Human activities, such as the release of greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels, are major factors in the current rise in Earth's mean surface temperature (global warming). Reducing the level of climate change and reducing human vulnerability to whatever climate changes do occur depend on the understanding of climate science, engineering capabilities, and other kinds of knowledge, such as understanding of human behavior and on applying that knowledge wisely in decisions and activities.
aliases.normalized_alias: human activities, such as the release of greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels, are major factors in the current rise in earth's mean surface temperature (global warming). reducing the level of climate change and reducing human vulnerability to whatever climate changes do occur depend on the understanding of climate science, engineering capabilities, and other kinds of knowledge, such as understanding of human behavior and on applying that knowledge wisely in decisions and activities.

aliases.alias_text: MS.ESS3.D
aliases.normalized_alias: ms.ess3.d

aliases.alias_text: Though the magnitudes of human impacts are greater than they have ever been, so too are human abilities to model, predict, and manage current and future impacts.
aliases.normalized_alias: though the magnitudes of human impacts are greater than they have ever been, so too are human abilities to model, predict, and manage current and future impacts.

aliases.alias_text: Through computer simulations and other studies, important discoveries are still being made about how the ocean, the atmosphere, and the biosphere interact and are modified in response to human activities.
aliases.normalized_alias: through computer simulations and other studies, important discoveries are still being made about how the ocean, the atmosphere, and the biosphere interact and are modified in response to human activities.

payload.core_idea: ESS3: Earth and Human Activity

chunks.text:
Concept ESS3.D
Family: DCI
Title: Global Climate Change
Context: ESS3: Earth and Human Activity
Aliases: ESS3.D | ESS3.D: Global Climate Change | Global Climate Change | HS.ESS3.D | Human activities, such as the release of greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels, are major factors in the current rise in Earth's mean surface temperature (global warming). Reducing the level of climate change and reducing human vulnerability to whatever climate changes do occur depend on the understanding of climate science, engineering capabilities, and other kinds of knowledge, such as understanding of human behavior and on applying that knowledge wisely in decisions and activities. | MS.ESS3.D | Though the magnitudes of human impacts are greater than they have ever been, so too are human abilities to model, predict, and manage current and future impacts. | Through computer simulations and other studies, important discoveries are still being made about how the ocean, the atmosphere, and the biosphere interact and are modified in response to human activities.
Progressions: high: Though the magnitudes of human impacts are greater than they have ever been, so too are human abilities to model, predict, and manage current and future impacts. | high: Through computer simulations and other studies, important discoveries are still being made about how the ocean, the atmosphere, and the biosphere interact and are modified in response to human activities. | middle: Human activities, such as the release of greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels, are major factors in the current rise in Earth's mean surface temperature (global warming). Reducing the level of climate change and reducing human vulnerability to whatever climate changes do occur depend on the understanding of climate science, engineering capabilities, and other kinds of knowledge, such as understanding of human behavior, and on applying that knowledge wisely in decisions and activities.
chunks.title: ESS3.D Global Climate Change

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze geoscience data and the results from global climate models to make an evidence-based forecast of the current rate of global or regional climate change and associated future impacts to Earth systems.

texts: Though the magnitudes of human impacts are greater than they have ever been, so too are human abilities to model, predict, and manage current and future impacts.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to one example of a climate change and its associated impacts.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence, for both data and climate model outputs, are for climate changes (such as precipitation and temperature) and their associated impacts (such as on sea level, glacial ice volumes, or atmosphere and ocean composition).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a computational representation to illustrate the relationships among Earth systems and how those relationships are being modified due to human activity.

texts: Through computer simulations and other studies, important discoveries are still being made about how the ocean, the atmosphere, and the biosphere interact and are modified in response to human activities.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include running computational representations but is limited to using the published results of scientific computational models.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of Earth systems to be considered are the hydrosphere, atmosphere, cryosphere, geosphere, and/or biosphere. An example of the far-reaching impacts from a human activity is how an increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide results in an increase in photosynthetic biomass on land and an increase in ocean acidification, with resulting impacts on sea organism health and marine populations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century.

texts: Human activities, such as the release of greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels, are major factors in the current rise in Earth's mean surface temperature (global warming). Reducing the level of climate change and reducing human vulnerability to whatever climate changes do occur depend on the understanding of climate science, engineering capabilities, and other kinds of knowledge, such as understanding of human behavior and on applying that knowledge wisely in decisions and activities.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of factors include human activities (such as fossil fuel combustion, cement production, and agricultural activity) and natural processes (such as changes in incoming solar radiation or volcanic activity). Examples of evidence can include tables, graphs, and maps of global and regional temperatures, atmospheric levels of gases such as carbon dioxide and methane, and the rates of human activities. Emphasis is on the major role that human activities play in causing the rise in global temperatures.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-5

aliases.alias_text: Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.

chunks.title: ETS1.A Defining and Delimiting an Engineering Problem

linked_performance_expectations.description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems
texts: Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-5-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.*

texts: Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.(secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Devices should be limited to those that convert motion energy to electric energy or use stored energy to cause motion or produce light or sound.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include electric circuits that convert electrical energy into motion energy of a vehicle, light, or sound; and, a passive solar heater that converts light into heat. Examples of constraints could include the materials, cost, or time to design the device.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze a major global challenge to specify qualitative and quantitative criteria and constraints for solutions that account for societal needs and wants.

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems
texts: Criteria and constraints also include satisfying any requirements set by society, such as taking issues of risk mitigation into account, and they should be quantified to the extent possible and stated in such a way that one can tell if a given design meets them.

Humanity faces major global challenges today, such as the need for supplies of clean water and food or for energy sources that minimize pollution, which can be addressed through engineering. These global challenges also may have manifestations in local communities.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific and engineering ideas to design, evaluate, and refine a device that minimizes the force on a macroscopic object during a collision.*

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting an Engineering Problem (secondary)
texts: Criteria and constraints also include satisfying any requirements set by society, such as taking issues of risk mitigation into account, and they should be quantified to the extent possible and stated in such a way that one can tell if a given design meets them. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative evaluations and/or algebraic manipulations.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evaluation and refinement could include determining the success of the device at protecting an object from damage and modifying the design to improve it. Examples of a device could include a football helmet or a parachute.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Design, build, and refine a device that works within given constraints to convert one form of energy into another form of energy.*

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting an Engineering Problem (secondary)
texts: Criteria and constraints also include satisfying any requirements set by society, such as taking issues of risk mitigation into account, and they should be quantified to the extent possible and stated in such a way that one can tell if a given design meets them. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment for quantitative evaluations is limited to total output for a given input. Assessment is limited to devices constructed with materials provided to students.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both qualitative and quantitative evaluations of devices. Examples of devices could include Rube Goldberg devices, wind turbines, solar cells, solar ovens, and generators. Examples of constraints could include use of renewable energy forms and efficiency.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems
texts: A situation that people want to change or create can be approached as a problem to be solved through engineering.
Asking questions, making observations, and gathering information are helpful in thinking about problems.
Before beginning to design a solution, it is important to clearly understand the problem.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-2-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for, and respond to, severe weather.*

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting an Engineering Problem (secondary)
texts: Asking questions, making observations, and gathering information are helpful in thinking about problems. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on local forms of severe weather.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data to determine if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a push or a pull.*

texts: A situation that people want to change or create can be approached as a problem to be solved through engineering. Such problems may have many acceptable solutions. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include friction as a mechanism for change in speed.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of problems requiring a solution could include having a marble or other object move a certain distance, follow a particular path, and knock down other objects. Examples of solutions could include tools such as a ramp to increase the speed of the object and a structure that would cause an object such as a marble or ball to turn.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision to ensure a successful solution, taking into account relevant scientific principles and potential impacts on people and the natural environment that may limit possible solutions.

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems
texts: The more precisely a design task’s criteria and constraints can be defined, the more likely it is that the designed solution will be successful. Specification of constraints includes consideration of scientific principles and other relevant knowledge that are likely to limit possible solutions.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.*

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.A: Defining and Delimiting an Engineering Problem (secondary)
texts: The more precisely a design task’s criteria and constraints can be defined, the more likely it is that the designed solution will be successful. Specification of constraints includes consideration of scientific principles and other relevant knowledge that is likely to limit possible solutions.(secondary to MS-PS3-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculating the total amount of thermal energy transferred.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include an insulated box, a solar cooker, and a Styrofoam cup.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-3

chunks.title: ETS1.B Developing Possible Solutions

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a simple model that mimics the function of an animal in dispersing seeds or pollinating plants.*

texts: Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people. (secondary)

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-LS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.

texts: Research on a problem should be carried out before beginning to design a solution. Testing a solution involves investigating how well it performs under a range of likely conditions.
At whatever stage, communicating with peers about proposed solutions is an important part of the design process, and shared ideas can lead to improved designs.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-5-ETS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.

texts: Tests are often designed to identify failure points or difficulties, which suggest the elements of the design that need to be improved.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-5-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple solutions to reduce the impacts of natural Earth processes on humans.*

edge_payload.raw_id: ETS1.B: Designing Solutions to Engineering Problems (secondary)
texts: Testing a solution involves investigating how well it performs under a range of likely conditions. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to earthquakes, floods, tsunamis, and volcanic eruptions.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include designing an earthquake resistant building and improving monitoring of volcanic activity.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions for developing, managing, and utilizing energy and mineral resources based on cost-benefit ratios.*

texts: When evaluating solutions, it is important to take into account a range of constraints, including cost, safety, reliability, and aesthetics, and to consider social, cultural, and environmental impacts. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the conservation, recycling, and reuse of resources (such as minerals and metals) where possible, and on minimizing impacts where it is not. Examples include developing best practices for agricultural soil use, mining (for coal, tar sands, and oil shales), and pumping (for petroleum and natural gas). Science knowledge indicates what can happen in natural systems—not what should happen.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate or refine a technological solution that reduces impacts of human activities on natural systems.*

texts: When evaluating solutions, it is important to take into account a range of constraints, including cost, safety, reliability, and aesthetics, and to consider social, cultural, and environmental impacts. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data on the impacts of human activities could include the quantities and types of pollutants released, changes to biomass and species diversity, or areal changes in land surface use (such as for urban development, agriculture and livestock, or surface mining). Examples for limiting future impacts could range from local efforts (such as reducing, reusing, and recycling resources) to large-scale geoengineering design solutions (such as altering global temperatures by making large changes to the atmosphere or ocean).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate a solution to a complex real-world problem based on prioritized criteria and trade-offs that account for a range of constraints, including cost, safety, reliability, and aesthetics as well as possible social, cultural, and environmental impacts.

texts: When evaluating solutions, it is important to take into account a range of constraints, including cost, safety, reliability, and aesthetics, and to consider social, cultural, and environmental impacts.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a computer simulation to model the impact of proposed solutions to a complex real-world problem with numerous criteria and constraints on interactions within and between systems relevant to the problem.

texts: Both physical models and computers can be used in various ways to aid in the engineering design process. Computers are useful for a variety of purposes, such as running simulations to test different ways of solving a problem or to see which one is most efficient or economical; and in making a persuasive presentation to a client about how a given design will meet his or her needs.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ETS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Design, evaluate, and refine a solution for reducing the impacts of human activities on the environment and biodiversity.*

texts: When evaluating solutions it is important to take into account a range of constraints including cost, safety, reliability and aesthetics and to consider social, cultural and environmental impacts. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human activities can include urbanization, building dams, and dissemination of invasive species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-7

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create or revise a simulation to test a solution to mitigate adverse impacts of human activity on biodiversity.*

texts: When evaluating solutions, it is important to take into account a range of constraints, including cost, safety, reliability, and aesthetics, and to consider social, cultural, and environmental impacts. (secondary)

Both physical models and computers can be used in various ways to aid in the engineering design process. Computers are useful for a variety of purposes, such as running simulations to test different ways of solving a problem or to see which one is most efficient or economical; and in making a persuasive presentation to a client about how a given design will meet his or her needs. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on designing solutions for a proposed problem related to threatened or endangered species, or to genetic variation of organisms for multiple species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.

texts: Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-2-ETS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the land, water, air, and/or other living things in the local environment.*

texts: Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human impact on the land could include cutting trees to produce paper and using resources to produce bottles. Examples of solutions could include reusing paper and recycling cans and bottles.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions using a systematic process to determine how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.

texts: There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.

texts: There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem.
Sometimes parts of different solutions can be combined to create a solution that is better than any of its predecessors.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.

texts: A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results, in order to improve it.
Models of all kinds are important for testing solutions.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.*

texts: There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of ecosystem services could include water purification, nutrient recycling, and prevention of soil erosion. Examples of design solution constraints could include scientific, economic, and social considerations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Undertake a design project to construct, test, and modify a device that either releases or absorbs thermal energy by chemical processes.*

texts: A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results, in order to improve it. (secondary to MS-PS1-6)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to the criteria of amount, time, and temperature of substance in testing the device.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the design, controlling the transfer of energy to the environment, and modification of a device using factors such as type and concentration of a substance. Examples of designs could involve chemical reactions such as dissolving ammonium chloride or calcium chloride.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.*

texts: A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results in order to improve it.
There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet criteria and constraints of a problem. (secondary to MS-PS3-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculating the total amount of thermal energy transferred.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include an insulated box, a solar cooker, and a Styrofoam cup.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-3

chunks.title: ETS1.C Optimizing the Design Solution

linked_performance_expectations.description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.*

texts: Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include different designs of dikes and windbreaks to hold back wind and water, and different designs for using shrubs, grass, and trees to hold back the land.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.

texts: Different solutions need to be tested in order to determine which of them best solves the problem, given the criteria and the constraints.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-5-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transfer information.*

texts: Different solutions need to be tested in order to determine which of them best solves the problem, given the criteria and the constraints. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include drums sending coded information through sound waves, using a grid of 1's and 0's representing black and white to send information about a picture, and using Morse code to send text.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Design a solution to a complex real-world problem by breaking it down into smaller, more manageable problems that can be solved through engineering.

texts: Criteria may need to be broken down into simpler ones that can be approached systematically, and decisions about the priority of certain criteria over others (tradeoffs) may be needed.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ETS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Refine the design of a chemical system by specifying a change in conditions that would produce increased amounts of products at equilibrium.*

texts: Criteria may need to be broken down into simpler ones that can be approached systematically, and decisions about the priority of certain criteria over others (trade-offs) may be needed. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to specifying the change in only one variable at a time. Assessment does not include calculating equilibrium constants and concentrations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the application of Le Chatelier’s Principle and on refining designs of chemical reaction systems, including descriptions of the connection between changes made at the macroscopic level and what happens at the molecular level. Examples of designs could include different ways to increase product formation including adding reactants or removing products.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific and engineering ideas to design, evaluate, and refine a device that minimizes the force on a macroscopic object during a collision.*

texts: Criteria may need to be broken down into simpler ones that can be approached systematically, and decisions about the priority of certain criteria over others (tradeoffs) may be needed. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative evaluations and/or algebraic manipulations.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evaluation and refinement could include determining the success of the device at protecting an object from damage and modifying the design to improve it. Examples of a device could include a football helmet or a parachute.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.

texts: Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-2-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.

texts: Although one design may not perform the best across all tests, identifying the characteristics of the design that performed the best in each test can provide useful information for the redesign process—that is, some of those characteristics may be incorporated into the new design.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.

texts: The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Undertake a design project to construct, test, and modify a device that either releases or absorbs thermal energy by chemical processes.*

texts: Although one design may not perform the best across all tests, identifying the characteristics of the design that performed the best in each test can provide useful information for the redesign process - that is, some of the characteristics may be incorporated into the new design. (secondary to MS-PS1-6)

The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution. (secondary to MS-PS1-6)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to the criteria of amount, time, and temperature of substance in testing the device.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the design, controlling the transfer of energy to the environment, and modification of a device using factors such as type and concentration of a substance. Examples of designs could involve chemical reactions such as dissolving ammonium chloride or calcium chloride.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-6

progression_statements.title: ETS1.C-M1

progression_statements.description: The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution.

payload.text: The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution.

progression_statements.title: ETS1.C-M2

progression_statements.description: Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs.

payload.text: Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs.

progression_statements.title: ETS1.C-P1

aliases.normalized_alias: 1.ls1.a

aliases.alias_text: All cells contain genetic information in the form of DNA molecules. Genes are regions in the DNA that contain the instructions that code for the formation of proteins. (secondary) (Note: This Disciplinary Core Idea is also addressed by HS-LS1-1.)
aliases.normalized_alias: all cells contain genetic information in the form of dna molecules. genes are regions in the dna that contain the instructions that code for the formation of proteins. (note: this disciplinary core idea is also addressed by hs-ls1-1.)

aliases.alias_text: All living things are made up of cells, which is the smallest unit that can be said to be alive. An organism may consist of one single cell (unicellular) or many different numbers and types of cells (multicellular).
aliases.normalized_alias: all living things are made up of cells, which is the smallest unit that can be said to be alive. an organism may consist of one single cell (unicellular) or many different numbers and types of cells (multicellular).

aliases.alias_text: All organisms have external parts. Different animals use their body parts in different ways to see, hear, grasp objects, protect themselves, move from place to place, and seek, find, and take in food, water and air. Plants also have different parts (roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits) that help them survive and grow.
aliases.normalized_alias: all organisms have external parts. different animals use their body parts in different ways to see, hear, grasp objects, protect themselves, move from place to place, and seek, find, and take in food, water and air. plants also have different parts (roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits) that help them survive and grow.

aliases.alias_text: Complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.
aliases.normalized_alias: complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.

aliases.alias_text: Complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the shapes, composition, and relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.
aliases.normalized_alias: complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the shapes, composition, and relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.

aliases.alias_text: Feedback mechanisms maintain a living system’s internal conditions within certain limits and mediate behaviors, allowing it to remain alive and functional even as external conditions change within some range. Feedback mechanisms can encourage (through positive feedback) or discourage (negative feedback) what is going on inside the living system.
aliases.normalized_alias: feedback mechanisms maintain a living system's internal conditions within certain limits and mediate behaviors, allowing it to remain alive and functional even as external conditions change within some range. feedback mechanisms can encourage (through positive feedback) or discourage (negative feedback) what is going on inside the living system.

aliases.alias_text: In multicellular organisms, the body is a system of multiple interacting subsystems. These subsystems are groups of cells that work together to form tissues and organs that are specialized for particular body functions.
aliases.normalized_alias: in multicellular organisms, the body is a system of multiple interacting subsystems. these subsystems are groups of cells that work together to form tissues and organs that are specialized for particular body functions.

aliases.alias_text: Investigating or designing new systems or structures requires a detailed examination of the properties of different materials, the structures of different components, and connections of components to reveal its function and/or solve a problem.
aliases.normalized_alias: investigating or designing new systems or structures requires a detailed examination of the properties of different materials, the structures of different components, and connections of components to reveal its function and/or solve a problem.

aliases.alias_text: Multicellular organisms have a hierarchical structural organization, in which any one system is made up of numerous parts and is itself a component of the next level.
aliases.normalized_alias: multicellular organisms have a hierarchical structural organization, in which any one system is made up of numerous parts and is itself a component of the next level.

aliases.alias_text: Plants and animals have both internal and external structures that serve various functions in growth, survival, behavior, and reproduction.
aliases.normalized_alias: plants and animals have both internal and external structures that serve various functions in growth, survival, behavior, and reproduction.

aliases.normalized_alias: structure and function

aliases.alias_text: Structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used. (MS-PS1-3)
aliases.normalized_alias: structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used. (ms-ps1-3)

aliases.alias_text: Structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used. (MS-PS4-2)

aliases.normalized_alias: structures can be designed to serve particular functions. (ms-ps4-3)

aliases.alias_text: Systems of specialized cells within organisms help them perform the essential functions of life.
aliases.normalized_alias: systems of specialized cells within organisms help them perform the essential functions of life.

aliases.alias_text: The functions and properties of natural and designed objects and systems can be inferred from their overall structure, the way their components are shaped and used, and the molecular substructures of its various materials.
aliases.normalized_alias: the functions and properties of natural and designed objects and systems can be inferred from their overall structure, the way their components are shaped and used, and the molecular substructures of its various materials.

records.description: LS1: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow, and meet their needs.*

texts: All organisms have external parts. Different animals use their body parts in different ways to see, hear, grasp objects, protect themselves, move from place to place, and seek, find, and take in food, water and air. Plants also have different parts (roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits) that help them survive and grow.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human problems that can be solved by mimicking plant or animal solutions could include designing clothing or equipment to protect bicyclists by mimicking turtle shells, acorn shells, and animal scales; stabilizing structures by mimicking animal tails and roots on plants; keeping out intruders by mimicking thorns on branches and animal quills; and, detecting intruders by mimicking eyes and ears.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow, and meet their needs.*

texts: The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human problems that can be solved by mimicking plant or animal solutions could include designing clothing or equipment to protect bicyclists by mimicking turtle shells, acorn shells, and animal scales; stabilizing structures by mimicking animal tails and roots on plants; keeping out intruders by mimicking thorns on branches and animal quills; and, detecting intruders by mimicking eyes and ears.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a simple model that mimics the function of an animal in dispersing seeds or pollinating plants.*

texts: The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-LS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.

texts: Plants and animals have both internal and external structures that serve various functions in growth, survival, behavior, and reproduction.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to macroscopic structures within plant and animal systems.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of structures could include thorns, stems, roots, colored petals, heart, stomach, lung, brain, and skin.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation of the properties of water and its effects on Earth materials and surface processes.

texts: The functions and properties of natural and designed objects and systems can be inferred from their overall structure, the way their components are shaped and used, and the molecular substructures of its various materials.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on mechanical and chemical investigations with water and a variety of solid materials to provide the evidence for connections between the hydrologic cycle and system interactions commonly known as the rock cycle. Examples of mechanical investigations include stream transportation and deposition using a stream table, erosion using variations in soil moisture content, or frost wedging by the expansion of water as it freezes. Examples of chemical investigations include chemical weathering and recrystallization (by testing the solubility of different materials) or melt generation (by examining how water lowers the melting temperature of most solids).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how the structure of DNA determines the structure of proteins which carry out the essential functions of life through systems of specialized cells.

texts: Systems of specialized cells within organisms help them perform the essential functions of life.

All cells contain genetic information in the form of DNA molecules. Genes are regions in the DNA that contain the instructions that code for the formation of proteins, which carry out most of the work of cells. (Note: This Disciplinary Core Idea is also addressed by HS-LS3-1.)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include identification of specific cell or tissue types, whole body systems, specific protein structures and functions, or the biochemistry of protein synthesis.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how the structure of DNA determines the structure of proteins which carry out the essential functions of life through systems of specialized cells.

texts: Investigating or designing new systems or structures requires a detailed examination of the properties of different materials, the structures of different components, and connections of components to reveal its function and/or solve a problem.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include identification of specific cell or tissue types, whole body systems, specific protein structures and functions, or the biochemistry of protein synthesis.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to illustrate the hierarchical organization of interacting systems that provide specific functions within multicellular organisms.

texts: Multicellular organisms have a hierarchical structural organization, in which any one system is made up of numerous parts and is itself a component of the next level.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include interactions and functions at the molecular or chemical reaction level.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on functions at the organism system level such as nutrient uptake, water delivery, and organism movement in response to neural stimuli. An example of an interacting system could be an artery depending on the proper function of elastic tissue and smooth muscle to regulate and deliver the proper amount of blood within the circulatory system.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that feedback mechanisms maintain homeostasis.

texts: Feedback mechanisms maintain a living system’s internal conditions within certain limits and mediate behaviors, allowing it to remain alive and functional even as external conditions change within some range. Feedback mechanisms can encourage (through positive feedback) or discourage (negative feedback) what is going on inside the living system.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the cellular processes involved in the feedback mechanism.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of investigations could include heart rate response to exercise, stomate response to moisture and temperature, and root development in response to water levels.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to clarify relationships about the role of DNA and chromosomes in coding the instructions for characteristic traits passed from parents to offspring.

texts: All cells contain genetic information in the form of DNA molecules. Genes are regions in the DNA that contain the instructions that code for the formation of proteins. (secondary) (Note: This Disciplinary Core Idea is also addressed by HS-LS1-1.)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.clarification_statement: Assessment Boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific and technical information about why the molecular-level structure is important in the functioning of designed materials.*

texts: Investigating or designing new systems or structures requires a detailed examination of the properties of different materials, the structures of different components, and connections of components to reveal its function and/or solve a problem.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to provided molecular structures of specific designed materials.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the attractive and repulsive forces that determine the functioning of the material. Examples could include why electrically conductive materials are often made of metal, flexible but durable materials are made up of long chained molecules, and pharmaceuticals are designed to interact with specific receptors.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.

texts: The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-2-ETS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that living things are made of cells; either one cell or many different numbers and types of cells.

texts: All living things are made up of cells, which is the smallest unit that can be said to be alive. An organism may consist of one single cell (unicellular) or many different numbers and types of cells (multicellular).

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on developing evidence that living things are made of cells, distinguishing between living and non-living things, and understanding that living things may be made of one cell or many and varied cells.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe the function of a cell as a whole and ways parts of cells contribute to the function.

texts: Within cells, special structures are responsible for particular functions, and the cell membrane forms the boundary that controls what enters and leaves the cell.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of organelle structure/function relationships is limited to the cell wall and cell membrane. Assessment of the function of the other organelles is limited to their relationship to the whole cell. Assessment does not include the biochemical function of cells or cell parts.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the cell functioning as a whole system and the primary role of identified parts of the cell, specifically the nucleus, chloroplasts, mitochondria, cell membrane, and cell wall.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe the function of a cell as a whole and ways parts of cells contribute to the function.

texts: Complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of organelle structure/function relationships is limited to the cell wall and cell membrane. Assessment of the function of the other organelles is limited to their relationship to the whole cell. Assessment does not include the biochemical function of cells or cell parts.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the cell functioning as a whole system and the primary role of identified parts of the cell, specifically the nucleus, chloroplasts, mitochondria, cell membrane, and cell wall.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.

texts: In multicellular organisms, the body is a system of multiple interacting subsystems. These subsystems are groups of cells that work together to form tissues and organs that are specialized for particular body functions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the mechanism of one body system independent of others. Assessment is limited to the circulatory, excretory, digestive, respiratory, muscular, and nervous systems.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the conceptual understanding that cells form tissues and tissues form organs specialized for particular body functions. Examples could include the interaction of subsystems within a system and the normal functioning of those systems.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe why structural changes to genes (mutations) located on chromosomes may affect proteins and may result in harmful, beneficial, or neutral effects to the structure and function of the organism.

texts: Complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the shapes, composition, and relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific changes at the molecular level, mechanisms for protein synthesis, or specific types of mutations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on conceptual understanding that changes in genetic material may result in making different proteins.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society.

texts: Structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used. (MS-PS1-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative information.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on natural resources that undergo a chemical process to form the synthetic material. Examples of new materials could include new medicine, new foods, and alternative fuels.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.

texts: Structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used. (MS-PS4-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative applications pertaining to light and mechanical waves.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both light and mechanical waves. Examples of models could include drawings, simulations, and written descriptions.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that digitized signals are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information than analog signals.

texts: Structures can be designed to serve particular functions. (MS-PS4-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include binary counting. Assessment does not include the specific mechanism of any given device.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a basic understanding that waves can be used for communication purposes. Examples could include using fiber optic cable to transmit light pulses, radio wave pulses in wifi devices, and conversion of stored binary patterns to make sound or text on a computer screen.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-3

aliases.alias_text: 1.LS1.B
aliases.normalized_alias: 1.ls1.b

aliases.alias_text: Adult plants and animals can have young. In many kinds of animals, parents and the offspring themselves engage in behaviors that help the offspring to survive.
aliases.normalized_alias: adult plants and animals can have young. in many kinds of animals, parents and the offspring themselves engage in behaviors that help the offspring to survive.

aliases.alias_text: Animals engage in characteristic behaviors that increase the odds of reproduction.
aliases.normalized_alias: animals engage in characteristic behaviors that increase the odds of reproduction.

aliases.alias_text: Genetic factors as well as local conditions affect the growth of the adult plant.
aliases.normalized_alias: genetic factors as well as local conditions affect the growth of the adult plant.

aliases.alias_text: Growth and Development of Organisms
aliases.normalized_alias: growth and development of organisms

aliases.alias_text: In multicellular organisms individual cells grow and then divide via a process called mitosis, thereby allowing the organism to grow. The organism begins as a single cell (fertilized egg) that divides successively to produce many cells, with each parent cell passing identical genetic material (two variants of each chromosome pair) to both daughter cells. Cellular division and differentiation produce and maintain a complex organism, composed of systems of tissues and organs that work together to meet the needs of the whole organism.
aliases.normalized_alias: in multicellular organisms individual cells grow and then divide via a process called mitosis, thereby allowing the organism to grow. the organism begins as a single cell (fertilized egg) that divides successively to produce many cells, with each parent cell passing identical genetic material (two variants of each chromosome pair) to both daughter cells. cellular division and differentiation produce and maintain a complex organism, composed of systems of tissues and organs that work together to meet the needs of the whole organism.

aliases.alias_text: LS1.B

records.description: LS1: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.description: Read texts and use media to determine patterns in behavior of parents and offspring that help offspring survive.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.B: Growth and Development of Organisms
texts: Adult plants and animals can have young. In many kinds of animals, parents and the offspring themselves engage in behaviors that help the offspring to survive.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns of behaviors could include the signals that offspring make (such as crying, cheeping, and other vocalizations) and the responses of the parents (such as feeding, comforting, and protecting the offspring).
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.B: Growth and Development of Organisms
texts: Reproduction is essential to the continued existence of every kind of organism. Plants and animals have unique and diverse life cycles.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of plant life cycles is limited to those of flowering plants. Assessment does not include details of human reproduction.
payload.clarification_statement: Changes organisms go through during their life form a pattern.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to illustrate the role of cellular division (mitosis) and differentiation in producing and maintaining complex organisms.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.B: Growth and Development of Organisms
texts: In multicellular organisms individual cells grow and then divide via a process called mitosis, thereby allowing the organism to grow. The organism begins as a single cell (fertilized egg) that divides successively to produce many cells, with each parent cell passing identical genetic material (two variants of each chromosome pair) to both daughter cells. Cellular division and differentiation produce and maintain a complex organism, composed of systems of tissues and organs that work together to meet the needs of the whole organism.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific gene control mechanisms or rote memorization of the steps of mitosis.
payload.clarification_statement: Assessment Boundary: Assessment does not include specific gene control mechanisms or rote memorization of the steps of mitosis.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use argument based on empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support an explanation for how characteristic animal behaviors and specialized plant structures affect the probability of successful reproduction of animals and plants respectively.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.B: Growth and Development of Organisms
texts: Animals engage in characteristic behaviors that increase the odds of reproduction.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of behaviors that affect the probability of animal reproduction could include nest building to protect young from cold, herding of animals to protect young from predators, and vocalization of animals and colorful plumage to attract mates for breeding. Examples of animal behaviors that affect the probability of plant reproduction could include transferring pollen or seeds, and creating conditions for seed germination and growth. Examples of plant structures could include bright flowers attracting butterflies that transfer pollen, flower nectar and odors that attract insects that transfer pollen, and hard shells on nuts that squirrels bury.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use argument based on empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support an explanation for how characteristic animal behaviors and specialized plant structures affect the probability of successful reproduction of animals and plants respectively.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.B: Growth and Development of Organisms
texts: Plants reproduce in a variety of ways, sometimes depending on animal behavior and specialized features for reproduction.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of behaviors that affect the probability of animal reproduction could include nest building to protect young from cold, herding of animals to protect young from predators, and vocalization of animals and colorful plumage to attract mates for breeding. Examples of animal behaviors that affect the probability of plant reproduction could include transferring pollen or seeds, and creating conditions for seed germination and growth. Examples of plant structures could include bright flowers attracting butterflies that transfer pollen, flower nectar and odors that attract insects that transfer pollen, and hard shells on nuts that squirrels bury.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.B: Growth and Development of Organisms
texts: Genetic factors as well as local conditions affect the growth of the adult plant.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include genetic mechanisms, gene regulation, or biochemical processes.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of local environmental conditions could include availability of food, light, space, and water. Examples of genetic factors could include large breed cattle and species of grass affecting growth of organisms. Examples of evidence could include drought decreasing plant growth, fertilizer increasing plant growth, different varieties of plant seeds growing at different rates in different conditions, and fish growing larger in large ponds than they do in small ponds.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.B: Growth and Development of Organisms
texts: Organisms reproduce, either sexually or asexually, and transfer their genetic information to their offspring. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using models such as Punnett squares, diagrams, and simulations to describe the cause and effect relationship of gene transmission from parent(s) to offspring and resulting genetic variation.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

aliases.alias_text: 5.LS1.C
aliases.normalized_alias: 5.ls1.c

aliases.alias_text: All animals need food in order to live and grow. They obtain their food from plants or from other animals. Plants need water and light to live and grow.
aliases.normalized_alias: all animals need food in order to live and grow. they obtain their food from plants or from other animals. plants need water and light to live and grow.

aliases.alias_text: As matter and energy flow through different organizational levels of living systems, chemical elements are recombined in different ways to form different products.
aliases.normalized_alias: as matter and energy flow through different organizational levels of living systems, chemical elements are recombined in different ways to form different products.

aliases.alias_text: Food provides animals with the materials they need for body repair and growth and the energy they need to maintain body warmth and for motion. (secondary)
aliases.normalized_alias: food provides animals with the materials they need for body repair and growth and the energy they need to maintain body warmth and for motion.
