aliases.alias_text: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
aliases.normalized_alias: organization for matter and energy flow in organisms

aliases.alias_text: Plants acquire their material for growth chiefly from air and water.
aliases.normalized_alias: plants acquire their material for growth chiefly from air and water.

aliases.alias_text: Plants, algae (including phytoplankton), and many microorganisms use the energy from light to make sugars (food) from carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and water through the process of photosynthesis, which also releases oxygen. These sugars can be used immediately or stored for growth or later use.
aliases.normalized_alias: plants, algae (including phytoplankton), and many microorganisms use the energy from light to make sugars (food) from carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and water through the process of photosynthesis, which also releases oxygen. these sugars can be used immediately or stored for growth or later use.

aliases.alias_text: The process of photosynthesis converts light energy to stored chemical energy by converting carbon dioxide plus water into sugars plus released oxygen.
aliases.normalized_alias: the process of photosynthesis converts light energy to stored chemical energy by converting carbon dioxide plus water into sugars plus released oxygen.

aliases.alias_text: The sugar molecules thus formed contain carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen: their hydrocarbon backbones are used to make amino acids and other carbon-based molecules that can be assembled into larger molecules (such as proteins or DNA), used for example to form new cells.
aliases.normalized_alias: the sugar molecules thus formed contain carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen: their hydrocarbon backbones are used to make amino acids and other carbon-based molecules that can be assembled into larger molecules (such as proteins or dna), used for example to form new cells.

aliases.alias_text: Within individual organisms, food moves through a series of chemical reactions in which it is broken down and rearranged to form new molecules, to support growth, or to release energy.
aliases.normalized_alias: within individual organisms, food moves through a series of chemical reactions in which it is broken down and rearranged to form new molecules, to support growth, or to release energy.

records.description: LS1: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.description: Support an argument that plants get the materials they need for growth chiefly from air and water.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
texts: Plants acquire their material for growth chiefly from air and water.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the idea that plant matter comes mostly from air and water, not from the soil.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use models to describe that energy in animals’ food (used for body repair, growth, motion, and to maintain body warmth) was once energy from the sun.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms (secondary)
texts: Food provides animals with the materials they need for body repair and growth and the energy they need to maintain body warmth and for motion. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include diagrams, and flow charts.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to illustrate how photosynthesis transforms light energy into stored chemical energy.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
texts: The process of photosynthesis converts light energy to stored chemical energy by converting carbon dioxide plus water into sugars plus released oxygen.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific biochemical steps.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on illustrating inputs and outputs of matter and the transfer and transformation of energy in photosynthesis by plants and other photosynthesizing organisms. Examples of models could include diagrams, chemical equations, and conceptual models.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct and revise an explanation based on evidence for how carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen from sugar molecules may combine with other elements to form amino acids and/or other large carbon-based molecules.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
texts: The sugar molecules thus formed contain carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen: their hydrocarbon backbones are used to make amino acids and other carbon-based molecules that can be assembled into larger molecules (such as proteins or DNA), used for example to form new cells.
As matter and energy flow through different organizational levels of living systems, chemical elements are recombined in different ways to form different products.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the details of the specific chemical reactions or identification of macromolecules.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using evidence from models and simulations to support explanations.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use a model to illustrate that cellular respiration is a chemical process whereby the bonds of food molecules and oxygen molecules are broken and the bonds in new compounds are formed resulting in a net transfer of energy.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
texts: As matter and energy flow through different organizational levels of living systems, chemical elements are recombined in different ways to form different products.

As a result of these chemical reactions, energy is transferred from one system of interacting molecules to another. Cellular respiration is a chemical process in which the bonds of food molecules and oxygen molecules are broken and new compounds are formed that can transport energy to muscles. Cellular respiration also releases the energy needed to maintain body temperature despite ongoing energy transfer to the surrounding environment.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment should not include identification of the steps or specific processes involved in cellular respiration.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the conceptual understanding of the inputs and outputs of the process of cellular respiration.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS1-7

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
texts: All animals need food in order to live and grow. They obtain their food from plants or from other animals. Plants need water and light to live and grow.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include that animals need to take in food but plants do not; the different kinds of food needed by different types of animals; the requirement of plants to have light; and, that all living things need water.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for the role of photosynthesis in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
texts: Plants, algae (including phytoplankton), and many microorganisms use the energy from light to make sugars (food) from carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and water through the process of photosynthesis, which also releases oxygen. These sugars can be used immediately or stored for growth or later use.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the biochemical mechanisms of photosynthesis.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on tracing movement of matter and flow of energy.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe how food is rearranged through chemical reactions forming new molecules that support growth and/or release energy as this matter moves through an organism.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS1.C: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
texts: Within individual organisms, food moves through a series of chemical reactions in which it is broken down and rearranged to form new molecules, to support growth, or to release energy.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include details of the chemical reactions for photosynthesis or respiration.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on describing that molecules are broken apart and put back together and that in this process, energy is released.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

aliases.alias_text: Animals have body parts that capture and convey different kinds of information needed for growth and survival. Animals respond to these inputs with behaviors that help them survive. Plants also respond to some external inputs.
aliases.normalized_alias: animals have body parts that capture and convey different kinds of information needed for growth and survival. animals respond to these inputs with behaviors that help them survive. plants also respond to some external inputs.

aliases.alias_text: Different sense receptors are specialized for particular kinds of information, which may be then processed by the animal's brain. Animals are able to use their perceptions and memories to guide their actions.
aliases.normalized_alias: different sense receptors are specialized for particular kinds of information, which may be then processed by the animal's brain. animals are able to use their perceptions and memories to guide their actions.

aliases.alias_text: Each sense receptor responds to different inputs (electromagnetic, mechanical, chemical), transmitting them as signals that travel along nerve cells to the brain. The signals are then processed in the brain, resulting in immediate behaviors or memories.
aliases.normalized_alias: each sense receptor responds to different inputs (electromagnetic, mechanical, chemical), transmitting them as signals that travel along nerve cells to the brain. the signals are then processed in the brain, resulting in immediate behaviors or memories.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human problems that can be solved by mimicking plant or animal solutions could include designing clothing or equipment to protect bicyclists by mimicking turtle shells, acorn shells, and animal scales; stabilizing structures by mimicking animal tails and roots on plants; keeping out intruders by mimicking thorns on branches and animal quills; and, detecting intruders by mimicking eyes and ears.

aliases.alias_text: 5.LS2.A
aliases.normalized_alias: 5.ls2.a

aliases.alias_text: Ecosystems have carrying capacities, which are limits to the numbers of organisms and populations they can support. These limits result from such factors as the availability of living and nonliving resources and from such challenges such as predation, competition, and disease. Organisms would have the capacity to produce populations of great size were it not for the fact that environments and resources are finite. This fundamental tension affects the abundance (number of individuals) of species in any given ecosystem.
aliases.normalized_alias: ecosystems have carrying capacities, which are limits to the numbers of organisms and populations they can support. these limits result from such factors as the availability of living and nonliving resources and from such challenges such as predation, competition, and disease. organisms would have the capacity to produce populations of great size were it not for the fact that environments and resources are finite. this fundamental tension affects the abundance (number of individuals) of species in any given ecosystem.

aliases.alias_text: Growth of organisms and population increases are limited by access to resources.
aliases.normalized_alias: growth of organisms and population increases are limited by access to resources.

aliases.alias_text: HS.LS2.A
aliases.normalized_alias: hs.ls2.a

aliases.alias_text: In any ecosystem, organisms and populations with similar requirements for food, water, oxygen, or other resources may compete with each other for limited resources, access to which consequently constrains their growth and reproduction.
aliases.normalized_alias: in any ecosystem, organisms and populations with similar requirements for food, water, oxygen, or other resources may compete with each other for limited resources, access to which consequently constrains their growth and reproduction.

aliases.alias_text: MS.LS2.A
aliases.normalized_alias: ms.ls2.a

aliases.alias_text: Organisms, and populations of organisms, are dependent on their environmental interactions both with other living things and with nonliving factors.
aliases.normalized_alias: organisms, and populations of organisms, are dependent on their environmental interactions both with other living things and with nonliving factors.

aliases.alias_text: Plants depend on animals for pollination or to move their seeds around.
aliases.normalized_alias: plants depend on animals for pollination or to move their seeds around.

aliases.alias_text: Plants depend on water and light to grow.
aliases.normalized_alias: plants depend on water and light to grow.

aliases.alias_text: Similarly, predatory interactions may reduce the number of organisms or eliminate whole populations of organisms. Mutually beneficial interactions, in contrast, may become so interdependent that each organism requires the other for survival. Although the species involved in these competitive, predatory, and mutually beneficial interactions vary across ecosystems, the patterns of interactions of organisms with their environments, both living and nonliving, are shared.
aliases.normalized_alias: similarly, predatory interactions may reduce the number of organisms or eliminate whole populations of organisms. mutually beneficial interactions, in contrast, may become so interdependent that each organism requires the other for survival. although the species involved in these competitive, predatory, and mutually beneficial interactions vary across ecosystems, the patterns of interactions of organisms with their environments, both living and nonliving, are shared.

aliases.alias_text: The food of almost any kind of animal can be traced back to plants. Organisms are related in food webs in which some animals eat plants for food and other animals eat the animals that eat plants. Some organisms, such as fungi and bacteria, break down dead organisms (both plants or plants parts and animals) and therefore operate as "decomposers." Decomposition eventually restores (recycles) some materials back to the soil. Organisms can survive only in environments in which their particular needs are met. A healthy ecosystem is one in which multiple species of different types are each able to meet their needs in a relatively stable web of life. Newly introduced species can damage the balance of an ecosystem.
aliases.normalized_alias: the food of almost any kind of animal can be traced back to plants. organisms are related in food webs in which some animals eat plants for food and other animals eat the animals that eat plants. some organisms, such as fungi and bacteria, break down dead organisms (both plants or plants parts and animals) and therefore operate as "decomposers." decomposition eventually restores (recycles) some materials back to the soil. organisms can survive only in environments in which their particular needs are met. a healthy ecosystem is one in which multiple species of different types are each able to meet their needs in a relatively stable web of life. newly introduced species can damage the balance of an ecosystem.

aliases.alias_text: Cycles of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems
aliases.normalized_alias: cycles of matter and energy transfer in ecosystems

aliases.alias_text: Food webs are models that demonstrate how matter and energy is transferred between producers, consumers, and decomposers as the three groups interact within an ecosystem. Transfers of matter into and out of the physical environment occur at every level. Decomposers recycle nutrients from dead plant or animal matter back to the soil in terrestrial environments or to the water in aquatic environments. The atoms that make up the organisms in an ecosystem are cycled repeatedly between the living and nonliving parts of the ecosystem.
aliases.normalized_alias: food webs are models that demonstrate how matter and energy is transferred between producers, consumers, and decomposers as the three groups interact within an ecosystem. transfers of matter into and out of the physical environment occur at every level. decomposers recycle nutrients from dead plant or animal matter back to the soil in terrestrial environments or to the water in aquatic environments. the atoms that make up the organisms in an ecosystem are cycled repeatedly between the living and nonliving parts of the ecosystem.

aliases.alias_text: LS2.B: Cycle of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems
aliases.normalized_alias: ls2.b: cycle of matter and energy transfer in ecosystems

aliases.alias_text: LS2.B: Cycles of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems
aliases.normalized_alias: ls2.b: cycles of matter and energy transfer in ecosystems

aliases.alias_text: Matter cycles between the air and soil and among plants, animals, and microbes as these organisms live and die. Organisms obtain gases, and water, from the environment, and release waste matter (gas, liquid, or solid) back into the environment.
aliases.normalized_alias: matter cycles between the air and soil and among plants, animals, and microbes as these organisms live and die. organisms obtain gases, and water, from the environment, and release waste matter (gas, liquid, or solid) back into the environment.

aliases.alias_text: MS.LS2.B
aliases.normalized_alias: ms.ls2.b

aliases.alias_text: Photosynthesis and cellular respiration (including anaerobic processes) provide most of the energy for life processes.
aliases.normalized_alias: photosynthesis and cellular respiration (including anaerobic processes) provide most of the energy for life processes.

aliases.alias_text: Photosynthesis and cellular respiration are important components of the carbon cycle, in which carbon is exchanged among the biosphere, atmosphere, oceans, and geosphere through chemical, physical, geological, and biological processes.
aliases.normalized_alias: photosynthesis and cellular respiration are important components of the carbon cycle, in which carbon is exchanged among the biosphere, atmosphere, oceans, and geosphere through chemical, physical, geological, and biological processes.

aliases.alias_text: Plants or algae form the lowest level of the food web. At each link upward in a food web, only a small fraction of the matter consumed at the lower level is transferred upward, to produce growth and release energy in cellular respiration at the higher level. Given this inefficiency, there are generally fewer organisms at higher levels of a food web. Some matter reacts to release energy for life functions, some matter is stored in newly made structures, and much is discarded. The chemical elements that make up the molecules of organisms pass through food webs and into and out of the atmosphere and soil, and they are combined and recombined in different ways. At each link in an ecosystem, matter and energy are conserved.
aliases.normalized_alias: plants or algae form the lowest level of the food web. at each link upward in a food web, only a small fraction of the matter consumed at the lower level is transferred upward, to produce growth and release energy in cellular respiration at the higher level. given this inefficiency, there are generally fewer organisms at higher levels of a food web. some matter reacts to release energy for life functions, some matter is stored in newly made structures, and much is discarded. the chemical elements that make up the molecules of organisms pass through food webs and into and out of the atmosphere and soil, and they are combined and recombined in different ways. at each link in an ecosystem, matter and energy are conserved.

Some matter reacts to release energy for life functions, some matter is stored in newly made structures, and much is discarded. The chemical elements that make up the molecules of organisms pass through food webs and into and out of the atmosphere and soil, and they are combined and recombined in different ways. At each link in an ecosystem, matter and energy are conserved.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to proportional reasoning to describe the cycling of matter and flow of energy.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using a mathematical model of stored energy in biomass to describe the transfer of energy from one trophic level to another and that matter and energy are conserved as matter cycles and energy flows through ecosystems. Emphasis is on atoms and molecules such as carbon, oxygen, hydrogen and nitrogen being conserved as they move through an ecosystem.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to illustrate the role of photosynthesis and cellular respiration in the cycling of carbon among the biosphere, atmosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS2.B: Cycles of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems
texts: Photosynthesis and cellular respiration are important components of the carbon cycle, in which carbon is exchanged among the biosphere, atmosphere, oceans, and geosphere through chemical, physical, geological, and biological processes.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the specific chemical steps of photosynthesis and respiration.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include simulations and mathematical models.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of matter and flow of energy among living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS2.B: Cycle of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems
texts: Food webs are models that demonstrate how matter and energy is transferred between producers, consumers, and decomposers as the three groups interact within an ecosystem. Transfers of matter into and out of the physical environment occur at every level. Decomposers recycle nutrients from dead plant or animal matter back to the soil in terrestrial environments or to the water in aquatic environments. The atoms that make up the organisms in an ecosystem are cycled repeatedly between the living and nonliving parts of the ecosystem.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the use of chemical reactions to describe the processes.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on describing the conservation of matter and flow of energy into and out of various ecosystems, and on defining the boundaries of the system.

aliases.alias_text: 3.LS2.C
aliases.normalized_alias: 3.ls2.c

aliases.alias_text: A complex set of interactions within an ecosystem can keep its numbers and types of organisms relatively constant over long periods of time under stable conditions. If a modest biological or physical disturbance to an ecosystem occurs, it may return to its more or less original status (i.e., the ecosystem is resilient), as opposed to becoming a very different ecosystem. Extreme fluctuations in conditions or the size of any population, however, can challenge the functioning of ecosystems in terms of resources and habitat availability.

aliases.alias_text: Biodiversity describes the variety of species found in Earth’s terrestrial and oceanic ecosystems. The completeness or integrity of an ecosystem’s biodiversity is often used as a measure of its health.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to a single environmental change. Assessment does not include the greenhouse effect or climate change.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of environmental changes could include changes in land characteristics, water distribution, temperature, food, and other organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support and revise explanations based on evidence about factors affecting biodiversity and populations in ecosystems of different scales.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS2.C: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience
texts: A complex set of interactions within an ecosystem can keep its numbers and types of organisms relatively constant over long periods of time under stable conditions. If a modest biological or physical disturbance to an ecosystem occurs, it may return to its more or less original status (i.e., the ecosystem is resilient), as opposed to becoming a very different ecosystem. Extreme fluctuations in conditions or the size of any population, however, can challenge the functioning of ecosystems in terms of resources and habitat availability.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to provided data.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of mathematical representations include finding the average, determining trends, and using graphical comparisons of multiple sets of data.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the claims, evidence, and reasoning that the complex interactions in ecosystems maintain relatively consistent numbers and types of organisms in stable conditions, but changing conditions may result in a new ecosystem.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS2.C: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience
texts: A complex set of interactions within an ecosystem can keep its numbers and types of organisms relatively constant over long periods of time under stable conditions. If a modest biological or physical disturbance to an ecosystem occurs, it may return to its more or less original status (i.e., the ecosystem is resilient), as opposed to becoming a very different ecosystem. Extreme fluctuations in conditions or the size of any population, however, can challenge the functioning of ecosystems in terms of resources and habitat availability.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of changes in ecosystem conditions could include modest biological or physical changes, such as moderate hunting or a seasonal flood; and extreme changes, such as volcanic eruption or sea level rise.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Design, evaluate, and refine a solution for reducing the impacts of human activities on the environment and biodiversity.*

edge_payload.raw_id: LS2.C: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience
texts: Moreover, anthropogenic changes (induced by human activity) in the environment — including habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, overexploitation, and climate change — can disrupt an ecosystem and threaten the survival of some species.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human activities can include urbanization, building dams, and dissemination of invasive species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-7

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument supported by empirical evidence that changes to physical or biological components of an ecosystem affect populations.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS2.C: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience
texts: Ecosystems are dynamic in nature; their characteristics can vary over time. Disruptions to any physical or biological component of an ecosystem can lead to shifts in all its populations.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on recognizing patterns in data and making warranted inferences about changes in populations, and on evaluating empirical evidence supporting arguments about changes to ecosystems.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.*

edge_payload.raw_id: LS2.C: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience
texts: Biodiversity describes the variety of species found in Earth’s terrestrial and oceanic ecosystems. The completeness or integrity of an ecosystem’s biodiversity is often used as a measure of its health.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of ecosystem services could include water purification, nutrient recycling, and prevention of soil erosion. Examples of design solution constraints could include scientific, economic, and social considerations.

aliases.alias_text: Each chromosome consists of a single very long DNA molecule, and each gene on the chromosome is a particular segment of that DNA. The instructions for forming species’ characteristics are carried in DNA. All cells in an organism have the same genetic content, but the genes used (expressed) by the cell may be regulated in different ways. Not all DNA codes for a protein; some segments of DNA are involved in regulatory or structural functions, and some have no as-yet known function.
aliases.normalized_alias: each chromosome consists of a single very long dna molecule, and each gene on the chromosome is a particular segment of that dna. the instructions for forming species' characteristics are carried in dna. all cells in an organism have the same genetic content, but the genes used (expressed) by the cell may be regulated in different ways. not all dna codes for a protein; some segments of dna are involved in regulatory or structural functions, and some have no as-yet known function.

aliases.alias_text: Genes are located in the chromosomes of cells, with each chromosome pair containing two variants of each of many distinct genes. Each distinct gene chiefly controls the production of specific proteins, which in turn affects the traits of the individual. Changes (mutations) to genes can result in changes to proteins, which can affect the structures and functions of the organism and thereby change traits.
aliases.normalized_alias: genes are located in the chromosomes of cells, with each chromosome pair containing two variants of each of many distinct genes. each distinct gene chiefly controls the production of specific proteins, which in turn affects the traits of the individual. changes (mutations) to genes can result in changes to proteins, which can affect the structures and functions of the organism and thereby change traits.

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment.

texts: Other characteristics result from individuals' interactions with the environment, which can range from diet to learning. Many characteristics involve both inheritance and environment.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of the environment affecting a trait could include normally tall plants grown with insufficient water are stunted; and, a pet dog that is given too much food and little exercise may become overweight.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to clarify relationships about the role of DNA and chromosomes in coding the instructions for characteristic traits passed from parents to offspring.

texts: Each chromosome consists of a single very long DNA molecule, and each gene on the chromosome is a particular segment of that DNA. The instructions for forming species’ characteristics are carried in DNA. All cells in an organism have the same genetic content, but the genes used (expressed) by the cell may be regulated in different ways. Not all DNA codes for a protein; some segments of DNA are involved in regulatory or structural functions, and some have no as-yet known function.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.clarification_statement: Assessment Boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe why structural changes to genes (mutations) located on chromosomes may affect proteins and may result in harmful, beneficial, or neutral effects to the structure and function of the organism.

texts: Genes are located in the chromosomes of cells, with each chromosome pair containing two variants of each of many distinct genes. Each distinct gene chiefly controls the production of specific proteins, which in turn affects the traits of the individual. Changes (mutations) to genes can result in changes to proteins, which can affect the structures and functions of the organism and thereby change traits.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific changes at the molecular level, mechanisms for protein synthesis, or specific types of mutations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on conceptual understanding that changes in genetic material may result in making different proteins.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.

texts: Variations of inherited traits between parent and offspring arise from genetic differences that result from the subset of chromosomes (and therefore genes) inherited.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using models such as Punnett squares, diagrams, and simulations to describe the cause and effect relationship of gene transmission from parent(s) to offspring and resulting genetic variation.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS3-2

aliases.normalized_alias: 3.ls3.b

aliases.alias_text: Different organisms vary in how they look and function because they have different inherited information.
aliases.normalized_alias: different organisms vary in how they look and function because they have different inherited information.

aliases.alias_text: Environmental factors also affect expression of traits, and hence affect the probability of occurrences of traits in a population. Thus, the variation and distribution of traits observed depends on both genetic and environmental factors.
aliases.normalized_alias: environmental factors also affect expression of traits, and hence affect the probability of occurrences of traits in a population. thus, the variation and distribution of traits observed depends on both genetic and environmental factors.

aliases.alias_text: In addition to variations that arise from sexual reproduction, genetic information can be altered because of mutations. Though rare, mutations may result in changes to the structure and function of proteins. Some changes are beneficial, others harmful, and some neutral to the organism.
aliases.normalized_alias: in addition to variations that arise from sexual reproduction, genetic information can be altered because of mutations. though rare, mutations may result in changes to the structure and function of proteins. some changes are beneficial, others harmful, and some neutral to the organism.

aliases.alias_text: In sexual reproduction, chromosomes can sometimes swap sections during the process of meiosis (cell division), thereby creating new genetic combinations and thus more genetic variation. Although DNA replication is tightly regulated and remarkably accurate, errors do occur and result in mutations, which are also a source of genetic variation. Environmental factors can also cause mutations in genes, and viable mutations are inherited.
aliases.normalized_alias: in sexual reproduction, chromosomes can sometimes swap sections during the process of meiosis (cell division), thereby creating new genetic combinations and thus more genetic variation. although dna replication is tightly regulated and remarkably accurate, errors do occur and result in mutations, which are also a source of genetic variation. environmental factors can also cause mutations in genes, and viable mutations are inherited.

aliases.alias_text: In sexually reproducing organisms, each parent contributes half of the genes acquired (at random) by the offspring. Individuals have two of each chromosome and hence two alleles of each gene, one acquired from each parent. These versions may be identical or may differ from each other.
aliases.normalized_alias: in sexually reproducing organisms, each parent contributes half of the genes acquired (at random) by the offspring. individuals have two of each chromosome and hence two alleles of each gene, one acquired from each parent. these versions may be identical or may differ from each other.

records.description: LS3: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that young plants and animals are like, but not exactly like, their parents.

texts: Individuals of the same kind of plant or animal are recognizable as similar but can also vary in many ways.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include inheritance or animals that undergo metamorphosis or hybrids.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include features plants or animals share. Examples of observations could include leaves from the same kind of plant are the same shape but can differ in size; and, a particular breed of dog looks like its parents but is not exactly the same.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from parents and that variation of these traits exists in a group of similar organisms.

texts: Different organisms vary in how they look and function because they have different inherited information.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include genetic mechanisms of inheritance and prediction of traits. Assessment is limited to non-human examples.
payload.clarification_statement: Patterns are the similarities and differences in traits shared between offspring and their parents, or among siblings. Emphasis is on organisms other than humans.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment.

texts: The environment also affects the traits that an organism develops.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of the environment affecting a trait could include normally tall plants grown with insufficient water are stunted; and, a pet dog that is given too much food and little exercise may become overweight.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make and defend a claim based on evidence that inheritable genetic variations may result from: (1) new genetic combinations through meiosis, (2) viable errors occurring during replication, and/or (3) mutations caused by environmental factors.

texts: In sexual reproduction, chromosomes can sometimes swap sections during the process of meiosis (cell division), thereby creating new genetic combinations and thus more genetic variation. Although DNA replication is tightly regulated and remarkably accurate, errors do occur and result in mutations, which are also a source of genetic variation. Environmental factors can also cause mutations in genes, and viable mutations are inherited.

Environmental factors also affect expression of traits, and hence affect the probability of occurrences of traits in a population. Thus the variation and distribution of traits observed depends on both genetic and environmental factors.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using data to support arguments for the way variation occurs.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to explain the variation and distribution of expressed traits in a population.

texts: Environmental factors also affect expression of traits, and hence affect the probability of occurrences of traits in a population. Thus, the variation and distribution of traits observed depends on both genetic and environmental factors.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy-Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the use of mathematics to describe the probability of traits as it relates to genetic and environmental factors in the expression of traits.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe why structural changes to genes (mutations) located on chromosomes may affect proteins and may result in harmful, beneficial, or neutral effects to the structure and function of the organism.

texts: In addition to variations that arise from sexual reproduction, genetic information can be altered because of mutations. Though rare, mutations may result in changes to the structure and function of proteins. Some changes are beneficial, others harmful, and some neutral to the organism.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include specific changes at the molecular level, mechanisms for protein synthesis, or specific types of mutations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on conceptual understanding that changes in genetic material may result in making different proteins.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.

texts: In sexually reproducing organisms, each parent contributes half of the genes acquired (at random) by the offspring. Individuals have two of each chromosome and hence two alleles of each gene, one acquired from each parent. These versions may be identical or may differ from each other.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using models such as Punnett squares, diagrams, and simulations to describe the cause and effect relationship of gene transmission from parent(s) to offspring and resulting genetic variation.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

aliases.alias_text: Anatomical similarities and differences between various organisms living today and between them and organisms in the fossil record, enable the reconstruction of evolutionary history and the inference of lines of evolutionary descent.
aliases.normalized_alias: anatomical similarities and differences between various organisms living today and between them and organisms in the fossil record, enable the reconstruction of evolutionary history and the inference of lines of evolutionary descent.

aliases.alias_text: Comparison of the embryological development of different species also reveals similarities that show relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.

aliases.alias_text: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity
aliases.normalized_alias: evidence of common ancestry and diversity

aliases.alias_text: Genetic information, like the fossil record, provides evidence of evolution. DNA sequences vary among species, but there are many overlaps; in fact, the ongoing branching that produces multiple lines of descent can be inferred by comparing the DNA sequences of different organisms. Such information is also derivable from the similarities and differences in amino acid sequences and from anatomical and embryological evidence.

records.description: LS4: Biological Evolution: Unity and Diversity

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of the organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS4.A: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity
texts: Some kinds of plants and animals that once lived on Earth are no longer found anywhere. (Note: moved from K-2)
Fossils provide evidence about the types of organisms that lived long ago and also about the nature of their environments.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include identification of specific fossils or present plants and animals. Assessment is limited to major fossil types and relative ages.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include type, size, and distributions of fossil organisms. Examples of fossils and environments could include marine fossils found on dry land, tropical plant fossils found in Arctic areas, and fossils of extinct organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific information that common ancestry and biological evolution are supported by multiple lines of empirical evidence.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS4.A: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity
texts: Genetic information, like the fossil record, provides evidence of evolution. DNA sequences vary among species, but there are many overlaps; in fact, the ongoing branching that produces multiple lines of descent can be inferred by comparing the DNA sequences of different organisms. Such information is also derivable from the similarities and differences in amino acid sequences and from anatomical and embryological evidence.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a conceptual understanding of the role each line of evidence has relating to common ancestry and biological evolution. Examples of evidence could include similarities in DNA sequences, anatomical structures, and order of appearance of structures in embryological development.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS4.A: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity
texts: The collection of fossils and their placement in chronological order (e.g., through the location of the sedimentary layers in which they are found or through radioactive dating) is known as the fossil record. It documents the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of many life forms throughout the history of life on Earth.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the names of individual species or geological eras in the fossil record.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on finding patterns of changes in the level of complexity of anatomical structures in organisms and the chronological order of fossil appearance in the rock layers.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for the anatomical similarities and differences among modern organisms and between modern and fossil organisms to infer evolutionary relationships.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS4.A: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity
texts: Anatomical similarities and differences between various organisms living today and between them and organisms in the fossil record, enable the reconstruction of evolutionary history and the inference of lines of evolutionary descent.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on explanations of the evolutionary relationships among organisms in terms of similarity or differences of the gross appearance of anatomical structures.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze displays of pictorial data to compare patterns of similarities in the embryological development across multiple species to identify relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.

edge_payload.raw_id: LS4.A: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity
texts: Comparison of the embryological development of different species also reveals similarities that show relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of comparisons is limited to gross appearance of anatomical structures in embryological development.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on inferring general patterns of relatedness among embryos of different organisms by comparing the macroscopic appearance of diagrams or pictures.

chunks.title: LS4.B Natural Selection

records.description: LS4: Biological Evolution: Unity and Diversity

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use evidence to construct an explanation for how the variations in characteristics among individuals of the same species may provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing.

texts: Sometimes the differences in characteristics between individuals of the same species provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of cause and effect relationships could be plants that have larger thorns than other plants may be less likely to be eaten by predators; and, animals that have better camouflage coloration than other animals may be more likely to survive and therefore more likely to leave offspring.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that the process of evolution primarily results from four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the heritable genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for limited resources, and (4) the proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in the environment.

texts: Natural selection occurs only if there is both (1) variation in the genetic information between organisms in a population and (2) variation in the expression of that genetic information — that is, trait variation — that leads to differences in performance among individuals.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include other mechanisms of evolution, such as genetic drift, gene flow through migration, and co-evolution.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using evidence to explain the influence each of the four factors has on the number of organisms, behaviors, morphology, or physiology in terms of ability to compete for limited resources and subsequent survival of individuals and adaptation of species. Examples of evidence could include mathematical models such as simple distribution graphs and proportional reasoning.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to support explanations that organisms with an advantageous heritable trait tend to increase in proportion to organisms lacking this trait.

texts: Natural selection occurs only if there is both (1) variation in the genetic information between organisms in a population and (2) variation in the expression of that genetic information — that is, trait variation — that leads to differences in performance among individuals.
The traits that positively affect survival are more likely to be reproduced, and thus are more common in the population.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to basic statistical and graphical analysis. Assessment does not include allele frequency calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on analyzing shifts in numerical distribution of traits and using these shifts as evidence to support explanations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that describes how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some individuals’ probability of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.

texts: Natural selection leads to the predominance of certain traits in a population, and the suppression of others.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using simple probability statements and proportional reasoning to construct explanations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and synthesize information about the technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.

texts: In artificial selection, humans have the capacity to influence certain characteristics of organisms by selective breeding. One can choose desired parental traits determined by genes, which are then passed on to offspring.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on synthesizing information from reliable sources about the influence of humans on genetic outcomes in artificial selection (such as genetic modification, animal husbandry, gene therapy); and, on the impacts these technologies have on society as well as the technologies leading to these scientific discoveries.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-5

aliases.alias_text: 3.LS4.C
aliases.normalized_alias: 3.ls4.c

aliases.alias_text: Adaptation
aliases.normalized_alias: adaptation

aliases.alias_text: Adaptation by natural selection acting over generations is one important process by which species change over time in response to changes in environmental conditions. Traits that support successful survival and reproduction in the new environment become more common; those that do not become less common. Thus, the distribution of traits in a population changes.
aliases.normalized_alias: adaptation by natural selection acting over generations is one important process by which species change over time in response to changes in environmental conditions. traits that support successful survival and reproduction in the new environment become more common; those that do not become less common. thus, the distribution of traits in a population changes.

aliases.alias_text: Changes in the physical environment, whether naturally occurring or human induced, have thus contributed to the expansion of some species, the emergence of new distinct species as populations diverge under different conditions, and the decline — and sometimes the extinction — of some species.
aliases.normalized_alias: changes in the physical environment, whether naturally occurring or human induced, have thus contributed to the expansion of some species, the emergence of new distinct species as populations diverge under different conditions, and the decline - and sometimes the extinction - of some species.

aliases.alias_text: Evolution is a consequence of the interaction of four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for an environment’s limited supply of the resources that individuals need in order to survive and reproduce, and (4) the ensuing proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in that environment.
aliases.normalized_alias: evolution is a consequence of the interaction of four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for an environment's limited supply of the resources that individuals need in order to survive and reproduce, and (4) the ensuing proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in that environment.

aliases.alias_text: For any particular environment, some kinds of organisms survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.
aliases.normalized_alias: for any particular environment, some kinds of organisms survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.

aliases.alias_text: HS.LS4.C
aliases.normalized_alias: hs.ls4.c

aliases.alias_text: MS.LS4.C
aliases.normalized_alias: ms.ls4.c

aliases.alias_text: Natural selection leads to adaptation, that is, to a population dominated by organisms that are anatomically, behaviorally, and physiologically well suited to survive and reproduce in a specific environment. That is, the differential survival and reproduction of organisms in a population that have an advantageous heritable trait leads to an increase in the proportion of individuals in future generations that have the trait and to a decrease in the proportion of individuals that do not.
aliases.normalized_alias: natural selection leads to adaptation, that is, to a population dominated by organisms that are anatomically, behaviorally, and physiologically well suited to survive and reproduce in a specific environment. that is, the differential survival and reproduction of organisms in a population that have an advantageous heritable trait leads to an increase in the proportion of individuals in future generations that have the trait and to a decrease in the proportion of individuals that do not.

records.description: LS4: Biological Evolution: Unity and Diversity

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an argument with evidence that in a particular habitat some organisms can survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.

texts: For any particular environment, some kinds of organisms survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence could include needs and characteristics of the organisms and habitats involved. The organisms and their habitat make up a system in which the parts depend on each other.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that the process of evolution primarily results from four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the heritable genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for limited resources, and (4) the proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in the environment.

texts: Evolution is a consequence of the interaction of four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for an environment’s limited supply of the resources that individuals need in order to survive and reproduce, and (4) the ensuing proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in that environment.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include other mechanisms of evolution, such as genetic drift, gene flow through migration, and co-evolution.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using evidence to explain the influence each of the four factors has on the number of organisms, behaviors, morphology, or physiology in terms of ability to compete for limited resources and subsequent survival of individuals and adaptation of species. Examples of evidence could include mathematical models such as simple distribution graphs and proportional reasoning.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to support explanations that organisms with an advantageous heritable trait tend to increase in proportion to organisms lacking this trait.

texts: Natural selection leads to adaptation, that is, to a population dominated by organisms that are anatomically, behaviorally, and physiologically well suited to survive and reproduce in a specific environment. That is, the differential survival and reproduction of organisms in a population that have an advantageous heritable trait leads to an increase in the proportion of individuals in future generations that have the trait and to a decrease in the proportion of individuals that do not.
Adaptation also means that the distribution of traits in a population can change when conditions change.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to basic statistical and graphical analysis. Assessment does not include allele frequency calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on analyzing shifts in numerical distribution of traits and using these shifts as evidence to support explanations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how natural selection leads to adaptation of populations.

texts: Natural selection leads to adaptation, that is, to a population dominated by organisms that are anatomically, behaviorally, and physiologically well suited to survive and reproduce in a specific environment. That is, the differential survival and reproduction of organisms in a population that have an advantageous heritable trait leads to an increase in the proportion of individuals in future generations that have the trait and to a decrease in the proportion of individuals that do not.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using data to provide evidence for how specific biotic and abiotic differences in ecosystems (such as ranges of seasonal temperature, long-term climate change, acidity, light, geographic barriers, or evolution of other organisms) contribute to a change in gene frequency over time, leading to adaptation of populations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the evidence supporting claims that changes in environmental conditions may result in: (1) increases in the number of individuals of some species, (2) the emergence of new species over time, and (3) the extinction of other species.

texts: Changes in the physical environment, whether naturally occurring or human induced, have thus contributed to the expansion of some species, the emergence of new distinct species as populations diverge under different conditions, and the decline — and sometimes the extinction — of some species.

Species become extinct because they can no longer survive and reproduce in their altered environment. If members cannot adjust to change that is too fast or drastic, the opportunity for the species’ evolution is lost.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on determining cause and effect relationships for how changes to the environment such as deforestation, fishing, application of fertilizers, drought, flood, and the rate of change of the environment affect distribution or disappearance of traits in species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create or revise a simulation to test a solution to mitigate adverse impacts of human activity on biodiversity.*

texts: Changes in the physical environment, whether naturally occurring or human induced, have thus contributed to the expansion of some species, the emergence of new distinct species as populations diverge under different conditions, and the decline — and sometimes the extinction — of some species.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on designing solutions for a proposed problem related to threatened or endangered species, or to genetic variation of organisms for multiple species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support explanations of how natural selection may lead to increases and decreases of specific traits in populations over time.

texts: Adaptation by natural selection acting over generations is one important process by which species change over time in response to changes in environmental conditions. Traits that support successful survival and reproduction in the new environment become more common; those that do not become less common. Thus, the distribution of traits in a population changes.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on using mathematical models, probability statements, and proportional reasoning to support explanations of trends in changes to populations over time.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-6

aliases.alias_text: Biodiversity and Humans
aliases.normalized_alias: biodiversity and humans

aliases.alias_text: Biodiversity is increased by the formation of new species (speciation) and decreased by the loss of species (extinction). (secondary)
aliases.normalized_alias: biodiversity is increased by the formation of new species (speciation) and decreased by the loss of species (extinction).

aliases.alias_text: Changes in biodiversity can influence humans’ resources, such as food, energy, and medicines, as well as ecosystem services that humans rely on—for example, water purification and recycling.(secondary)
aliases.normalized_alias: changes in biodiversity can influence humans' resources, such as food, energy, and medicines, as well as ecosystem services that humans rely on-for example, water purification and recycling.

aliases.alias_text: HS.LS4.D
aliases.normalized_alias: hs.ls4.d

aliases.alias_text: Humans depend on the living world for the resources and other benefits provided by biodiversity. But human activity is also having adverse impacts on biodiversity through overpopulation, overexploitation, habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and climate change. Thus sustaining biodiversity so that ecosystem functioning and productivity are maintained is essential to supporting and enhancing life on Earth. Sustaining biodiversity also aids humanity by preserving landscapes of recreational or inspirational value. (Note: This Disciplinary Core Idea is also addressed by HS-LS2-7.)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the diversity of living things in each of a variety of different habitats.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem caused when the environment changes and the types of plants and animals that live there may change.*

texts: Populations live in a variety of habitats, and change in those habitats affects the organisms living there.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to a single environmental change. Assessment does not include the greenhouse effect or climate change.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of environmental changes could include changes in land characteristics, water distribution, temperature, food, and other organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Design, evaluate, and refine a solution for reducing the impacts of human activities on the environment and biodiversity.*

texts: Biodiversity is increased by the formation of new species (speciation) and decreased by the loss of species (extinction). (secondary)

Humans depend on the living world for the resources and other benefits provided by biodiversity. But human activity is also having adverse impacts on biodiversity through overpopulation, overexploitation, habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and climate change. Thus sustaining biodiversity so that ecosystem functioning and productivity are maintained is essential to supporting and enhancing life on Earth. Sustaining biodiversity also aids humanity by preserving landscapes of recreational or inspirational value. (secondary) (Note: This Disciplinary Core Idea is also addressed by HS-LS4-6.)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human activities can include urbanization, building dams, and dissemination of invasive species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-7

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create or revise a simulation to test a solution to mitigate adverse impacts of human activity on biodiversity.*

texts: Humans depend on the living world for the resources and other benefits provided by biodiversity. But human activity is also having adverse impacts on biodiversity through overpopulation, overexploitation, habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and climate change. Thus sustaining biodiversity so that ecosystem functioning and productivity are maintained is essential to supporting and enhancing life on Earth. Sustaining biodiversity also aids humanity by preserving landscapes of recreational or inspirational value. (Note: This Disciplinary Core Idea is also addressed by HS-LS2-7.)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on designing solutions for a proposed problem related to threatened or endangered species, or to genetic variation of organisms for multiple species.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.*

texts: Changes in biodiversity can influence humans’ resources, such as food, energy, and medicines, as well as ecosystem services that humans rely on—for example, water purification and recycling.(secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of ecosystem services could include water purification, nutrient recycling, and prevention of soil erosion. Examples of design solution constraints could include scientific, economic, and social considerations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-5

aliases.alias_text: A stable molecule has less energy than the same set of atoms separated; one must provide at least this energy in order to take the molecule apart.
aliases.normalized_alias: a stable molecule has less energy than the same set of atoms separated; one must provide at least this energy in order to take the molecule apart.

aliases.alias_text: Different kinds of matter exist and many of them can be either solid or liquid, depending on temperature. Matter can be described and classified by its observable properties.
aliases.normalized_alias: different kinds of matter exist and many of them can be either solid or liquid, depending on temperature. matter can be described and classified by its observable properties.

aliases.alias_text: Different properties are suited to different purposes.
aliases.normalized_alias: different properties are suited to different purposes.

aliases.alias_text: Each atom has a charged substructure consisting of a nucleus, which is made of protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons.
aliases.normalized_alias: each atom has a charged substructure consisting of a nucleus, which is made of protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons.

aliases.alias_text: Each pure substance has characteristic physical and chemical properties (for any bulk quantity under given conditions) that can be used to identify it. (MS-PS1-2),(MS-PS1-3)

aliases.alias_text: HS.PS1.A
aliases.normalized_alias: hs.ps1.a

aliases.alias_text: Matter of any type can be subdivided into particles that are too small to see, but even then the matter still exists and can be detected by other means. A model showing that gases are made from matter particles that are too small to see and are moving freely around in space can explain many observations, including the inflation and shape of a balloon and the effects of air on larger particles or objects.

aliases.alias_text: Measurements of a variety of properties can be used to identify materials. (Boundary: At this grade level, mass and weight are not distinguished, and no attempt is made to define the unseen particles or explain the atomic-scale mechanism of evaporation and condensation.)
