chunks.title: PS3.B Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations to provide evidence that energy can be transferred from place to place by sound, light, heat, and electric currents.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Energy is present whenever there are moving objects, sound, light, or heat. When objects collide, energy can be transferred from one object to another, thereby changing their motion. In such collisions, some energy is typically also transferred to the surrounding air; as a result, the air gets heated and sound is produced.
Light also transfers energy from place to place.

Energy can also be transferred from place to place by electric currents, which can then be used locally to produce motion, sound, heat, or light. The currents may have been produced to begin with by transforming the energy of motion into electrical energy.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include quantitative measurements of energy.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions and predict outcomes about the changes in energy that occur when objects collide.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Energy is present whenever there are moving objects, sound, light, or heat. When objects collide, energy can be transferred from one object to another, thereby changing their motion. In such collisions, some energy is typically also transferred to the surrounding air; as a result, the air gets heated and sound is produced.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include quantitative measurements of energy.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the change in the energy due to the change in speed, not on the forces, as objects interact.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.*

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Energy can also be transferred from place to place by electric currents, which can then be used locally to produce motion, sound, heat, or light. The currents may have been produced to begin with by transforming the energy of motion into electrical energy.

payload.assessment_boundary: Devices should be limited to those that convert motion energy to electric energy or use stored energy to cause motion or produce light or sound.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include electric circuits that convert electrical energy into motion energy of a vehicle, light, or sound; and, a passive solar heater that converts light into heat. Examples of constraints could include the materials, cost, or time to design the device.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Create a computational model to calculate the change in the energy of one component in a system when the change in energy of the other component(s) and energy flows in and out of the system are known.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Conservation of energy means that the total change of energy in any system is always equal to the total energy transferred into or out of the system.
Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can be transported from one place to another and transferred between systems.

Mathematical expressions, which quantify how the stored energy in a system depends on its configuration (e.g., relative positions of charged particles, compression of a spring) and how kinetic energy depends on mass and speed, allow the concept of conservation of energy to be used to predict and describe system behavior.
The availability of energy limits what can occur in any system.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to basic algebraic expressions or computations; to systems of two or three components; and to thermal energy, kinetic energy, and/or the energies in gravitational, magnetic, or electric fields.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on explaining the meaning of mathematical expressions used in the model.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that the transfer of thermal energy when two components of different temperature are combined within a closed system results in a more uniform energy distribution among the components in the system (second law of thermodynamics).

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can be transported from one place to another and transferred between systems.

Uncontrolled systems always evolve toward more stable states—that is, toward more uniform energy distribution (e.g., water flows downhill, objects hotter than their surrounding environment cool down).

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to investigations based on materials and tools provided to students.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on analyzing data from student investigations and using mathematical thinking to describe the energy changes both quantitatively and conceptually. Examples of investigations could include mixing liquids at different initial temperatures or adding objects at different temperatures to water.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations to determine the effect of sunlight on Earth’s surface.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Sunlight warms Earth's surface.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of temperature is limited to relative measures such as warmer/cooler.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of Earth’s surface could include sand, soil, rocks, and water.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use tools and materials to design and build a structure that will reduce the warming effect of sunlight on an area.*

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Sunlight warms Earth's surface.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of structures could include umbrellas, canopies, and tents that minimize the warming effect of the sun.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.*

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: Energy is spontaneously transferred out of hotter regions or objects and into colder ones. (MS-PS3-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculating the total amount of thermal energy transferred.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include an insulated box, a solar cooker, and a Styrofoam cup.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: The amount of energy transfer needed to change the temperature of a matter sample by a given amount depends on the nature of the matter, the size of the sample, and the environment. (MS-PS3-4)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculating the total amount of thermal energy transferred.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of experiments could include comparing final water temperatures after different masses of ice melted in the same volume of water with the same initial temperature, the temperature change of samples of different materials with the same mass as they cool or heat in the environment, or the same material with different masses when a specific amount of energy is added.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct, use, and present arguments to support the claim that when the kinetic energy of an object changes, energy is transferred to or from the object.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer
texts: When the motion energy of an object changes, there is inevitably some other change in energy at the same time. (MS-PS3-5)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include calculations of energy.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of empirical evidence used in arguments could include an inventory or other representation of the energy before and after the transfer in the form of temperature changes or motion of object.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-5

payload.core_idea: PS3: Energy
payload.full_name: PS3.B: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer

progression_statements.description: Energy is present whenever there are moving objects, sound, light, or heat. When objects collide, energy can be transferred from one object to another, thereby changing their motion. In such collisions, some energy is typically also transferred to the surrounding air; as a result, the air gets heated and sound is produced.

payload.text: Energy is present whenever there are moving objects, sound, light, or heat. When objects collide, energy can be transferred from one object to another, thereby changing their motion. In such collisions, some energy is typically also transferred to the surrounding air; as a result, the air gets heated and sound is produced.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-E1

progression_statements.description: Light also transfers energy from place to place.

payload.text: Light also transfers energy from place to place.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-E2

progression_statements.description: Energy can also be transferred from place to place by electric currents, which can then be used locally to produce motion, sound, heat, or light. The currents may have been produced to begin with by transforming the energy of motion into electrical energy.

payload.text: Energy can also be transferred from place to place by electric currents, which can then be used locally to produce motion, sound, heat, or light. The currents may have been produced to begin with by transforming the energy of motion into electrical energy.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-E3

progression_statements.description: Conservation of energy means that the total change of energy in any system is always equal to the total energy transferred into or out of the system.

payload.text: Conservation of energy means that the total change of energy in any system is always equal to the total energy transferred into or out of the system.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-H1

progression_statements.description: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can be transported from one place to another and transferred between systems.

payload.text: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can be transported from one place to another and transferred between systems.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-H2

progression_statements.description: Mathematical expressions, which quantify how the stored energy in a system depends on its configuration (e.g., relative positions of charged particles, compression of a spring) and how kinetic energy depends on mass and speed, allow the concept of conservation of energy to be used to predict and describe system behavior.

payload.text: Mathematical expressions, which quantify how the stored energy in a system depends on its configuration (e.g., relative positions of charged particles, compression of a spring) and how kinetic energy depends on mass and speed, allow the concept of conservation of energy to be used to predict and describe system behavior.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-H3

progression_statements.description: The availability of energy limits what can occur in any system.

payload.text: The availability of energy limits what can occur in any system.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-H4

progression_statements.description: Uncontrolled systems always evolve toward more stable states—that is, toward more uniform energy distribution (e.g., water flows downhill, objects hotter than their surrounding environment cool down).

payload.text: Uncontrolled systems always evolve toward more stable states—that is, toward more uniform energy distribution (e.g., water flows downhill, objects hotter than their surrounding environment cool down).

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-H5

progression_statements.description: When the motion energy of an object changes, there is inevitably some other change in energy at the same time.

payload.text: When the motion energy of an object changes, there is inevitably some other change in energy at the same time.

progression_statements.title: PS3.B-M1

progression_statements.description: The amount of energy transfer needed to change the temperature of a matter sample by a given amount depends on the nature of the matter, the size of the sample, and the environment.

payload.text: The amount of energy transfer needed to change the temperature of a matter sample by a given amount depends on the nature of the matter, the size of the sample, and the environment.

chunks.title: PS3.C Relationship Between Energy and Forces

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions and predict outcomes about the changes in energy that occur when objects collide.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.C: Relationship Between Energy and Forces
texts: When objects collide, the contact forces transfer energy so as to change the objects' motions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include quantitative measurements of energy.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the change in the energy due to the change in speed, not on the forces, as objects interact.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model of two objects interacting through electric or magnetic fields to illustrate the forces between objects and the changes in energy of the objects due to the interaction.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.C: Relationship Between Energy and Forces
texts: When two objects interacting through a field change relative position, the energy stored in the field is changed.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to systems containing two objects.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include drawings, diagrams, and texts, such as drawings of what happens when two charges of opposite polarity are near each other.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to compare the effects of different strengths or different directions of pushes and pulls on the motion of an object.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.C: Relationship Between Energy and Forces (secondary)
texts: A bigger push or pull makes things speed up or slow down more quickly. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to different relative strengths or different directions, but not both at the same time. Assessment does not include non-contact pushes or pulls such as those produced by magnets.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of pushes or pulls could include a string attached to an object being pulled, a person pushing an object, a person stopping a rolling ball, and two objects colliding and pushing on each other.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe that when the arrangement of objects interacting at a distance changes, different amounts of potential energy are stored in the system.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.C: Relationship Between Energy and Forces
texts: When two objects interact, each one exerts a force on the other that can cause energy to be transferred to or from the object. (MS-PS3-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to two objects and electric, magnetic, and gravitational interactions.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on relative amounts of potential energy, not on calculations of potential energy. Examples of objects within systems interacting at varying distances could include: the Earth and either a roller coaster cart at varying positions on a hill or objects at varying heights on shelves, changing the direction/orientation of a magnet, and a balloon with static electrical charge being brought closer to a classmate’s hair. Examples of models could include representations, diagrams, pictures, and written descriptions of systems.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-2

chunks.title: PS3.D Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.*

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.D: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life
texts: The expression "produce energy" typically refers to the conversion of stored energy into a desired form for practical use.

payload.assessment_boundary: Devices should be limited to those that convert motion energy to electric energy or use stored energy to cause motion or produce light or sound.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include electric circuits that convert electrical energy into motion energy of a vehicle, light, or sound; and, a passive solar heater that converts light into heat. Examples of constraints could include the materials, cost, or time to design the device.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use models to describe that energy in animals’ food (used for body repair, growth, motion, and to maintain body warmth) was once energy from the sun.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.D: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life
texts: The energy released [from] food was once energy from the sun that was captured by plants in the chemical process that forms plant matter (from air and water).

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include diagrams, and flow charts.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-PS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model based on evidence to illustrate the life span of the sun and the role of nuclear fusion in the sun’s core to release energy in the form of radiation.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.D: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life (secondary)
texts: Nuclear fusion processes in the center of the sun release the energy that ultimately reaches Earth as radiation. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include details of the atomic and sub-atomic processes involved with the sun’s nuclear fusion.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the energy transfer mechanisms that allow energy from nuclear fusion in the sun’s core to reach Earth. Examples of evidence for the model include observations of the masses and lifetimes of other stars, as well as the ways that the sun’s radiation varies due to sudden solar flares (“space weather”), the 11-year sunspot cycle, and non-cyclic variations over centuries.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to illustrate the role of photosynthesis and cellular respiration in the cycling of carbon among the biosphere, atmosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere.

texts: The main way that solar energy is captured and stored on Earth is through the complex chemical process known as photosynthesis. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the specific chemical steps of photosynthesis and respiration.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include simulations and mathematical models.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS2-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Design, build, and refine a device that works within given constraints to convert one form of energy into another form of energy.*

texts: Although energy cannot be destroyed, it can be converted to less useful forms — for example, to thermal energy in the surrounding environment.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment for quantitative evaluations is limited to total output for a given input. Assessment is limited to devices constructed with materials provided to students.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both qualitative and quantitative evaluations of devices. Examples of devices could include Rube Goldberg devices, wind turbines, solar cells, solar ovens, and generators. Examples of constraints could include use of renewable energy forms and efficiency.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that the transfer of thermal energy when two components of different temperature are combined within a closed system results in a more uniform energy distribution among the components in the system (second law of thermodynamics).

texts: Although energy cannot be destroyed, it can be converted to less useful forms — for example, to thermal energy in the surrounding environment.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to investigations based on materials and tools provided to students.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on analyzing data from student investigations and using mathematical thinking to describe the energy changes both quantitatively and conceptually. Examples of investigations could include mixing liquids at different initial temperatures or adding objects at different temperatures to water.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS3-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate technical information about how some technological devices use the principles of wave behavior and wave interactions with matter to transmit and capture information and energy.*

texts: Solar cells are human-made devices that likewise capture the sun’s energy and produce electrical energy. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessments are limited to qualitative information. Assessments do not include band theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include solar cells capturing light and converting it to electricity; medical imaging; and communications technology.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for the role of photosynthesis in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.D: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life
texts: The chemical reaction by which plants produce complex food molecules (sugars) requires an energy input (i.e., from sunlight) to occur. In this reaction, carbon dioxide and water combine to form carbon-based organic molecules and release oxygen. (secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the biochemical mechanisms of photosynthesis.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on tracing movement of matter and flow of energy.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe how food is rearranged through chemical reactions forming new molecules that support growth and/or release energy as this matter moves through an organism.

edge_payload.raw_id: PS3.D: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life
texts: Cellular respiration in plants and animals involve chemical reactions with oxygen that release stored energy. In these processes, complex molecules containing carbon react with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and other materials.(secondary)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include details of the chemical reactions for photosynthesis or respiration.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on describing that molecules are broken apart and put back together and that in this process, energy is released.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-7

aliases.alias_text: Sound can make matter vibrate, and vibrating matter can make sound.
aliases.normalized_alias: sound can make matter vibrate, and vibrating matter can make sound.

aliases.alias_text: The wavelength and frequency of a wave are related to one another by the speed of travel of the wave, which depends on the type of wave and the medium through which it is passing.

aliases.alias_text: Wave Properties

aliases.alias_text: Waves, which are regular patterns of motion, can be made in water by disturbing the surface. When waves move across the surface of deep water, the water goes up and down in place; there is no net motion in the direction of the wave except when the water meets a beach. (Note: This grade band endpoint was moved from K-2.)

payload.core_idea: PS4: Waves and Their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

chunks.text:
Concept PS4.A
Family: DCI
Title: Wave Properties
Context: PS4: Waves and Their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer
Aliases: 4.PS4.A | A simple wave has a repeating pattern with a specific wavelength, frequency, and amplitude. (MS-PS4-1) | A sound wave needs a medium through which it is transmitted. (MS-PS4-2) | Information can be digitized (e.g., a picture stored as the values of an array of pixels); in this form, it can be stored reliably in computer memory and sent over long distances as a series of wave pulses. | MS.PS4.A | PS4.A | PS4.A: Wave Properties | Sound can make matter vibrate, and vibrating matter can make sound. | The wavelength and frequency of a wave are related to one another by the speed of travel of the wave, which depends on the type of wave and the medium through which it is passing. | Wave Properties | Waves, which are regular patterns of motion, can be made in water by disturbing the surface. When waves move across the surface of deep water, the water goes up and down in place; there is no net motion in the direction of the wave except when the water meets a beach. (Note: This grade band endpoint was moved from K-2.) | [From the 3–5 grade band endpoints] Waves can add or cancel one another as they cross, depending on their relative phase (i.e., relative position of peaks and troughs of the waves), but they emerge unaffected by each other. (Boundary: The discussion at this grade level is qualitative only; it can be based on the fact that two different sounds can pass a location in different directions without getting mixed up.)
Progressions: elementary: Waves, which are regular patterns of motion, can be made in water by disturbing the surface. When waves move across the surface of deep water, the water goes up and down in place; there is no net motion in the direction of the wave except when the water meets a beach. | elementary: Waves of the same type can differ in amplitude (height of the wave) and wavelength (spacing between wave peaks). | high: Waves can add or cancel one another as they cross, depending on their relative phase (i.e., relative position of peaks and troughs of the waves), but they emerge unaffected by each other. (Boundary: The discussion at this grade level is qualitative only; it can be based on the fact that two different sounds can pass a location in different directions without getting mixed up.) | high: The wavelength and frequency of a wave are related to one another by the speed of travel of the wave, which depends on the type of wave and the medium through which it is passing. | high: Information can be digitized (e.g., a picture stored as the values of an array of pixels); in this form, it can be stored reliably in computer memory and sent over long distances as a series of wave pulses. | high: Geologists use seismic waves and their reflection at interfaces between layers to probe structures deep in the planet. | middle: A simple wave has a repeating pattern with a specific wavelength, frequency, and amplitude. | middle: A sound wave needs a medium through which it is transmitted. | primary: Sound can make matter vibrate, and vibrating matter can make sound.
chunks.title: PS4.A Wave Properties

records.description: PS4: Waves and Their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct investigations to provide evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.

texts: Sound can make matter vibrate, and vibrating matter can make sound.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of vibrating materials that make sound could include tuning forks and plucking a stretched string. Examples of how sound can make matter vibrate could include holding a piece of paper near a speaker making sound and holding an object near a vibrating tuning fork.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength and that waves can cause objects to move.

texts: Waves, which are regular patterns of motion, can be made in water by disturbing the surface. When waves move across the surface of deep water, the water goes up and down in place; there is no net motion in the direction of the wave except when the water meets a beach. (Note: This grade band endpoint was moved from K-2.)
Waves of the same type can differ in amplitude (height of the wave) and wavelength (spacing between wave peaks).

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include interference effects, electromagnetic waves, non-periodic waves, or quantitative models of amplitude and wavelength.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of models could include diagrams, analogies, and physical models using wire to illustrate wavelength and amplitude of waves.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to support a claim regarding relationships among the frequency, wavelength, and speed of waves traveling in various media.

texts: The wavelength and frequency of a wave are related to one another by the speed of travel of the wave, which depends on the type of wave and the medium through which it is passing.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to algebraic relationships and describing those relationships qualitatively.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include electromagnetic radiation traveling in a vacuum and glass, sound waves traveling through air and water, and seismic waves traveling through the Earth.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate questions about the advantages of using a digital transmission and storage of information.

texts: Information can be digitized (e.g., a picture stored as the values of an array of pixels); in this form, it can be stored reliably in computer memory and sent over long distances as a series of wave pulses.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of advantages could include that digital information is stable because it can be stored reliably in computer memory, transferred easily, and copied and shared rapidly. Disadvantages could include issues of easy deletion, security, and theft.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the claims, evidence, and reasoning behind the idea that electromagnetic radiation can be described either by a wave model or a particle model, and that for some situations one model is more useful than the other.

texts: [From the 3–5 grade band endpoints] Waves can add or cancel one another as they cross, depending on their relative phase (i.e., relative position of peaks and troughs of the waves), but they emerge unaffected by each other. (Boundary: The discussion at this grade level is qualitative only; it can be based on the fact that two different sounds can pass a location in different directions without getting mixed up.)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include using quantum theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how the experimental evidence supports the claim and how a theory is generally modified in light of new evidence. Examples of a phenomenon could include resonance, interference, diffraction, and photoelectric effect.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate technical information about how some technological devices use the principles of wave behavior and wave interactions with matter to transmit and capture information and energy.*

texts: Information can be digitized (e.g., a picture stored as the values of an array of pixels); in this form, it can be stored reliably in computer memory and sent over long distances as a series of wave pulses.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessments are limited to qualitative information. Assessments do not include band theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include solar cells capturing light and converting it to electricity; medical imaging; and communications technology.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave.

texts: A simple wave has a repeating pattern with a specific wavelength, frequency, and amplitude. (MS-PS4-1)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include electromagnetic waves and is limited to standard repeating waves.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on describing waves with both qualitative and quantitative thinking.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.

texts: A sound wave needs a medium through which it is transmitted. (MS-PS4-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative applications pertaining to light and mechanical waves.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both light and mechanical waves. Examples of models could include drawings, simulations, and written descriptions.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-2

aliases.alias_text: An object can be seen when light reflected from its surface enters the eyes.

aliases.alias_text: Atoms of each element emit and absorb characteristic frequencies of light. These characteristics allow identification of the presence of an element, even in microscopic quantities. (secondary)

aliases.alias_text: Electromagnetic Radiation
aliases.normalized_alias: electromagnetic radiation

aliases.alias_text: Electromagnetic radiation (e.g., radio, microwaves, light) can be modeled as a wave of changing electric and magnetic fields or as particles called photons. The wave model is useful for explaining many features of electromagnetic radiation, and the particle model explains other features.
aliases.normalized_alias: electromagnetic radiation (e.g., radio, microwaves, light) can be modeled as a wave of changing electric and magnetic fields or as particles called photons. the wave model is useful for explaining many features of electromagnetic radiation, and the particle model explains other features.

aliases.alias_text: Some materials allow light to pass through them, others allow only some light through and others block all the light and create a dark shadow on any surface beyond them, where the light cannot reach. Mirrors can be used to redirect a light beam. (Boundary: The idea that light travels from place to place is developed through experiences with light sources, mirrors, and shadows, but no attempt is made to discuss the speed of light.)

aliases.alias_text: When light or longer wavelength electromagnetic radiation is absorbed in matter, it is generally converted into thermal energy (heat). Shorter wavelength electromagnetic radiation (ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays) can ionize atoms and cause damage to living cells.

Concept PS4.B
Family: DCI
Title: Electromagnetic Radiation
Context: PS4: Waves and Their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer
Aliases: 1.PS4.B | 4.PS4.B | An object can be seen when light reflected from its surface enters the eyes. | Atoms of each element emit and absorb characteristic frequencies of light. These characteristics allow identification of the presence of an element, even in microscopic quantities. (secondary) | Electromagnetic Radiation | Electromagnetic radiation (e.g., radio, microwaves, light) can be modeled as a wave of changing electric and magnetic fields or as particles called photons. The wave model is useful for explaining many features of electromagnetic radiation, and the particle model explains other features. | HS.PS4.B | MS.PS4.B | Objects can be seen if light is available to illuminate them or if they give off their own light. | PS4.B | PS4.B: Electromagnetic Radiation | PS4.B: Electromagnetic Radiation (secondary)
Progressions: elementary: An object can be seen when light reflected from its surface enters the eyes. | high: Electromagnetic radiation (e.g., radio, microwaves, light) can be modeled as a wave of changing electric and magnetic fields or as particles called photons. The wave model is useful for explaining many features of electromagnetic radiation, and the particle model explains other features. | high: When light or longer wavelength electromagnetic radiation is absorbed in matter, it is generally converted into thermal energy (heat). Shorter wavelength electromagnetic radiation (ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays) can ionize atoms and cause damage to living cells. | high: Photovoltaic materials emit electrons when they absorb light of a high-enough frequency. | high: Atoms of each element emit and absorb characteristic frequencies of light. These characteristics allow identification of the presence of an element, even in microscopic quantities. | middle: When light shines on an object, it is reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through the object, depending on the object's material and the frequency (color) of the light. | middle: The path that light travels can be traced as straight lines, except at surfaces between different transparent materials (e.g., air and water, air and glass) where the light path bends. | middle: A wave model of light is useful for explaining brightness, color, and the frequency-dependent bending of light at a surface between media. | middle: However, because light can travel through space, it cannot be a matter wave, like sound or water waves. | primary: Objects can be seen only when light is available to illuminate them. Some objects give off their own light. | primary: Some materials allow light to pass through them, others allow only some light through, and still others block all the light and create a dark shadow on any surface beyond them, where the light cannot reach. Mirrors can be used to redirect a light beam. (Boundary: The idea that light travels from place to place is developed through experiences with light sources, mirrors, and shadows, but no attempt is made to discuss the speed of light.)

records.description: PS4: Waves and Their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that objects can be seen only when illuminated.

texts: Objects can be seen if light is available to illuminate them or if they give off their own light.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of observations could include those made in a completely dark room, a pinhole box, and a video of a cave explorer with a flashlight. Illumination could be from an external light source or by an object giving off its own light.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine the effect of placing objects made with different materials in the path of a beam of light.

texts: Some materials allow light to pass through them, others allow only some light through and others block all the light and create a dark shadow on any surface beyond them, where the light cannot reach. Mirrors can be used to redirect a light beam. (Boundary: The idea that light travels from place to place is developed through experiences with light sources, mirrors, and shadows, but no attempt is made to discuss the speed of light.)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the speed of light.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of materials could include those that are transparent (such as clear plastic), translucent (such as wax paper), opaque (such as cardboard), and reflective (such as a mirror).
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop a model to describe that light reflecting from objects and entering the eye allows objects to be seen.

texts: An object can be seen when light reflected from its surface enters the eyes.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include knowledge of specific colors reflected and seen, the cellular mechanisms of vision, or how the retina works.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct an explanation of the Big Bang theory based on astronomical evidence of light spectra, motion of distant galaxies, and composition of matter in the universe.

texts: Atoms of each element emit and absorb characteristic frequencies of light. These characteristics allow identification of the presence of an element, even in microscopic quantities. (secondary)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the astronomical evidence of the red shift of light from galaxies as an indication that the universe is currently expanding, the cosmic microwave background as the remnant radiation from the Big Bang, and the observed composition of ordinary matter of the universe, primarily found in stars and interstellar gases (from the spectra of electromagnetic radiation from stars), which matches that predicted by the Big Bang theory (3/4 hydrogen and 1/4 helium).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the claims, evidence, and reasoning behind the idea that electromagnetic radiation can be described either by a wave model or a particle model, and that for some situations one model is more useful than the other.

texts: Electromagnetic radiation (e.g., radio, microwaves, light) can be modeled as a wave of changing electric and magnetic fields or as particles called photons. The wave model is useful for explaining many features of electromagnetic radiation, and the particle model explains other features.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include using quantum theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how the experimental evidence supports the claim and how a theory is generally modified in light of new evidence. Examples of a phenomenon could include resonance, interference, diffraction, and photoelectric effect.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the validity and reliability of claims in published materials of the effects that different frequencies of electromagnetic radiation have when absorbed by matter.

texts: When light or longer wavelength electromagnetic radiation is absorbed in matter, it is generally converted into thermal energy (heat). Shorter wavelength electromagnetic radiation (ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays) can ionize atoms and cause damage to living cells.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative descriptions.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the idea that photons associated with different frequencies of light have different energies, and the damage to living tissue from electromagnetic radiation depends on the energy of the radiation. Examples of published materials could include trade books, magazines, web resources, videos, and other passages that may reflect bias.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate technical information about how some technological devices use the principles of wave behavior and wave interactions with matter to transmit and capture information and energy.*

texts: Photoelectric materials emit electrons when they absorb light of a high-enough frequency.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessments are limited to qualitative information. Assessments do not include band theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include solar cells capturing light and converting it to electricity; medical imaging; and communications technology.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.

texts: When light shines on an object, it is reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through the object, depending on the object’s material and the frequency (color) of the light. (MS-PS4-2)
The path that light travels can be traced as straight lines, except at surfaces between different transparent materials (e.g., air and water, air and glass) where the light path bends. (MS-PS4-2)
A wave model of light is useful for explaining brightness, color, and the frequency-dependent bending of light at a surface between media. (MS-PS4-2)
However, because light can travel through space, it cannot be a matter wave, like sound or water waves. (MS-PS4-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative applications pertaining to light and mechanical waves.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on both light and mechanical waves. Examples of models could include drawings, simulations, and written descriptions.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-2

records.description: PS4: Waves and Their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use tools and materials to design and build a device that uses light or sound to solve the problem of communicating over a distance.*

texts: People also use a variety of devices to communicate (send and receive information) over long distances.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include technological details for how communication devices work.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices could include a light source to send signals, paper cup and string "telephones," and a pattern of drum beats.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-PS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transfer information.*

texts: Digitized information can be transmitted over long distances without significant degradation. High-tech devices, such as computers or cell phones, can receive and decode information—convert it from digitized form to voice—and vice versa.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of solutions could include drums sending coded information through sound waves, using a grid of 1's and 0's representing black and white to send information about a picture, and using Morse code to send text.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate technical information about how some technological devices use the principles of wave behavior and wave interactions with matter to transmit and capture information and energy.*

texts: Multiple technologies based on the understanding of waves and their interactions with matter are part of everyday experiences in the modern world (e.g., medical imaging, communications, scanners) and in scientific research. They are essential tools for producing, transmitting, and capturing signals and for storing and interpreting the information contained in them.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessments are limited to qualitative information. Assessments do not include band theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include solar cells capturing light and converting it to electricity; medical imaging; and communications technology.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that digitized signals are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information than analog signals.

texts: Digitized signals (sent as wave pulses) are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information. (MS-PS4-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include binary counting. Assessment does not include the specific mechanism of any given device.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a basic understanding that waves can be used for communication purposes. Examples could include using fiber optic cable to transmit light pulses, radio wave pulses in wifi devices, and conversion of stored binary patterns to make sound or text on a computer screen.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-3

chunks.text:
Concept AQDP
Family: SEP
Title: Asking Questions and Defining Problems
Context: Science and Engineering Practices
Aliases: AQDP: Asking Questions and Defining Problems | Asking Questions and Defining Problems | Asking questions and defining problems in 3–5 builds on grades K–2 experiences and progresses to specifying qualitative relationships. | Asking questions and defining problems in 9-12 builds on K-8 experiences and progresses to formulating, refining and evaluating empirically testable questions and design problems using models and simulations. | Asking questions and defining problems in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to formulating, refining, and evaluating empirically testable questions and design problems using models and simulations. | Asking questions and defining problems in K–2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to simple descriptive questions. | Asking questions and defining problems in grades 3-5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to specifying qualitative relationships. | Asking questions and defining problems in grades 3-5 builds on grades K-2 experiences and progresses to specifying qualitative relationships. | Asking questions and defining problems in grades 6–8 builds from grades K–5 experiences and progresses to specifying relationships between variables, and clarifying arguments and models. | Asking questions and defining problems in grades 6–8 builds on grades K–5 experiences and progresses to specifying relationships between variables, and clarifying arguments and models. | Asking questions and defining problems in grades 9–12 builds from grades K–8 experiences and progresses to formulating, refining, and evaluating empirically testable questions and design problems using models and simulations. | Asking questions and defining problems in grades K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to simple descriptive questions that can be tested.
Progressions: elementary: Ask questions about what would happen if a variable is changed. | elementary: Identify scientific (testable) and non-scientific (non-testable) questions. | elementary: Ask questions that can be investigated and predict reasonable outcomes based on patterns such as cause and effect relationships. | elementary: Use prior knowledge to describe problems that can be solved. | elementary: Define a simple design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process, or system and includes several criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost. | high: Ask questions that arise from careful observation of phenomena, or unexpected results, to clarify and/or seek additional information. | high: Ask questions that arise from examining models or a theory, to clarify and/or seek additional information and relationships. | high: Ask questions to determine relationships, including quantitative relationships, between independent and dependent variables. | high: Ask questions to clarify and refine a model, an explanation, or an engineering problem. | high: Evaluate a question to determine if it is testable and relevant. | high: Ask questions that can be investigated within the scope of the school laboratory, research facilities, or field (e.g., outdoor environment) with available resources and, when appropriate, frame a hypothesis based on a model or theory. | high: Ask and/or evaluate questions that challenge the premise(s) of an argument, the interpretation of a data set, or the suitability of the design.
chunks.title: AQDP Asking Questions and Defining Problems

linked_performance_expectations.description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in 3–5 builds on grades K–2 experiences and progresses to specifying qualitative relationships.
Define a simple design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process, or system and includes several criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-5-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to determine cause and effect relationships of electric or magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades 3-5 builds on grades K-2 experiences and progresses to specifying qualitative relationships.
Ask questions that can be investigated based on patterns such as cause and effect relationships.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to forces produced by objects that can be manipulated by students, and electrical interactions are limited to static electricity.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of an electric force could include the force on hair from an electrically charged balloon and the electrical forces between a charged rod and pieces of paper; examples of a magnetic force could include the force between two permanent magnets, the force between an electromagnet and steel paperclips, and the force exerted by one magnet versus the force exerted by two magnets. Examples of cause and effect relationships could include how the distance between objects affects strength of the force and how the orientation of magnets affects the direction of the magnetic force.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-PS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Define a simple design problem that can be solved by applying scientific ideas about magnets.*

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades 3-5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to specifying qualitative relationships.
Define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of problems could include constructing a latch to keep a door shut and creating a device to keep two moving objects from touching each other.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-PS2-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions and predict outcomes about the changes in energy that occur when objects collide.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades 3-5 builds on grades K-2 experiences and progresses to specifying qualitative relationships.
Ask questions that can be investigated and predict reasonable outcomes based on patterns such as cause and effect relationships.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include quantitative measurements of energy.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the change in the energy due to the change in speed, not on the forces, as objects interact.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-PS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze a major global challenge to specify qualitative and quantitative criteria and constraints for solutions that account for societal needs and wants.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to formulating, refining, and evaluating empirically testable questions and design problems using models and simulations.
Analyze complex real-world problems by specifying criteria and constraints for successful solutions.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to clarify relationships about the role of DNA and chromosomes in coding the instructions for characteristic traits passed from parents to offspring.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in 9-12 builds on K-8 experiences and progresses to formulating, refining and evaluating empirically testable questions and design problems using models and simulations.
Ask questions that arise from examining models or a theory to clarify relationships.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.clarification_statement: Assessment Boundary: Assessment does not include the phases of meiosis or the biochemical mechanism of specific steps in the process.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate questions about the advantages of using a digital transmission and storage of information.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades 9–12 builds from grades K–8 experiences and progresses to formulating, refining, and evaluating empirically testable questions and design problems using models and simulations.
Evaluate questions that challenge the premise(s) of an argument, the interpretation of a data set or the suitability of a design.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of advantages could include that digital information is stable because it can be stored reliably in computer memory, transferred easily, and copied and shared rapidly. Disadvantages could include issues of easy deletion, security, and theft.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in K–2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to simple descriptive questions.
Ask questions based on observations to find more information about the natural and/or designed world(s).
Define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-2-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for, and respond to, severe weather.*

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to simple descriptive questions that can be tested.
Ask questions based on observations to find more information about the designed world.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on local forms of severe weather.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades 6–8 builds on grades K–5 experiences and progresses to specifying relationships between variables, and clarifying arguments and models.
Ask questions to identify and clarify evidence of an argument.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of factors include human activities (such as fossil fuel combustion, cement production, and agricultural activity) and natural processes (such as changes in incoming solar radiation or volcanic activity). Examples of evidence can include tables, graphs, and maps of global and regional temperatures, atmospheric levels of gases such as carbon dioxide and methane, and the rates of human activities. Emphasis is on the major role that human activities play in causing the rise in global temperatures.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision to ensure a successful solution, taking into account relevant scientific principles and potential impacts on people and the natural environment that may limit possible solutions.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades 6–8 builds on grades K–5 experiences and progresses to specifying relationships between variables, and clarifying arguments and models.

Define a design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process or system and includes multiple criteria and constraints, including scientific knowledge that may limit possible solutions.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces.

texts: Asking questions and defining problems in grades 6–8 builds from grades K–5 experiences and progresses to specifying relationships between variables, and clarifying arguments and models.

Ask questions that can be investigated within the scope of the classroom, outdoor environment, and museums and other public facilities with available resources and, when appropriate, frame a hypothesis based on observations and scientific principles. (MS-PS2-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment about questions that require quantitative answers is limited to proportional reasoning and algebraic thinking.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of devices that use electric and magnetic forces could include electromagnets, electric motors, or generators. Examples of data could include the effect of the number of turns of wire on the strength of an electromagnet, or the effect of increasing the number or strength of magnets on the speed of an electric motor.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS2-3
