chunks.text:
Concept DATA
Family: SEP
Title: Analyzing and Interpreting Data
Context: Science and Engineering Practices
Aliases: Analyzing and Interpreting Data | Analyzing data in 3-5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to introducing quantitative approaches to collecting data and conducting multiple trials of qualitative observations. When possible and feasible, digital tools should be used. | Analyzing data in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to introducing quantitative approaches to collecting data and conducting multiple trials of qualitative observations. When possible and feasible, digital tools should be used. | Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis. | Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis. | Analyzing data in 9-12 builds on K-8 experiences and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data. | Analyzing data in 9–12 builds on K–8 and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data. | Analyzing data in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data. | Analyzing data in K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations. | Analyzing data in K–2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations. | DATA: Analyzing and Interpreting Data
Progressions: elementary: Represent data in tables and/or various graphical displays (bar graphs, pictographs, and/or pie charts) to reveal patterns that indicate relationships. | elementary: Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena, using logical reasoning, mathematics, and/or computation. | elementary: Compare and contrast data collected by different groups in order to discuss similarities and differences in their findings. | elementary: Analyze data to refine a problem statement or the design of a proposed object, tool, or process. | elementary: Use data to evaluate and refine design solutions. | high: Analyze data using tools, technologies, and/or models (e.g., computational, mathematical) in order to make valid and reliable scientific claims or determine an optimal design solution. | high: Apply concepts of statistics and probability (including determining function fits to data, slope, intercept, and correlation coefficient for linear fits) to scientific and engineering questions and problems, using digital tools when feasible. | high: Consider limitations of data analysis (e.g., measurement error, sample selection) when analyzing and interpreting data. | high: Compare and contrast various types of data sets (e.g., self-generated, archival) to examine consistency of measurements and observations. | high: Evaluate the impact of new data on a working explanation and/or model of a proposed process or system. | high: Analyze data to identify design features or characteristics of the components of a proposed process, or system to optimize it relative to criteria for success. | middle: Construct, analyze, and/or interpret graphical displays of data and/or large data sets to identify linear and nonlinear relationships.
chunks.title: DATA Analyzing and Interpreting Data

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use observations of the sun, moon, and stars to describe patterns that can be predicted.

texts: Analyzing data in K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations.
Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns in the natural world in order to answer scientific questions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of star patterns is limited to stars being seen at night and not during the day.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include that the sun and moon appear to rise in one part of the sky, move across the sky, and set; and stars other than our sun are visible at night but not during the day.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-ESS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data obtained from testing different materials to determine which materials have the properties that are best suited for an intended purpose.*

texts: Analyzing data in K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations.
Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of quantitative measurements is limited to length.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of properties could include, strength, flexibility, hardness, texture, and absorbency.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Represent data in tables and graphical displays to describe typical weather conditions expected during a particular season.

texts: Analyzing data in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to introducing quantitative approaches to collecting data and conducting multiple trials of qualitative observations. When possible and feasible, digital tools should be used.
Represent data in tables and various graphical displays (bar graphs and pictographs) to reveal patterns that indicate relationships.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of graphical displays is limited to pictographs and bar graphs. Assessment does not include climate change.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include average temperature, precipitation, and wind direction.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from parents and that variation of these traits exists in a group of similar organisms.

texts: Analyzing data in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to introducing quantitative approaches to collecting data and conducting multiple trials of qualitative observations. When possible and feasible, digital tools should be used.
Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena using logical reasoning.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include genetic mechanisms of inheritance and prediction of traits. Assessment is limited to non-human examples.
payload.clarification_statement: Patterns are the similarities and differences in traits shared between offspring and their parents, or among siblings. Emphasis is on organisms other than humans.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of the organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.

texts: Analyzing data in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to introducing quantitative approaches to collecting data and conducting multiple trials of qualitative observations. When possible and feasible, digital tools should be used.
Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena using logical reasoning.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include identification of specific fossils or present plants and animals. Assessment is limited to major fossil types and relative ages.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include type, size, and distributions of fossil organisms. Examples of fossils and environments could include marine fossils found on dry land, tropical plant fossils found in Arctic areas, and fossils of extinct organisms.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data from maps to describe patterns of Earth’s features.

texts: Analyzing data in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to introducing quantitative approaches to collecting data and conducting multiple trials of qualitative observations. When possible and feasible, digital tools should be used.
Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena using logical reasoning.

payload.clarification_statement: Maps can include topographic maps of Earth’s land and ocean floor, as well as maps of the locations of mountains, continental boundaries, volcanoes, and earthquakes.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.

texts: Analyzing data in 3-5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to introducing quantitative approaches to collecting data and conducting multiple trials of qualitative observations. When possible and feasible, digital tools should be used.
Represent data in graphical displays (bar graphs, pictographs and/or pie charts) to reveal patterns that indicate relationships.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include causes of seasons.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include the position and motion of Earth with respect to the sun and selected stars that are visible only in particular months.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze geoscience data to make the claim that one change to Earth's surface can create feedbacks that cause changes to other Earth systems.

texts: Analyzing data in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data.
Analyze data using tools, technologies, and/or models (e.g., computational, mathematical) in order to make valid and reliable scientific claims or determine an optimal design solution.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples should include climate feedbacks, such as how an increase in greenhouse gases causes a rise in global temperatures that melts glacial ice, which reduces the amount of sunlight reflected from Earth's surface, increasing surface temperatures and further reducing the amount of ice. Examples could also be taken from other system interactions, such as how the loss of ground vegetation causes an increase in water runoff and soil erosion; how dammed rivers increase groundwater recharge, decrease sediment transport, and increase coastal erosion; or how the loss of wetlands causes a decrease in local humidity that further reduces the wetland extent.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze geoscience data and the results from global climate models to make an evidence-based forecast of the current rate of global or regional climate change and associated future impacts to Earth systems.

texts: Analyzing data in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data.
Analyze data using computational models in order to make valid and reliable scientific claims.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to one example of a climate change and its associated impacts.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of evidence, for both data and climate model outputs, are for climate changes (such as precipitation and temperature) and their associated impacts (such as on sea level, glacial ice volumes, or atmosphere and ocean composition).

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS3-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to explain the variation and distribution of expressed traits in a population.

texts: Analyzing data in 9-12 builds on K-8 experiences and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data.

Apply concepts of statistics and probability (including determining function fits to data, slope, intercept, and correlation coefficient for linear fits) to scientific and engineering questions and problems, using digital tools when feasible.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include Hardy-Weinberg calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the use of mathematics to describe the probability of traits as it relates to genetic and environmental factors in the expression of traits.
payload.topic_title: Heredity: Inheritance and Variation of Traits

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability to support explanations that organisms with an advantageous heritable trait tend to increase in proportion to organisms lacking this trait.

texts: Analyzing data in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data.

Apply concepts of statistics and probability (including determining function fits to data, slope, intercept, and correlation coefficient for linear fits) to scientific and engineering questions and problems, using digital tools when feasible.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to basic statistical and graphical analysis. Assessment does not include allele frequency calculations.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on analyzing shifts in numerical distribution of traits and using these shifts as evidence to support explanations.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data to support the claim that Newton’s second law of motion describes the mathematical relationship among the net force on a macroscopic object, its mass, and its acceleration.

texts: Analyzing data in 9–12 builds on K–8 and progresses to introducing more detailed statistical analysis, the comparison of data sets for consistency, and the use of models to generate and analyze data.
Analyze data using tools, technologies, and/or models (e.g., computational, mathematical) in order to make valid and reliable scientific claims or determine an optimal design solution.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to one-dimensional motion and to macroscopic objects moving at non-relativistic speeds.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data could include tables or graphs of position or velocity as a function of time for objects subject to a net unbalanced force, such as a falling object, an object rolling down a ramp, or a moving object being pulled by a constant force.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.

texts: Analyzing data in K–2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations.
Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-2-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use and share observations of local weather conditions to describe patterns over time.

texts: Analyzing data in K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations.
Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns in the natural world in order to answer scientific questions.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of quantitative observations limited to whole numbers and relative measures such as warmer/cooler.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of qualitative observations could include descriptions of the weather (such as sunny, cloudy, rainy, and warm); examples of quantitative observations could include numbers of sunny, windy, and rainy days in a month. Examples of patterns could include that it is usually cooler in the morning than in the afternoon and the number of sunny days versus cloudy days in different months.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.

texts: Analyzing data in K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations.
Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns in the natural world in order to answer scientific questions.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns could include that animals need to take in food but plants do not; the different kinds of food needed by different types of animals; the requirement of plants to have light; and, that all living things need water.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-LS1-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data to determine if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a push or a pull.*

texts: Analyzing data in K-2 builds on prior experiences and progresses to collecting, recording, and sharing observations.
Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include friction as a mechanism for change in speed.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of problems requiring a solution could include having a marble or other object move a certain distance, follow a particular path, and knock down other objects. Examples of solutions could include tools such as a ramp to increase the speed of the object and a structure that would cause an object such as a marble or ball to turn.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-PS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include recalling facts about properties of the planets and other solar system bodies.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the analysis of data from Earth-based instruments, space-based telescopes, and spacecraft to determine similarities and differences among solar system objects. Examples of scale properties include the sizes of an object's layers (such as crust and atmosphere), surface features (such as volcanoes), and orbital radius. Examples of data include statistical information, drawings and photographs, and models.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for phenomena.

payload.assessment_boundary: Paleomagnetic anomalies in oceanic and continental crust are not assessed.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of data include similarities of rock and fossil types on different continents, the shapes of the continents (including continental shelves), and the locations of ocean structures (such as ridges, fracture zones, and trenches).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on natural hazards to forecast future catastrophic events and inform the development of technologies to mitigate their effects.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on how some natural hazards, such as volcanic eruptions and severe weather, are preceded by phenomena that allow for reliable predictions, but others, such as earthquakes, occur suddenly and with no notice, and thus are not yet predictable. Examples of natural hazards can be taken from interior processes (such as earthquakes and volcanic eruptions), surface processes (such as mass wasting and tsunamis), or severe weather events (such as hurricanes, tornadoes, and floods). Examples of data can include the locations, magnitudes, and frequencies of the natural hazards. Examples of technologies can be global (such as satellite systems to monitor hurricanes or forest fires) or local (such as building basements in tornado-prone regions or reservoirs to mitigate droughts).

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-ETS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for phenomena.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on cause and effect relationships between resources and growth of individual organisms and the numbers of organisms in ecosystems during periods of abundant and scarce resources.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS2-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include the names of individual species or geological eras in the fossil record.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on finding patterns of changes in the level of complexity of anatomical structures in organisms and the chronological order of fossil appearance in the rock layers.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze displays of pictorial data to compare patterns of similarities in the embryological development across multiple species to identify relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze displays of data to identify linear and nonlinear relationships.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment of comparisons is limited to gross appearance of anatomical structures in embryological development.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on inferring general patterns of relatedness among embryos of different organisms by comparing the macroscopic appearance of diagrams or pictures.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings. (MS-PS1-2)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to analysis of the following properties: density, melting point, boiling point, solubility, flammability, and odor.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples of reactions could include burning sugar or steel wool, fat reacting with sodium hydroxide, and mixing zinc with hydrogen chloride.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass of an object and to the speed of an object.

texts: Analyzing data in 6–8 builds on K–5 and progresses to extending quantitative analysis to investigations, distinguishing between correlation and causation, and basic statistical techniques of data and error analysis.
Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to identify linear and nonlinear relationships. (MS-PS3-1)

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on descriptive relationships between kinetic energy and mass separately from kinetic energy and speed. Examples could include riding a bicycle at different speeds, rolling different sizes of rocks downhill, and getting hit by a wiffle ball versus a tennis ball.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS3-1

payload.full_name: DATA: Analyzing and Interpreting Data

progression_statements.description: Represent data in tables and/or various graphical displays (bar graphs, pictographs, and/or pie charts) to reveal patterns that indicate relationships.

payload.text: Represent data in tables and/or various graphical displays (bar graphs, pictographs, and/or pie charts) to reveal patterns that indicate relationships.

progression_statements.title: DATA-E1

progression_statements.description: Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena, using logical reasoning, mathematics, and/or computation.

payload.text: Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena, using logical reasoning, mathematics, and/or computation.

progression_statements.title: DATA-E2

progression_statements.description: Compare and contrast data collected by different groups in order to discuss similarities and differences in their findings.

payload.text: Compare and contrast data collected by different groups in order to discuss similarities and differences in their findings.

progression_statements.title: DATA-E3

progression_statements.description: Analyze data to refine a problem statement or the design of a proposed object, tool, or process.

payload.text: Analyze data to refine a problem statement or the design of a proposed object, tool, or process.

progression_statements.title: DATA-E4

progression_statements.description: Use data to evaluate and refine design solutions.

payload.text: Use data to evaluate and refine design solutions.

progression_statements.title: DATA-E5

progression_statements.description: Analyze data using tools, technologies, and/or models (e.g., computational, mathematical) in order to make valid and reliable scientific claims or determine an optimal design solution.

payload.text: Analyze data using tools, technologies, and/or models (e.g., computational, mathematical) in order to make valid and reliable scientific claims or determine an optimal design solution.

progression_statements.title: DATA-H1

progression_statements.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability (including determining function fits to data, slope, intercept, and correlation coefficient for linear fits) to scientific and engineering questions and problems, using digital tools when feasible.

payload.text: Apply concepts of statistics and probability (including determining function fits to data, slope, intercept, and correlation coefficient for linear fits) to scientific and engineering questions and problems, using digital tools when feasible.

progression_statements.title: DATA-H2

progression_statements.description: Consider limitations of data analysis (e.g., measurement error, sample selection) when analyzing and interpreting data.

payload.text: Consider limitations of data analysis (e.g., measurement error, sample selection) when analyzing and interpreting data.

progression_statements.title: DATA-H3

progression_statements.description: Compare and contrast various types of data sets (e.g., self-generated, archival) to examine consistency of measurements and observations.

payload.text: Compare and contrast various types of data sets (e.g., self-generated, archival) to examine consistency of measurements and observations.

progression_statements.title: DATA-H4

progression_statements.description: Evaluate the impact of new data on a working explanation and/or model of a proposed process or system.

payload.text: Evaluate the impact of new data on a working explanation and/or model of a proposed process or system.

progression_statements.title: DATA-H5

progression_statements.description: Analyze data to identify design features or characteristics of the components of a proposed process, or system to optimize it relative to criteria for success.

payload.text: Analyze data to identify design features or characteristics of the components of a proposed process, or system to optimize it relative to criteria for success.

progression_statements.title: DATA-H6

progression_statements.description: Construct, analyze, and/or interpret graphical displays of data and/or large data sets to identify linear and nonlinear relationships.

payload.text: Construct, analyze, and/or interpret graphical displays of data and/or large data sets to identify linear and nonlinear relationships.

progression_statements.title: DATA-M1

progression_statements.description: Use graphical displays (e.g., maps, charts, graphs, and/or tables) of large data sets to identify temporal and spatial relationships.

payload.text: Use graphical displays (e.g., maps, charts, graphs, and/or tables) of large data sets to identify temporal and spatial relationships.

progression_statements.title: DATA-M2

progression_statements.description: Distinguish between causal and correlational relationships in data.

payload.text: Distinguish between causal and correlational relationships in data.

progression_statements.title: DATA-M3

progression_statements.description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for phenomena.

payload.text: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for phenomena.

progression_statements.title: DATA-M4

progression_statements.description: Apply concepts of statistics and probability (including mean, median, mode, and variability) to analyze and characterize data, using digital tools when feasible.

payload.text: Apply concepts of statistics and probability (including mean, median, mode, and variability) to analyze and characterize data, using digital tools when feasible.

progression_statements.title: DATA-M5

progression_statements.description: Consider limitations of data analysis (e.g., measurement error) and/or seek to improve precision and accuracy of data with better technological tools and methods (e.g., multiple trials).

payload.text: Consider limitations of data analysis (e.g., measurement error) and/or seek to improve precision and accuracy of data with better technological tools and methods (e.g., multiple trials).

progression_statements.title: DATA-M6

progression_statements.description: Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.

payload.text: Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.

progression_statements.title: DATA-M7

progression_statements.description: Analyze data to define an optimal operational range for a proposed object, tool, process or system that best meets criteria for success.

payload.text: Analyze data to define an optimal operational range for a proposed object, tool, process or system that best meets criteria for success.

progression_statements.title: DATA-M8

progression_statements.description: Record information (observations, thoughts, and ideas).

payload.text: Record information (observations, thoughts, and ideas).

progression_statements.title: DATA-P1

progression_statements.description: Use and share pictures, drawings, and/or writings of observations.

payload.text: Use and share pictures, drawings, and/or writings of observations.

progression_statements.title: DATA-P2

progression_statements.description: Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns and/or relationships in the natural and designed world(s) in order to answer scientific questions and solve problems.

payload.text: Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns and/or relationships in the natural and designed world(s) in order to answer scientific questions and solve problems.

progression_statements.title: DATA-P3

progression_statements.description: Compare predictions (based on prior experiences) to what occurred (observable events).

payload.text: Compare predictions (based on prior experiences) to what occurred (observable events).

progression_statements.title: DATA-P4

progression_statements.description: Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.

payload.text: Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.

progression_statements.title: DATA-P5

chunks.text:
Concept INFO
Family: SEP
Title: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information
Context: Science and Engineering Practices
Aliases: INFO: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information | Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 3-5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to evaluate the merit and accuracy of ideas and methods. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to evaluating the merit and accuracy of ideas and methods. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 6-8 builds on K-5 experiences and progresses to evaluating the merit and validity of ideas and methods. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 6–8 builds on K–5 and progresses to evaluating the merit and validity of ideas and methods. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to evaluating the merit and validity of ideas and methods. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 9–12 builds on K–8 and progresses to evaluating the validity and reliability of the claims, methods, and designs. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to evaluating the validity and reliability of the claims, methods, and designs. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in K-2 builds on prior experiences and uses observations and texts to communicate new information. | Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in K–2 builds on prior experiences and uses observations and texts to communicate new information.
Progressions: elementary: Read and comprehend grade-appropriate complex texts and/or other reliable media to summarize and obtain scientific and technical ideas and describe how they are supported by evidence. | elementary: Compare and/or combine across complex texts and/or other reliable media to support the engagement in other scientific and/or engineering practices. | elementary: Combine information in written text with that contained in corresponding tables, diagrams, and/or charts to support the engagement in other scientific and/or engineering practices. | elementary: Obtain and combine information from books and/or other reliable media to explain phenomena or solutions to a design problem. | elementary: Communicate scientific and/or technical information orally and/or in written formats, including various forms of media as well as tables, diagrams, and charts. | high: Critically read scientific literature adapted for classroom use to determine the central ideas or conclusions and/or to obtain scientific and/or technical information to summarize complex evidence, concepts, processes, or information presented in a text by paraphrasing them in simpler but still accurate terms. | high: Compare, integrate and evaluate sources of information presented in different media or formats (e.g., visually, quantitatively) as well as in words in order to address a scientific question or solve a problem. | high: Gather, read, and evaluate scientific and/or technical information from multiple authoritative sources, assessing the evidence and usefulness of each source. | high: Evaluate the validity and reliability of and/or synthesize multiple claims, methods, and/or designs that appear in scientific and technical texts or media reports, verifying the data when possible. | high: Communicate scientific and/or technical information or ideas (e.g., about phenomena and/or the process of development and the design and performance of a proposed process or system) in multiple formats (i.e., orally, graphically, textually, and mathematically). | middle: Critically read scientific texts adapted for classroom use to determine the central ideas and/or obtain scientific and/or technical information to describe patterns in and/or evidence about the natural and designed world(s). | middle: Integrate qualitative and/or quantitative scientific and/or technical information in written text with that contained in media and visual displays to clarify claims and findings.
chunks.title: INFO Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information

linked_performance_expectations.description: Read texts and use media to determine patterns in behavior of parents and offspring that help offspring survive.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in K–2 builds on prior experiences and uses observations and texts to communicate new information.
Read grade-appropriate texts and use media to obtain scientific information to determine patterns in the natural world.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of patterns of behaviors could include the signals that offspring make (such as crying, cheeping, and other vocalizations) and the responses of the parents (such as feeding, comforting, and protecting the offspring).
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: 1-LS1-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain information to identify where water is found on Earth and that it can be solid or liquid.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in K-2 builds on prior experiences and uses observations and texts to communicate new information.

Obtain information using various texts, text features (e.g., headings, tables of contents, glossaries, electronic menus, icons), and other media that will be useful in answering a scientific question.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 2-ESS2-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information to describe climates in different regions of the world.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to evaluating the merit and accuracy of ideas and methods.
Obtain and combine information from books and other reliable media to explain phenomena.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 3-ESS2-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information to describe that energy and fuels are derived from natural resources and that their uses affect the environment.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 3-5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to evaluate the merit and accuracy of ideas and methods.
Obtain and combine information from books and other reliable media to explain phenomena.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of renewable energy resources could include wind energy, water behind dams, and sunlight; non-renewable energy resources are fossil fuels and fissile materials. Examples of environmental effects could include loss of habitat due to dams, loss of habitat due to surface mining, and air pollution from burning of fossil fuels.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 4-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science ideas to protect the Earth’s resources and environment.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 3–5 builds on K-2 experiences and progresses to evaluating the merit and accuracy of ideas and methods.
Obtain and combine information from books and/or other reliable media to explain phenomena or solutions to a design problem.

linked_performance_expectations.title: 5-ESS3-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific ideas about the way stars, over their life cycle, produce elements.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to evaluating the validity and reliability of the claims, methods, and designs.

Communicate scientific ideas (e.g., about phenomena and/or the process of development and the design and performance of a proposed process or system) in multiple formats (including orally, graphically, textually, and mathematically).

payload.assessment_boundary: Details of the many different nucleosynthesis pathways for stars of differing masses are not assessed.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the way nucleosynthesis, and therefore the different elements created, varies as a function of the mass of a star and the stage of its lifetime.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-ESS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific information that common ancestry and biological evolution are supported by multiple lines of empirical evidence.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 9–12 builds on K–8 experiences and progresses to evaluating the validity and reliability of the claims, methods, and designs.

Communicate scientific information (e.g., about phenomena and/or the process of development and the design and performance of a proposed process or system) in multiple formats (including orally, graphically, textually, and mathematically).

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a conceptual understanding of the role each line of evidence has relating to common ancestry and biological evolution. Examples of evidence could include similarities in DNA sequences, anatomical structures, and order of appearance of structures in embryological development.

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-LS4-1

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate scientific and technical information about why the molecular-level structure is important in the functioning of designed materials.*

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 9–12 builds on K–8 and progresses to evaluating the validity and reliability of the claims, methods, and designs.

Communicate scientific and technical information (e.g., about the process of development and the design and performance of a proposed process or system) in multiple formats (including orally, graphically, textually, and mathematically).

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to provided molecular structures of specific designed materials.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the attractive and repulsive forces that determine the functioning of the material. Examples could include why electrically conductive materials are often made of metal, flexible but durable materials are made up of long chained molecules, and pharmaceuticals are designed to interact with specific receptors.
payload.topic_title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS2-6

linked_performance_expectations.description: Evaluate the validity and reliability of claims in published materials of the effects that different frequencies of electromagnetic radiation have when absorbed by matter.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 9–12 builds on K–8 and progresses to evaluating the validity and reliability of the claims, methods, and designs.
Evaluate the validity and reliability of multiple claims that appear in scientific and technical texts or media reports, verifying the data when possible.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative descriptions.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on the idea that photons associated with different frequencies of light have different energies, and the damage to living tissue from electromagnetic radiation depends on the energy of the radiation. Examples of published materials could include trade books, magazines, web resources, videos, and other passages that may reflect bias.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-4

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate technical information about how some technological devices use the principles of wave behavior and wave interactions with matter to transmit and capture information and energy.*

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 9–12 builds on K–8 and progresses to evaluating the validity and reliability of the claims, methods, and designs.

Communicate technical information or ideas (e.g., about phenomena and/or the process of development and the design and performance of a proposed process or system) in multiple formats (including orally, graphically, textually, and mathematically).

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessments are limited to qualitative information. Assessments do not include band theory.
payload.clarification_statement: Examples could include solar cells capturing light and converting it to electricity; medical imaging; and communications technology.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: HS-PS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for, and respond to, severe weather.*

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in K-2 builds on prior experiences and uses observations and texts to communicate new information.
Read grade-appropriate texts and/or use media to obtain scientific information to describe patterns in the natural world.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on local forms of severe weather.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-2

linked_performance_expectations.description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the land, water, air, and/or other living things in the local environment.*

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in K-2 builds on prior experiences and uses observations and texts to communicate new information.
Communicate solutions with others in oral and/or written forms using models and/or drawings that provide detail about scientific ideas.

payload.clarification_statement: Examples of human impact on the land could include cutting trees to produce paper and using resources to produce bottles. Examples of solutions could include reusing paper and recycling cans and bottles.

linked_performance_expectations.title: K-ESS3-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and synthesize information that sensory receptors respond to stimuli by sending messages to the brain for immediate behavior or storage as memories.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 6-8 builds on K-5 experiences and progresses to evaluating the merit and validity of ideas and methods.

Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence.

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include mechanisms for the transmission of this information.
payload.topic_title: From Molecules to Organisms: Structures and Processes

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS1-8

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and synthesize information about the technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 6–8 builds on K–5 experiences and progresses to evaluating the merit and validity of ideas and methods.

Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence.

payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on synthesizing information from reliable sources about the influence of humans on genetic outcomes in artificial selection (such as genetic modification, animal husbandry, gene therapy); and, on the impacts these technologies have on society as well as the technologies leading to these scientific discoveries.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-LS4-5

linked_performance_expectations.description: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 6–8 builds on K–5 and progresses to evaluating the merit and validity of ideas and methods.

Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence. (MS-PS1-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment is limited to qualitative information.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on natural resources that undergo a chemical process to form the synthetic material. Examples of new materials could include new medicine, new foods, and alternative fuels.

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS1-3

linked_performance_expectations.description: Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that digitized signals are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information than analog signals.

texts: Obtaining, evaluating, and communicating information in 6–8 builds on K–5 and progresses to evaluating the merit and validity of ideas and methods.
Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information in written text with that contained in media and visual displays to clarify claims and findings. (MS-PS4-3)

payload.assessment_boundary: Assessment does not include binary counting. Assessment does not include the specific mechanism of any given device.
payload.clarification_statement: Emphasis is on a basic understanding that waves can be used for communication purposes. Examples could include using fiber optic cable to transmit light pulses, radio wave pulses in wifi devices, and conversion of stored binary patterns to make sound or text on a computer screen.
payload.topic_title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer

linked_performance_expectations.title: MS-PS4-3

payload.full_name: INFO: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information

progression_statements.description: Read and comprehend grade-appropriate complex texts and/or other reliable media to summarize and obtain scientific and technical ideas and describe how they are supported by evidence.

payload.text: Read and comprehend grade-appropriate complex texts and/or other reliable media to summarize and obtain scientific and technical ideas and describe how they are supported by evidence.

progression_statements.title: INFO-E1

progression_statements.description: Compare and/or combine across complex texts and/or other reliable media to support the engagement in other scientific and/or engineering practices.

payload.text: Compare and/or combine across complex texts and/or other reliable media to support the engagement in other scientific and/or engineering practices.

progression_statements.title: INFO-E2

progression_statements.description: Combine information in written text with that contained in corresponding tables, diagrams, and/or charts to support the engagement in other scientific and/or engineering practices.

payload.text: Combine information in written text with that contained in corresponding tables, diagrams, and/or charts to support the engagement in other scientific and/or engineering practices.

progression_statements.title: INFO-E3

progression_statements.description: Obtain and combine information from books and/or other reliable media to explain phenomena or solutions to a design problem.

payload.text: Obtain and combine information from books and/or other reliable media to explain phenomena or solutions to a design problem.

progression_statements.title: INFO-E4

progression_statements.description: Communicate scientific and/or technical information orally and/or in written formats, including various forms of media as well as tables, diagrams, and charts.

payload.text: Communicate scientific and/or technical information orally and/or in written formats, including various forms of media as well as tables, diagrams, and charts.

progression_statements.title: INFO-E5

progression_statements.description: Critically read scientific literature adapted for classroom use to determine the central ideas or conclusions and/or to obtain scientific and/or technical information to summarize complex evidence, concepts, processes, or information presented in a text by paraphrasing them in simpler but still accurate terms.

payload.text: Critically read scientific literature adapted for classroom use to determine the central ideas or conclusions and/or to obtain scientific and/or technical information to summarize complex evidence, concepts, processes, or information presented in a text by paraphrasing them in simpler but still accurate terms.

progression_statements.title: INFO-H1

progression_statements.description: Compare, integrate and evaluate sources of information presented in different media or formats (e.g., visually, quantitatively) as well as in words in order to address a scientific question or solve a problem.

payload.text: Compare, integrate and evaluate sources of information presented in different media or formats (e.g., visually, quantitatively) as well as in words in order to address a scientific question or solve a problem.

progression_statements.title: INFO-H2

progression_statements.description: Gather, read, and evaluate scientific and/or technical information from multiple authoritative sources, assessing the evidence and usefulness of each source.

payload.text: Gather, read, and evaluate scientific and/or technical information from multiple authoritative sources, assessing the evidence and usefulness of each source.

progression_statements.title: INFO-H3

progression_statements.description: Evaluate the validity and reliability of and/or synthesize multiple claims, methods, and/or designs that appear in scientific and technical texts or media reports, verifying the data when possible.

payload.text: Evaluate the validity and reliability of and/or synthesize multiple claims, methods, and/or designs that appear in scientific and technical texts or media reports, verifying the data when possible.

progression_statements.title: INFO-H4

progression_statements.description: Communicate scientific and/or technical information or ideas (e.g., about phenomena and/or the process of development and the design and performance of a proposed process or system) in multiple formats (i.e., orally, graphically, textually, and mathematically).

payload.text: Communicate scientific and/or technical information or ideas (e.g., about phenomena and/or the process of development and the design and performance of a proposed process or system) in multiple formats (i.e., orally, graphically, textually, and mathematically).

progression_statements.title: INFO-H5

progression_statements.description: Critically read scientific texts adapted for classroom use to determine the central ideas and/or obtain scientific and/or technical information to describe patterns in and/or evidence about the natural and designed world(s).

payload.text: Critically read scientific texts adapted for classroom use to determine the central ideas and/or obtain scientific and/or technical information to describe patterns in and/or evidence about the natural and designed world(s).

progression_statements.title: INFO-M1

progression_statements.description: Integrate qualitative and/or quantitative scientific and/or technical information in written text with that contained in media and visual displays to clarify claims and findings.

payload.text: Integrate qualitative and/or quantitative scientific and/or technical information in written text with that contained in media and visual displays to clarify claims and findings.

progression_statements.title: INFO-M2

progression_statements.description: Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence.

payload.text: Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence.

progression_statements.title: INFO-M3

progression_statements.description: Evaluate data, hypotheses, and/or conclusions in scientific and technical texts in light of competing information or accounts.

payload.text: Evaluate data, hypotheses, and/or conclusions in scientific and technical texts in light of competing information or accounts.

progression_statements.title: INFO-M4

progression_statements.description: Communicate scientific and/or technical information (e.g., about a proposed object, tool, process, system) in writing and/or through oral presentations.

payload.text: Communicate scientific and/or technical information (e.g., about a proposed object, tool, process, system) in writing and/or through oral presentations.

progression_statements.title: INFO-M5

progression_statements.description: Read grade-appropriate texts and/or use media to obtain scientific and/or technical information to determine patterns in and/or evidence about the natural and designed world(s).

payload.text: Read grade-appropriate texts and/or use media to obtain scientific and/or technical information to determine patterns in and/or evidence about the natural and designed world(s).

progression_statements.title: INFO-P1

progression_statements.description: Describe how specific images (e.g., a diagram showing how a machine works) support a scientific or engineering idea.

payload.text: Describe how specific images (e.g., a diagram showing how a machine works) support a scientific or engineering idea.

progression_statements.title: INFO-P2

progression_statements.description: Obtain information using various texts, text features (e.g., headings, tables of contents, glossaries, electronic menus, icons), and other media that will be useful in answering a scientific question and/or supporting a scientific claim.

payload.text: Obtain information using various texts, text features (e.g., headings, tables of contents, glossaries, electronic menus, icons), and other media that will be useful in answering a scientific question and/or supporting a scientific claim.

progression_statements.title: INFO-P3

progression_statements.description: Communicate information or design ideas and/or solutions with others in oral and/or written forms using models, drawings, writing, or numbers that provide detail about scientific ideas, practices, and/or design ideas.

payload.text: Communicate information or design ideas and/or solutions with others in oral and/or written forms using models, drawings, writing, or numbers that provide detail about scientific ideas, practices, and/or design ideas.

progression_statements.title: INFO-P4
